experiments.title: How gift array options in a high urgency sticky bar impact donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a gift array to the HUC sticky bar for IMB website visitors will increase engagement and donor conversion by minimizing the number of steps on the donation page, reducing friction and decision-making, resulting in more gifts.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/15/2026 - 01/19/2026
During IMB’s 2026 Winter Olympics campaign, we re-tested the sticky bar’s gift array buttons to understand their impact on donor behavior.
In reviewing the original execution, we identified opportunities to improve how the sticky bar guided visitors toward giving—particularly around campaign value proposition and giving array amounts. We re-tested this approach to determine whether modifying the sticky bar could more effectively encourage gifts and to gather data needed to inform future fundraising tactics.
For this test, 50% of site traffic was shown the standard sticky bar experience featuring a single call-to-action button. The remaining 50% received a treatment version with three buttons mirroring the suggested gift amounts on the donation page, allowing visitors to engage with a giving option earlier in their journey.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment shows that adding a gift array to the IMB website’s sticky bar significantly increased both visitor engagement and donor conversion. The treatment version—featuring three suggested gift amount buttons—outperformed the standard sticky bar by 448.4% in Clicks for All Traffic, with 99.9% statistical confidence.
These results suggest that introducing giving options earlier in the donor journey reduces friction and simplifies decision-making on the donation page—leading to more completed gifts.
Downstream Impact on Donations:
To understand how increased engagement translated into giving behavior, we analyzed downstream donation metrics. During the test period, the control version generated 1 gift, while the treatment generated 16 gifts, representing a 1,495% increase in transactions (100% LOC).
This confirms that the gift array not only drives more clicks but also meaningfully motivates visitors to complete a donation.
Gift Amount Performance:
We also evaluated which gift array buttons led to the most donations:
$25 Button: 6 gifts, all at $25
 	$50 Button: 3 gifts, all at $50
 	$100 Button: 3 gifts (2 at $100, 1 at $150)
 	More Options: 4 gifts, resulting in varied amounts ($20, $50, $50, $200)
Notably, most donors retained the amount they selected in the sticky bar through to the completion of the donation, reinforcing the value of early commitment.
Conclusion & Recommendation:
Overall, the gift array on the sticky bar effectively removes a key decision point from the donation page, leading to higher engagement and more completed gifts.
For future testing, IMB should continue exploring gift arrays and similar friction-reducing elements in high-visibility areas of the site. Ongoing iteration will help further optimize the giving experience and maximize donor conversion and revenue.

experiments.title: How increasing the urgency impacts conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the urgency and need for rural education donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/01/2025 - 07/08/2025
During the Make Summer Fair campaign, we wanted to test whether increasing the urgency of the need on the donation form value prop copy, specifically tied to rural education, would impact conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test explored whether shifting rural education messaging and imagery to emphasize need — rather than happiness — would motivate more donors to give, and to give higher amounts.
Key Results:
Overall conversion dropped by 20% at 97% confidence — a statistically significant decline. RPV decreased by 6%, though that result wasn't statistically significant. The average gift, however, increased by $13.
The decrease in conversions outweighed the increase in average gift size, resulting in a net negative impact on overall performance.
Performance by Segment:
On desktop, conversion fell 23% while RPV nudged up 16% — suggesting the more urgent messaging resonated with a subset of desktop donors willing to give more, even as it turned others away. Mobile told a similar story: conversion dropped 15%, and RPV fell 21%, indicating the imagery may have been particularly off-putting on smaller screens where it's more prominent.
One-time giving saw a 19% drop in conversion at the 93% confidence level. Recurring giving declined across the board — conversion down 24% and RPV down 46% — though the sample wasn't large enough to draw firm conclusions there.
What the Results Suggest
The messaging didn't connect with most donors, but for those it did reach, it encouraged higher gifts. This suggests a segmentation opportunity rather than a broad messaging shift — the tone of urgency may work for a specific audience but feels less effective for the majority.
Next Steps:
Rather than abandoning the approach, we'll continue iterating to find a version that captures the average gift lift without the conversion drop. For example, we'll test a softer framing of need that feels compelling without being off-putting.

experiments.title: How a donor goal on a high urgency campaign pushdown impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor goal to the February Sustainers Pushdown for homepage visitors will increase donor conversion metrics because potential donors will feel their monthly gift would have a larger impact on the goal.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/03/2025
During The Navigators’ February Sustainers Campaign, we decided to test adding a donor goal into the pushdown copy.
The first treatment variation set a blanket donor goal of 100 monthly donors, and the second treatment variation set donor goals for each amount depicted on the gift array (i.e. Will you be one of the 50 monthly donors to make a $15 monthly gift, 30 donors to make a $25 monthly gift, or 20 donors to make a $50 monthly gift today?)
The goal of this test was to determine whether The Navigators’ audience would respond positively to a defined donor goal and, if so, whether they were more motivated by an overarching goal or by a goal broken into smaller amounts that felt more attainable.
Our hypothesis was that breaking down the donor goal into smaller, specific amounts would make it feel more achievable, increasing the likelihood of giving. By seeing a clearer, tangible impact, donors would be more inclined to contribute, believing their gift could make a greater difference.
experiments.keyLearnings: The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a donor goal to the February Sustainers Pushdown for homepage visitors significantly increased donor conversion metrics. The treatment variation that set donor goals for each amount depicted on the gift array resulted in a 266.8% increase in donations across all traffic compared to the control group, with a 96.8% statistical level of confidence. It is possible that Treatment #2 caused more confusion and friction on the page, rather than the very clear goal of 100 donors in Treatment #1. This suggests that The Navigators' audience reacts positively to clarity and set goal amounts. Furthermore, Treatment #2 had a 301% lift in revenue at a 97.4% confidence level. Not only did the overall donor goal motivate more people to give, but it also motivated them to give more than in the control group. In future experimentation, we should continue to incorporate donor goals into our fundraising campaigns. By setting specific, attainable donor goals, donors are more likely to feel their contributions will make a difference and be more motivated to give. Additionally, breaking the overall donor goal into smaller amounts can make it seem more achievable and encourage more donors to participate. Overall, the success of this experiment demonstrates the importance of setting donor goals and providing donors with a clear understanding of how their contributions can make an impact. This strategy can be utilized in future fundraising efforts to drive donor engagement and increase donations for The Navigators.

experiments.title: How quantifying the time investment in a high urgency email appeal impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe quantifying the time investment for donors will increase the click-through rate because more people will see it as within reach.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2025 - 12/18/2025
The IMB was running a donor survey as part of their year end campaign. They knew that people generally enjoyed giving their opinions, but wondered whether many thought it might take too long and therefore wouldn’t click.
The survey was short, but that couldn’t be seen in the email. They decided to run an experiment with a treatment that explicitly stated the survey would take “two minutes” to help donors answer the mental question: “Do I have time for this?” and see if it impacted click-through rate from the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that quantifying the time investment for donors by stating that the survey would only take "two minutes" led to a 6.2% increase in clickthrough rate from the email. This indicates that explicitly mentioning the survey's short duration positively affected recipients' decision to engage with it.
In future experimentation, it would be valuable to continue testing the effectiveness of providing specific details, such as time investment, to reduce donors' perceived effort. This approach can increase engagement and ultimately improve the success of campaigns or initiatives designed to elicit responses from the target audience. Additionally, it is important to incorporate similar strategies across other communication channels to further enhance donor participation and interaction.

experiments.title: How a monthly first ask on a high urgency campaign impacts recurring donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a monthly first ask for Fall Share giving page visitors will achieve an increase in recurring giving because more donors will choose to give monthly because the option is presented as the recommended and manageable choice. By also highlighting the benefits of recurring giving, donors will be further motivated to see monthly giving. because  more donors will choose to give monthly because the option is presented as the recommended and manageable choice. By also highlighting the benefits of recurring giving, donors will be further motivated to see monthly giving.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/16/2025 - 09/26/2025
Each year, Moody Radio runs a Fall Share campaign to inspire generosity among listeners.
This year, we made a strategic shift: instead of prioritizing one-time gifts, we focused on growing Moody’s recurring donor file. To accomplish this, we tested a monthly-first ask during the Fall Share campaign.
The goal was to build their recurring file while minimizing the negative impact on overall conversion on the giving page.
The test ran for 11 days, with traffic evenly split between each variant.
Note: The majority of participants came from known audiences. While a separate non-donor–specific donation page was available, many of the web assets directed to this general campaign page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing a monthly-first ask for Fall Share donation page visitors significantly increased recurring giving. The treatment group experienced a 168.2% increase in donations compared to the control group, with a 99.7% level of confidence in the results.
Furthermore, with this large increase in recurring giving, we wanted to be sure it wasn't at the expense of overall transactions. After further analysis, we discovered the treatment was resulting in a 5.5% decrease in overall transactions at a 42% level of confidence. Based on these results, the value of the more recurring donors outweighs the slight (and invalid) decrease in overall giving.
In future experimentation, we should continue to prioritize the monthly-first ask strategy, as it proved to be highly effective in increasing recurring donor conversions without significantly reducing overall transactions. This approach capitalizes on the convenience and appeal of monthly giving for donors, leading to a higher likelihood of acceptance of the pre-set option and a perception of monthly giving as both the recommended and more manageable choice. By consistently incorporating this strategy in future campaigns and experiments, we can continue to grow our recurring donor file and maximize the impact of our fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: How removing the organization’s name from an email sender impacts engagement during a high urgency campaign

rows.name: From: Jody at Bible League Canada

experiments.researchQuestion: We hypothesized that removing” at Bible League Canada” from the email sender name would improve the open rate of their emails. This would make the email look and feel more like a one-to-one communication in the inbox instead of an organization’s “marketing” email.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/19/2024 - 04/29/2024
Bible League Canada (BLC) is a ministry organization that uses members of its staff as email senders. The sender name for these staff emails always includes the person’s name and” at Bible League Canada” after it. The content of BLC’s emails feels very authentic and has helped foster strong relationships with their email list subscribers. Still, we wanted to collect data to understand whether including their organization’s name in their email sender name was hurting their email open rates.
We decided to test this concept during one of BLC’s high-urgency campaigns. We tested it with a direct appeal email sent from Hannah, one of Bible League Canada’s main email senders. The results of that test can be found here.
We wanted to retest this hypothesis using another sender during the campaign to see if the positive results we discovered would be limited to the sender we chose.
We ran this as an A/B email test, with 50% of BLC’s housefile list receiving the control email and the other 50% receiving the treatment email. The email’s subject line and content were the same for both variants; the only variable changed was the email sender’s name. These were the variants in this test:
Control Email – From name was “Jody at Bible League Canada”
Treatment Email – From name was “Jody”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that removing the organization's name from the email sender's name can significantly increase open rates. The treatment email, with the sender name "Jody," saw a 40.8% increase in opens compared to the control email, "Jody at Bible League Canada." This result was statistically significant at a 100.0% confidence level, indicating a strong impact.
We could not validate an increase in clicks from the treatment version. With this second test result, Bible League Canada's audience appears to respond well when appeals are sent without the "at Bible League Canada" postscript. This test should be revisited in the future to see if it is a "fad" or something that remains constant for their email file. But for now, using personal, authentic sender names should be the template for email cultivation and activation.

experiments.title: How Having a Humanized Exit Intent pop up during Calendar Year End affect conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized version of the exit intent for users wanting to exit out of the main donation page will achieve an increase in donations because seeing a message from someone from Time of Grace would motivate them to want to participate in the CYE efforts.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/13/2024 - 12/16/2024
For Time of Grace’s Calendar Year End, we wanted to test two variations of an exit intent: a regular one and a humanized version that would come from a person. We ran this experiment on their main donation page to capture users leaving the page and reinforce why they should donate. The exit intent would appear when the user hovered over the exit button.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the humanized version of the exit intent for users wanting to leave the main donation page did not achieve the desired outcome. In fact, it resulted in a significant decrease in transactions for all traffic, with a 100% statistical confidence level, and an even larger reduction of one-time donations.
In future experimentation, carefully considering the messaging and timing of exit intents is crucial. While adding a human touch can often be beneficial in marketing and communication strategies, in this case, it did not resonate with users in a way that encouraged donations.
It may be beneficial to test different messaging variations, including humanized and more traditional approaches, to see what resonates best with users. Additionally, considering the timing of the exit intent and ensuring it is strategically placed in the user journey may also impact its effectiveness.
Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of testing and iterating on different strategies to determine what motivates users to donate, ultimately leading to more successful fundraising efforts for Time of Grace Ministry.

experiments.title: How “Yes/No” Autonomy Language on a CYE Sticky Bar Impacts Conversion Rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a yes/no CTA on the MAF homepage will increase conversion rate because users will be more likely to give when their autonomy is preserved and they are given a “micro-decision” at the top of the funnel before being asked to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/04/2023 - 12/31/2023
One simple but powerful way to encourage donors to take action is by giving them a small choice up front. In this test, the idea was to use “yes/no” language in a sticky bar at the top of the homepage. Rather than jumping straight to a donation ask, the message first invited visitors to make a micro‑decision—a quick yes or no—before leading into the giving opportunity.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that incorporating a yes/no CTA that provides users with a micro-decision at the top of the funnel significantly increased the donor conversion rate. The treatment group saw a 62.4% increase in donations with high statistical confidence.

In future experimentation, it is important to continue exploring the impact of autonomy and micro-decision making on user behavior. This approach of giving users a sense of control and involvement in the donation process could be applied to other areas of the website or fundraising campaigns.

Overall, this experiment's results highlight the value of providing users with autonomy and decision-making opportunities in driving conversions. By understanding and leveraging this psychology, future experimentation efforts can focus on creating more engaging and compelling user experiences.

experiments.title: How a direct ask vs survey during a high urgency campaign lower third impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the Listener Survey treatment variant for Moody Radio website visitors will increase donor conversion. By first inviting visitors to share their feedback, the survey fosters a sense of being valued and deepens their emotional connection with Moody Radio, making them more likely to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/09/2025 - 09/16/2025
During Moody Radio’s 2025 Fall Share campaign, we tested two approaches: a direct ask to give toward Fall Share and an invitation to complete the Listener Survey.
Our goal was to learn whether Moody Radio’s audience responds more strongly to straightforward giving language, or if they prefer first engaging through a survey before being invited to make a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the direct ask approach to give toward Fall Share outperformed the invitation to complete the Listener Survey in terms of donor conversion. Despite hypothesizing that the survey would increase engagement and emotional connection, leading to more donations, the results showed a significant decrease in transactions for the survey variant.

In future experimentation, it is essential to carefully consider the preferences and behaviors of Moody Radio's audience. While exploring different approaches to engage donors is valuable, the results indicate that a straightforward giving ask may be more effective in driving donations during campaigns.

Moving forward, it would be beneficial to continue testing and refining messaging and calls to action to align with audience preferences. Additionally, gathering feedback from donors and website visitors through surveys or interviews can provide insights into their motivations and preferences, helping to inform future experimentation and fundraising strategies.

experiments.title: How defaulting the monthly tab on an acquisition offer as a part of a high urgency campaign affects recurring conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to the monthly tab will achieve an increase in recurring conversion rates because it reduces friction by aligning with the donor’s default behavior, subtly encourages sustained giving through visual anchoring, and reframes the donation as an ongoing commitment rather than a one-time gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/11/2025 - 07/19/2025
The Heritage Foundation is running their annual President’s Match Challenge campaign to members and non-members of Heritage. In this experiment, they wanted to test how defaulting to the monthly tab on the acquisition donation page of an ebook offer as apart of the campaign would affect the rate of recurring giving.
50% of traffic was randomly assigned to each variation (Control vs Treatment). The primary goal was to determine whether defaulting the donation format to monthly giving (rather than one-time giving) would influence the recurring conversion rate.
The idea to run this test stemmed from two core insights:
Behavioral Economics Research:
People tend to go with the default option presented to them. When monthly giving is presented as the default tab, it subtly frames that behavior as the norm or recommended path.
Organizational Goal: Boost Recurring Revenue:
Like many nonprofits, Heritage is invested in growing their base of recurring donors. Monthly gifts provide more predictable, long-term revenue.
By making “Make a Monthly Gift” the default selection (as seen in the treatment version), the hypothesis was that donors would be more likely to choose that option simply because it required no extra clicks or decisions to get there.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that defaulting the donation format to monthly giving can significantly influence the rate of recurring conversion. In this case, defaulting to the monthly tab led to a 1.6% increase in Transactions - Monthly for all traffic with a high level of statistical confidence.
In future experimentation, we should continue to leverage the insights from behavioral economics research and organizational goals to design and implement tests that capitalize on the tendency for people to go with the default option presented to them. By defaulting to monthly giving, we can subtly frame this behavior as the recommended path and encourage sustained giving through visual anchoring.
Additionally, we can use the learnings from this experiment to inform future campaign strategies and donation page designs. By understanding the impact of default options on donor behavior, we can optimize our fundraising efforts to maximize recurring revenue and overall donor engagement.

experiments.title: How copy and checkbox design drive GDPR consent opt-ins during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the GDPR consent process by using a single checkbox with clear privacy language will reduce friction for users and increase the opt-in rate for receiving future communications.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/17/2025 - 04/24/2025
CitizenGO is a global community that uses online petitions and action alerts to defend life, family, and liberty. Their platform enables individuals to influence decision-makers worldwide by leveraging the collective support of like-minded citizens.
This experiment stems from a review of GDPR (General Data Protection Regulation) requirements, which mandate that organizations obtain explicit consent from users before collecting or processing their personal data, including email addresses. As part of this compliance, it’s important to design petition sign-up forms in a way that ensures users are properly informed and opt in to receive future communications.
For this test, we are evaluating different designs for the GDPR consent checkbox on petition landing pages. The current design includes the following two options:
I want to know if this petition wins and how I can help other citizen petitions.
I don’t want to know how this petition or other important petitions are progressing.
The proposed test will compare three variations of the GDPR consent checkbox:
Control – The current checkbox design to track opt-ins.
Treatment 1 – A simplified design with a single checkbox and long privacy copy above it, providing detailed information in accordance with GDPR requirements.
Treatment 2 – A “Christian Lawyer” style design featuring a concise statement next to the checkbox, with a link to additional privacy policy details placed below the petition submission button.
The test will be run on the German petition pages (launching inline with their Stop Digital Euro Campaign) with traffic split evenly across all three variations. This experiment will help determine which design leads to higher consent opt-in rates while maintaining GDPR compliance.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test ran for 1 week and we observed significant differences in opt-in clicks across the different treatments. This result is parallel to what we saw when we ran this test on Hazteoir petition pages.

Results:
Control: 14,099 samples | 535 opt-in clicks | 6,707 email acquisitions
 	Treatment 1: 14,274 samples | 1,272 opt-in clicks | 6,667 email acquisitions
 	Treatment 2: 14,155 samples | 1,680 opt-in clicks | 6,808 email acquisitions
137.7%
213.7% increase
100% Level of Confidence (LoC)
Control conversion rate: ~47.6%
 	Treatment 1 conversion rate: ~46.7%
 	Treatment 2 conversion rate: ~48.1%
Recommendation:
Treatment 2

experiments.title: Using an Exit-Intent Popup to Increase Donor Conversion During a High-Urgency Campaign (May Match 2025)
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that presenting a lower donation ask in an exit-intent pop-up will encourage more users to complete their gift, capturing donations that might have otherwise been abandoned during a high-urgency campaign.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/06/2025 - 06/03/2025
As part of the May Match 2025 campaign—an urgency-driven effort to acquire new monthly donors (FamilyLife Partners)—we wanted to test whether an exit intent popup on the donation page could reduce abandonment and increase conversions.
To do this, we decided to present an exit-intent popup, which would give users a lower-barrier monthly donation ask if they showed intent to leave the page, and instead encourage them to complete a smaller gift. The treatment experience included a popup triggered by mouse-exit behavior on desktop or time delay on mobile, offering a $10/month ask with emotionally resonant, impact-focused messaging. The control experience maintained the standard donation page with no popup intervention.
We intentionally excluded visitors who had previously interacted with an impact popup earlier in the session, to isolate the effect of this standalone exit-intent experience. The popup emphasized that even a small monthly gift could make a lasting difference—supporting 76 families with Christ-centered resources.
We wanted to focus this test on recapturing users who demonstrated exit behavior, hypothesizing that a timely, emotionally framed, and low-friction ask could nudge hesitant users to commit before abandoning the donation process.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test demonstrated that introducing an exit-intent popup with a lower-barrier ask on the donation page can meaningfully reduce abandonment and increase both one-time and recurring donor conversion—especially during high-urgency campaigns like May Match.
The treatment experience, which offered a $10/month giving option when users showed intent to leave, outperformed the control by 44.2% in overall donor conversion (2.7% vs. 1.8%), achieving statistical significance at 99.4% confidence.
Looking at giving types:
Recurring gifts more than doubled, with a 102.1% increase in recurring donor conversion (0.7% vs. 0.3%, 98.3% confidence).One-time gifts also increased by 32.2% (2.1% vs. 1.6%, 94.2% confidence).
These results suggest that a well-timed, emotionally compelling, and low-friction offer can recapture hesitant donors right before they leave—and that framing the donation ask as small but impactful can activate both urgency and achievability.
The success of the recurring donor uplift is particularly important in the context of long-term donor value. This test validates the exit-intent popup as a powerful conversion tool and opens the door for future testing around:
Tailoring the popup message for one-time vs. recurring emphasisOptimizing design and placement for mobile vs. desktopTesting variations in gift amounts and message tone (e.g., more urgent vs. more relational)
This was a high-impact win with broad implications for donation page optimization.

experiments.title: How including a gift array in a high urgency campaign sticky bar affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding gift array buttons to a lower third for Today in the Word homepage visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because because it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2025 - 06/10/2025
During Today in the Word’s FYE Campaign, we tested adding gift array buttons into the lower third vs a one-button ask in a lower third. For this test, we tracked CTA clicks on both the control and treatment to measure how effective each one of these web assets persuaded someone to navigate to the donation page, as well as transaction and revenue that came directly from these assets.
This test ran for 8 days and the treatment was shown to 50% of traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding gift array buttons to a lower third for Today in the Word homepage visitors significantly increased donor conversion metrics. The treatment resulted in a 172.0% increase in Transactions (src=tw_fye25_footer*) for All Traffic with a 98.2% level of confidence, indicating that the treatment was successful in persuading users to navigate to the donation page and complete a transaction. Based on these results, we should be using a lower third with a gift array in it for future lower thirds for Today in the Word.
We also tracked clicks on both the control and treatment lower thirds and the treatment saw a 51.4% lift in clicks ( 97.1% LOC) compared to the control. These results indicate that the treatment not only encouraged more people to navigate to the donation page, but also resulted in an increase in transcations.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the impact of providing users with the opportunity to select a gift amount before reaching the donation page. This can streamline the giving process for users and potentially lead to higher conversion rates. Additionally, it is essential to continue testing different variations of donation prompts and CTAs to optimize donor conversion metrics further.

experiments.title: How including a premium in slide out copy impacts clicks during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including the premium in the fiscal year end slide out copy for Moody Radio’s content page visitors will achieve an increase in click rate because it offers an incentive to give to their fiscal year end campaign.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2025 - 06/05/2025
During Moody Radio’s Fiscal Year End Campaign, they were offering a physical premium with any gift amount. In order to better understand how their audience responds to premiums, we tested mentioning the premium within the copy of the slide-out. All other factors remained constant.
The treatment was shown to half of the traffic to Moody Radio’s content pages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:

1. Including the premium offering in the copy of the fiscal year end slide-out for Moody Radio content pages resulted in a significant increase in clicks.

2. The treatment group experienced a 43.6% increase in clicks compared to the control group, showing the effectiveness of mentioning the premium as an incentive for donations.

3. The statistical significance level of 98.6% provides high confidence in the results of the experiment meaning that the treatment should be used in future campaigns.

Implications for Future Experimentation:

1. Future experiments should continue to explore the impact of premiums or incentives on donor behavior.

2. It is recommended to consistently test different types of premiums and messaging strategies to optimize the effectiveness of incentives in driving donations.

3. Consider implementing similar tactics in other fundraising campaigns or donation drives to leverage the success observed in this experiment.

4. Continuously monitor and analyze the results of experiments to refine strategies and improve performance in fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: How including a premium in slide out copy impacts donor conversion metrics during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including the premium in the fiscal year end slide out copy for Moody Radio content page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it offers an incentive to give to their fiscal year end campaign.

Timeframe: 06/02/2025 - 06/05/2025

experiments.keyLearnings:

In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing the effectiveness of incentives, such as physical premiums, in encouraging donations. It would be beneficial to explore different types of incentives and how they impact donor behavior. Additionally, considering the success of this experiment, it may be worthwhile to incorporate information about premiums in other fundraising initiatives to further boost donor conversion rates. Regular testing and optimization based on data-driven insights will help Moody Radio continue to improve their fundraising efforts and maximize donor engagement.

experiments.title: Does Direct Messaging Increase Donor Conversion During High-Urgency Campaigns?
experiments.researchQuestion: The direct messaging treatment will drive higher conversion rates by reducing ambiguity and increasing perceived urgency; individuals are more likely to take action when the problem and required response are stated explicitly.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/22/2025 - 05/01/2025
This experiment was conducted by an NPR member station serving listeners across North Carolina, during their Spring Fund Drive. The campaign took place during a period of elevated urgency, as potential federal funding cuts were in the news cycle and top of mind for many supporters.
The goal of the experiment was to test the impact of message explicitness on donor engagement. We compared two versions of campaign messaging: the control featured a more indirect, suggestive tone, implying the risk to public media funding without naming specific actors. In contrast, the treatment used more direct, unambiguous language, explicitly referencing Congress and the political threats to public media funding.
While the tone and framing of the email copy differed, both versions directed users to the same donation landing page. This allowed us to isolate the effect of message directness on donor behavior, particularly under conditions of heightened urgency and media salience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This messaging experiment, run during a period of heightened urgency around public media funding, tested two approaches:
A Funding Version with direct, issue-specific language referencing Congressional threats, and
 	A General Version with a softer, more implied message.
Key Learnings:
The Funding Version drove nearly double the total attributed purchases (125 vs. 67), including post-view conversions, indicating a stronger overall persuasive effect.
 	Both versions saw similar direct donor counts (11 vs. 10), but the Funding Version's stronger post-view performance suggests it had a more lasting cognitive impact—viewers retained the message and returned later to donate, even without clicking.
 	Despite lower click volume, the Funding Version likely attracted more qualified or high-intent users—those who clicked were more likely to convert, contributing to a lower cost per donor ($54.22 vs. $59.62).
 	The alignment between the Funding Version’s direct messaging and the donation page content may have created a more seamless and cognitively consistent user experience, reducing friction and increasing likelihood to give.
 	Although the General Version received more clicks overall, these users were less likely to donate, highlighting the importance of click quality over quantity.
Takeaway:
Lean into Direct, Issue-Oriented Messaging
Test Message-to-Landing Page Alignment
Explore New Creative to Expand Reach
new creative formats
Differentiate Messaging by Audience Segment
Analyze Post-View Conversion Behavior

experiments.title: How testing whether an urgency-driven subject line or a specific biblical reference leads to higher opens and engagement
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a specific and intriguing biblical reference (“Discover the richness of Exodus”) instead of a more generic faith-based phrase (“Don’t miss this opportunity to deepen your faith”) for people viewing a content offer email in their inbox will achieve an increase in email opens because specificity and curiosity drive engagement, making the subject line feel more relevant and compelling to the reader.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/08/2025 - 02/08/2025
Luther Seminary creates ebooks and digital resources to help their audience deepen their faith and biblical knowledge. Their latest ebook explores the book of Exodus, and they aimed to maximize engagement by optimizing the subject line of their promotional email to their housefile audience.
To determine what type of subject line would drive more opens and clicks, we conducted a series of A/B tests. In a prior test, we compared exclusivity-based language to a curiosity-driven subject line, finding that a curiosity-driving subject line increased opens by 9.4% with 100% statistical confidence.
For this next test, we wanted to know whether an urgency-based subject line (“Don’t miss this opportunity to deepen your faith”) or a subject line that was more specific and intriguing (“Discover the richness of Exodus”) would drive higher engagement. We hypothesized that the more specific and curiosity-driven subject line would increase opens by making the content feel more relevant and compelling to the reader.
This was a 50/50 A/B test, with half of the audience receiving the Control subject line and the other half receiving the Treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This test produced unexpected but valuable insights about the relationship between subject line messaging and user engagement.

While the Control subject line ("Don't miss this opportunity to deepen your faith") generated more opens, the Treatment subject line ("Discover the richness of Exodus") resulted in fewer opens (a 3.8% decrease) but led to a 24.5% increase in email clicks, with a 98.4% statistical level of confidence.

This suggests that while urgency-driven language in the Control created a stronger initial impulse to open the email, the specificity of the Treatment subject line attracted a more engaged audience—those who opened were more likely to click through to the content.

Psychologically, this aligns with two key principles:
FOMO (Fear of Missing Out) and urgency – The Control subject line may have driven more opens by making the email feel time-sensitive and broadly relevant.
 	Relevance and commitment – The Treatment subject line set clearer expectations about the content, likely attracting recipients who were already interested in the topic, leading to higher engagement post-open.

experiments.title: How a donor goal on a high urgency campaign pushdown impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor goal to the February Sustainers Pushdown for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the potential donor would feel like their monthly gift would have a larger impact on the goal.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/03/2025
During The Navigators February Sustainers Campaign, we decided to test into adding a donor goal into the pushdown copy.
The first treatment variation set a blanket donor goal of 100 monthly donors, and the second treatment variation set donor goals for each amount depicted on the gift array (i.e. Will you be one of the 50 monthly donors to make a $15 monthly gift, 30 donors to make a $25 monthly gift, or 20 donors to make a $50 monthly gift today?)
The goal of this test was to determine whether The Navigators’ audience would respond positively to a defined donor goal, and if so, whether they were more motivated by an overarching goal or one broken up into different amounts that felt more attainable.
Our hypothesis was that breaking down the donor goal into smaller, specific amounts would make it feel more achievable, increasing the likelihood of giving. By seeing a clearer, tangible impact, donors would be more inclined to contribute, believing their gift could make a greater difference.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a donor goal to the February Sustainers Pushdown for homepage visitors significantly increased donor conversion metrics. The treatment variation that set donor goals for each amount depicted on the gift array resulted in a 266.8% increase in donations for all traffic compared to the control group, with a 96.8% statistical level of confidence.
It is possible that Treatment #2 caused more confusion and friction on the page rather than the very clear goal of 100 donors in Treatment #1. This suggests that The Navigators audience reacts positively to clarity and set goal amounts.
Furthermore, Treatment #2 had a 301% lift in revenue at a valid 97.4% level of confidence. Not only did the overall donor goal motivate more people to give, but it motivated them to give more than the control.
In future experimentation, we should continue to incorporate donor goals into our fundraising campaigns. By setting specific and attainable donor goals, donors are more likely to feel that their contribution will make a difference and be more motivated to give. Additionally, breaking down the overall donor goal into smaller amounts can make the goal seem more achievable and encourage more donors to participate.
Overall, the success of this experiment demonstrates the importance of setting donor goals and providing donors with a clear understanding of how their contributions can make an impact. This strategy can be utilized in future fundraising efforts to drive donor engagement and increase donations for The Navigators.

experiments.title: How beginning a high urgency appeal email with a story impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that beginning an email appeal with a story for CYE Email 4 recipients will achieve an increase in clicks because the reader will be immediately engaged and drawn in by the narrative therefore increasing the reader retention.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2025
For Email 4 of The Navigators CYE campaign, we opted to test into removing a personal reflection from the beginning of an appeal email.
We believe that starting the email with the story directly will immediately engage the reader by drawing them into a compelling narrative, leading to increased reader retention and higher click-through rates.
This approach bypasses the personal reflection, which may delay the email’s emotional hook, ensuring the audience connects more quickly with the mission and impact of the Navigator staff.
The treatment was sent to 50% of the Email 4 recipients.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings:

1. Beginning an email appeal with a story for CYE Email 4 recipients resulted in a 44.0% increase in clicks compared to starting with a personal reflection.

2. The treatment showed a high level of statistical confidence at 97.4%, indicating that the results are reliable and significant.

3. By immediately engaging the reader with a compelling narrative, the treatment increased reader retention and click-through rates.

Future Experimentation:

1. Consider incorporating storytelling at the beginning of email appeals for other campaigns to enhance reader engagement and drive desired actions.

2. Test different types of stories, such as success stories, impact stories, or personal narratives, to determine which resonates best with the audience.

3. Continue to prioritize testing and data analysis to identify effective strategies for engaging donors and supporters in email communication.

By leveraging the success of this experiment and applying the key learnings to future campaigns, The Navigators can continue to optimize their email appeals for increased impact and engagement.

experiments.title: How a countdown timer impacts donations on a CYE donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a countdown timer on the donation page for email subscribers will achieve an increase in donation because the timer represents urgency to the deadline.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/29/2024 - 12/30/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
For PTV’s calendar year-end campaign, we tested the countdown timer feature provided by the donation platform Anedot.
For this test, we specifically targeted email subscribers.
Our hypothesis is that the timer would help to drive urgency and therefore increase donations to the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted for the Pathway to Victory year-end campaign aimed to test the effectiveness of adding a countdown timer on the donation page for email subscribers to increase donations. The hypothesis was that the timer would create a sense of urgency and lead to a higher donation rate.
However, after running the experiment for 1 day, it was found that the treatment (with the countdown timer) experienced a 42.3% decrease in transactions for all traffic compared to the control group. The results were statistically significant at a 97.3% confidence level, indicating that the control group performed better.
Key Learnings:
1. **Countdown Timer Decreased Transactions:** Contrary to the hypothesis, the presence of a countdown timer did not increase donations, but instead led to a decrease in transactions. This suggests that the timer may have deterred potential donors or created a negative impact on the donation process.
2. **Importance of Testing:** The results highlight the importance of conducting experiments and A/B testing before implementing changes, even if the changes seem promising. In this case, testing revealed that the countdown timer did not have the intended effect.
3. **Understand Audience Preferences:** It is crucial to understand the preferences and behaviors of the target audience before implementing changes. In this case, the audience of Pathway to Victory may not have responded positively to the countdown timer approach.
4. **Optimize Messaging:** While urgency can be a powerful motivator for donations, the manner in which it is communicated is important. Future experimentation should focus on optimizing messaging to create urgency without detracting from the donation experience.
How to Use What Was Learned:
1. **Iterate and Test:** Use the learnings from this experiment to iterate on future campaigns and continue testing different strategies. Experiment with variations in messaging, design, and calls to action to find what resonates best with the audience.
2. **Collect Feedback:** Gather feedback from donors and subscribers to understand their preferences and solicit suggestions for improvement. Implement feedback loops to continuously refine donation strategies.
3. **Monitor Performance:** Keep a close eye on donation metrics and performance indicators to quickly identify any negative trends. Adjust strategies accordingly to optimize donation outcomes.
Overall, the key takeaway from this experiment is that while the addition of a countdown timer did not yield the desired results, it provided valuable insights that can be used to refine future experimentation and donation strategies for Pathway to Victory.

Moving forward, we should consider implementing gift arrays or similar features in future experiments and campaigns to further test and optimize their effectiveness in driving donor conversions.

experiments.title: How a donor goal and exclusivity language in a high-urgency email appeal impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a donor goal and exclusivity language for Reason to Believe’s housefile appeal will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it leverages that the donor is part of a special group, and breaks the goal into something they can visualize their impact upon.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/19/2024 - 12/03/2024
This year, rather than celebrating Giving Tuesday, Reasons to Believe decided to launch the organization’s very first RTB Giving Day. This was a high-urgency appeal that would last only one-day, with the goal of raising $15,000. We hypothesized that translating this to a 180 donor goal rather than a revenue goal, would increase donor conversion rate for two reasons. First, we believed that it would help underscore these donors as being part of a special group — donors to the first RTB Giving Day; second, we believed that this would help break the more intimidating $15,000 goal into something manageable that the donor could more easily see their own impact in.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results of this experiment illustrate that the use of a donor goal and exclusivity language in the appeal significantly increased the donor conversion rate for Reasons to Believe's RTB Giving Day campaign. This approach helped emphasize the donors' participation in a special group and made the donation goal more manageable and tangible for the donors. The treatment group, which received the donor goal and exclusivity language, saw a 54.0% increase in donations compared to the control group, with a high level of statistical confidence (95.3%).
In future experimentation, we should consider the context around a high-urgency goal and the donor's place in them. The amount of time communicated to reach the goal and perceived difficulty of reaching the goal can all be things that potentially discourage the donor from understanding their impact rather than communicating it. Within the context of a 24-hour appeal and the efforts of RTB to start something unique, it appears that speaking directly to the donors needed to accomplish this was most effective.

experiments.title: How a sliding bar on a campaign donation page allowing donors to adjust their impact influences donor conversion during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that allowing donors to select the level of their impact dynamically will increase donor conversion during a high-urgency campaign by increasing the perceived clarity and appeal of the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/25/2024 - 10/25/2024
During this organization’s Fall recurring campaign, we wanted to increase the tangibility and clarity of the donor’s impact on the campaign’s donation page. The page already highlighted the number of people that would be reached with a donor’s gift. However, we hypothesized that allowing the donors to drag a slider that dynamically changes the number of people reached would increase the clarity of the offer when compared with the current experience.
We used VWO to create a graphical, interactive slider on the page that dynamically changed the gift amount and the corresponding number of people the gift would reach. We split traffic 50/50, and all campaign sources were directed to the test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that allowing donors to select the level of their impact dynamically increased donor conversion significantly during a high-urgency campaign. The treatment resulted in a 37.4% increase in donations for all traffic with a high level of statistical confidence at 97.6%.

Additionally, we increased recurring gifts on the page by 33% at a 90% level of confidence.

We are next going to test this approach on their acquisition offer pages to see what impact the urgency of the campaign had on the results of this experiment.

experiments.title: How a slide-up with gift array vs sticky bar impacts clicks to the donation page during a high-urgency direct appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a slide-up with a gift array for users on Luther’s Working Preacher site will achieve an increase in clicks to the donation page because it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page. because  it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page. because  it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page. because  it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/15/2024 - 10/17/2024
Luther Seminary was running a high-urgency campaign to support their Working Preacher site, and one of the web elements on the site was a standard direct appeal sticky bar. We wanted to test whether using a slide-up element instead of a sticky bar would drive more user engagement through clicks to the donation page.
The sticky bar and slide-up both used the same language, but the slide-up presented users with 3 gift options. Clicking a gift option would direct users to the donation page and the form would be pre-filled with their selected gift amount.
Our hypothesis was that the slide-up would drive an increase in clicks to the donation page because it lets users begin the process of giving before they even land on the donation page. We also hoped that this would result in a downstream lift in donations from the slide-up.
We split the website traffic 50/50 between the two variants, and the test ran for ~3 days before we ran out of testing credits and had to end it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a slide-up with a gift array on Luther Seminary's Working Preacher site led to a significant increase in clicks to the donation page compared to a standard sticky bar. The treatment resulted in a 43.6% increase in Clicks for All Traffic with a high statistical level of confidence (99.5%)!
We also found that the slide-up led to a 33.4% increase in donor conversion, but this was only with a 68.3% level of confidence.
From this test we learned that it is important to continue exploring different ways to engage Working Preacher users and streamline the donation process for them. It is clear that providing users with the option to select a gift amount before landing on the donation page can be effective in driving more clicks and potentially increasing donations. For future testing, we want to experiment with the language in the slide-up, as well as the psychology of price anchoring with our gift array amounts.

experiments.title: What Drives More Urgency A Popup or a Wikipedia-style banner?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that switching the style of an ask for the article pages will achieve a higher conversion rate because the new element will draw more attention to it because users have not seen it before.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/14/2024 - 07/02/2024
An organization has been using wiki bars on their article pages for several high urgency campaigns. After an element has been present for a while on the page “banner blindness” can become present and the element might catch the eye of the users for them to take action.
For this test we wanted to see if switching the element on the article pages could increase transactions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that switching the style of an ask for the article pages actually led to a decrease in transactions. Despite the hypothesis that the new element would draw more attention due to novelty, the treatment group experienced a 38.4% decrease in Transactions - One-Time for All Traffic compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at a 96.2% confidence level, indicating a high level of certainty.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the potential impact of changing elements on the page, especially when it comes to high urgency campaigns like the ones run by this organization. It is crucial to balance the need for catching the users' attention with the risk of causing confusion or decreased engagement. Additionally, it is essential to continually monitor and evaluate the performance of different elements to ensure that any changes made are positively impacting the desired outcomes.

Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of testing assumptions and continuously learning from the results to inform and improve future strategies and campaigns.

experiments.title: How a more positive tone in copy impacts donor conversion metrics on a high urgency campaign donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the 2023 copy will perform better than the 2024 copy for Dr. Carson’s birthday card signers will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because we tend to use a more negative approach of the “what ifs” if you don’t make a gift which typically performs well for ACI.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/26/2024 - 08/29/2024
Every year, ACI runs a high urgency campaign for Dr. Carson’s birthday for people to sign his card.
This year, we wanted to test running this year’s donation page copy vs last year’s copy. The 2024 copy had more visual styling on the page in the form of headers and more easy to consume paragraphs and sections as well as a heavier focus on Dr. Carson’s values and a positive tone.
The 2023 copy had a more negative and fearful approach to acquiring more donations and used a much more simple page style.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the 2023 copy, with its negative and fearful approach, did not perform better than the 2024 copy, which had a more positive tone and focused on Dr. Carson's values. In fact, the 2024 copy outperformed the 2023 copy significantly, with a 60.0% decrease in transactions for all traffic in just 3 days, with a statistical level of confidence of 96.0%.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the tone and messaging used in donation pages. While a negative approach may have worked well in the past for ACI, it is essential to continuously test and iterate on different messaging strategies to see what resonates best with donors. Additionally, it is important to continue utilizing A/B testing to gather data and insights on donor behaviors and preferences, which can inform future decision-making and strategy.

Overall, the results of this experiment emphasize the importance of testing different approaches and being open to new ideas and strategies to effectively engage donors and drive conversions.

experiments.title: How a one-step donation page vs a two-step donation page impacts donor conversion metrics during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a one-step funnel for Dr. Carson’s Birthday Card Signers will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the visitor would be forced to make a gift in order to submit their note.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/05/2024 - 09/10/2024
Every year, ACI runs a high urgency campaign focused around Dr. Ben Carson’s birthday in effort to encourage individuals to sign his birthday card and then make a gift.
This year, we decided to test a one-step funnel vs a two step funnel. The two-step funnel followed our normal funnel process: the visitor lands on a landing page asking them to sign Dr. Carson’s Birthday card. After signing it (filling out the form) they are directed to a donation page to make a gift.
The one-step funnel however combined the landing and donation page together. So, in this case, the visitor lands immediately on the donation page where there is an embedded form to sign Dr. Carson’s Birthday Card. The only way the visitors birthday wishes would be submitted was by completing a gift.
Our hypothesis was that the one-step funnel would increase our donor conversion metrics because the perception that they would not be able to send their birthday note to Dr. Carson would encourage them to make a gift.
This test ran as a Split URL test, split equally between all traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing a one-step funnel for Dr. Ben Carson's Birthday Card Signers led to a significant increase in donor conversion metrics. The results showed a 30.9% increase in Transactions for All Traffic with a 95.8% confidence level, indicating that the treatment was successful in driving more donations.

Moreover, the One-Time Donation metric experienced a remarkable 352% increase with a 97% confidence level, further emphasizing the effectiveness of the one-step funnel approach in encouraging individuals to make a gift in order to submit their birthday note to Dr. Carson.

We were curious how the one-step funnel would impact revenue, and surprisingly, we sae a 54.6% lift in revenue at an 86.7% LOC. While invalid, these results suggest that individuals are more likely to give larger gifts in a one-step funnel.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the impact of simplifying the donation process and creating a sense of urgency or exclusivity. By leveraging similar strategies, such as combining actions (e.g., signing a birthday card and making a donation) into a single step, nonprofits like the American Cornerstone Institute can potentially boost donor conversion rates and maximize fundraising efforts. Additionally, it is crucial to continue analyzing and testing different funnel approaches to identify the most effective methods for engaging supporters and driving donations.

experiments.title: How a slide-up with gift array vs an exit-intent impacts donor conversion during high-urgency appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that slide-up with gift array for users on profile sites during the 2-day match appeal will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate and revenue because it allows the users to already begin the donation process, by selecting an amount, before they even arrive on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/24/2024 - 09/26/2024
CaringBridge was running a 2-day high-urgency match appeal. Historically, this has been communicated using an exit-intent pop-up that informs users about how many donors are needed as well as that the match will double their impact. We hypothesized that a slide-up with a gift array of three amounts would ultimately be more eye-catching, empower the user with more options up front, and increase revenue by ensuring that whatever amount they selected would be pre-filled on the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment show that utilizing a slide-up with a gift array during high-urgency match appeals can significantly increase donor conversion rates and revenue. In this particular case, the treatment resulted in a 45.5% increase in Donations - Non-Tribute for All Traffic with a high level of statistical confidence, indicating that the new approach was successful in prompting more users to donate.
Moving forward, these learnings should be applied to future experimentation by considering the effectiveness of providing users with donation options upfront, allowing them to start the donation process before reaching the actual donation page. This approach not only streamlines the donation process for users but also serves as a powerful motivator for them to contribute, particularly during time-sensitive campaigns like match appeals.
Overall, the success of this experiment highlights the importance of user experience and the potential impact of small changes in the donation journey. It is essential to continue testing and iterating on different strategies to optimize fundraising efforts and ultimately drive more support for the organization's mission.
In the future, we would like to re-test this experience during an evergreen phase, outside of the 2-day match appeal to see its impact on donors and revenue.

experiments.title: How more hyperlinks within a high-urgency campaign email appeal impact email clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a higher number of links  for Eagle Lake email appeal recipients will achieve an increase in email clicks because it gives the reader more opportunities to navigate to the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/08/2024
Every year, The Navigators run a high-urgency campaign called Eagle Lake 2 between June and July to raise money for the camps they host.
We have seen success using more hyperlinks within an email appeals in terms of both unique clicks and transactions, therefore, we wanted to try testing this for The Navigators.
The control maintained the number of clicks that we used in each of the previous appeal emails – typically 1, occasionally 2. However, the treatment contained a total of 5 links to the donation page.
Our hypothesis was that giving the reader more opportunities to navigate to the donation page the more likely they are to click.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that increasing the number of links in email appeals for The Navigators' Eagle Lake campaign led to a significant increase in clicks from recipients. The treatment group, which had 5 links to the donation page, saw a 32.8% increase in clicks compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at a confidence level of 97.5%. These results are valid - meaning it is recommended to use this tactic moving forward if your goal is to increase email clicks.
Further, we also wanted to see what the downstream metrics looked like on the donation page for this test. From email clicks to donations, the treatment saw a 10.5% decrease in transactions at a 40.4% level of confidence. While from email sends to donations, the treatment actually saw an 18.9% lift in transactions at a 52.4% level of confidence.
It is important to view both of these metrics as the different perspectives offer very different results. While these metrics did not validate, it is important to remember that when we push more traffic to a donation page, the instant conversion rate with decrease. But in actuality, there was an 18.9% lift in transactions.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the impact of the number of links in email appeals. This suggests that providing recipients with more opportunities to navigate to the donation page can result in higher engagement and potentially more donations. It would be interesting to test different variations of the number of links to determine the optimal amount for maximizing clicks and conversions.
Overall, the success of this experiment highlights the importance of testing and optimizing email strategies to enhance the effectiveness of fundraising campaigns for The Navigators.

experiments.title: How a relevant image in a high urgency email appeal impacts email clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a relevant image for Eagle Lake 2 email recipients will achieve an increase in clicks because people give to people and it would establish a more personable connection to the camp.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 08/08/2024
Every year, The Navigators run a high-urgency campaign called Eagle Lake 2 between June and July to raise money for the camps they host.
The Navigators audience is very comfortable and familiar with Abby Anderson, the main sender we use for their emails and texts. Therefore, we wanted to see whether an image of Abby at Eagle Lake camps when she was young would influence email clicks and increase donations as a result.
Our hypothesis was that the image of Abby would allow the recipient to feel more connected to Eagle Lake camps therefore increasing the likelihood that they would click the donation link, then give a gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including a relevant image of Abby at Eagle Lake camps in the email led to a significant increase in clicks for all traffic. The treatment group, which received the email with the image, saw a 48.1% increase in clicks compared to the control group. This result was statistically significant at a 99.9% confidence level, indicating a strong impact of the image on recipient engagement.
These results are valid and therefore, we should consider using relevant images within appeal emails moving forward.
We also pulled number of donations out of the number of clicks within this test and saw an invalid 25.7 decrease in donations at an 85.9% level of confidence. As well as number of donations put of the number of email sends resulted in a 10.1% lift in donations at a 33.4% level of confidence.
We decided to view both of these metrics because based if we only viewed the number of donations out of clickthrough rate, it appears that the image of Abby decreased conversions, but in actuality, there was a 10% increase in donations. It only seems as though there was a drop because we pushed a larger group of people to the donation page.
Neither of these metrics validated, but the results do offer helpful learnings and context of The Navigators audience.
This experiment demonstrates the effectiveness of using personal, relatable images to establish a more personable connection with the audience and drive engagement. In future experimentation, it is recommended to continue incorporating relevant images of people or scenarios that resonate with the audience to increase interaction and response rates. Additionally, the success of this experiment highlights the importance of testing different elements in email campaigns to identify strategies that yield the best results and improve overall campaign performance.

experiments.title: How using a faux forward during a high urgency campaign impacted donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a faux forward for donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2022 - 11/29/2022
Belmont Abbey College is a university in North Carolina whose mission is to educate students in the liberal arts and sciences so that in all things God may be glorified. In this endeavor, they are guided by the Catholic intellectual tradition and the Benedictine spirit of prayer and learning.
Their Giving Tuesday campaign had a goal of raising $30,000 in a single day; this experiment focused on their donor segment, and looked at whether using a faux forward tactic in email, versus a resend of the AM email, could positively influence donor conversion rates.
Our hypothesis with this experiment is that by using a faux forward, we would be able to cut through the inbox clutter potential donors experience on Giving Tuesday, and thus increase conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While the results of this experiment show that using a faux forward had a dramatic increase in conversion rates, this test did not have enough total donations to really show a valid result.

Moreover, the copy on the faux forward version referenced a micro-goal, whereas the control just had the overall goal, so our hypothesis is that it was a combination of that, plus the faux forward, that led to people donating more. This is further reinforced by the fact that open rates for both versions were essentially the same (23% versus 24%).

Further refinement of this test is warranted.

experiments.title: How adding urgency to Facebook ad copy effects registration rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We hypothesize that incorporating urgency into the ad copy will lead to higher completion rates for registrations. This approach aims to instill a sense of time sensitivity among prospects, emphasizing the relevance of acquiring this information in anticipation of the election year.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/24/2024
This experiment was conducted on behalf of WUNC, a North Carolina-based radio station, using a paid media channel on Facebook. The ad copy promotes an email series content offer providing prospects with information on elections and politics. Our objective was to determine whether adding urgency to the copy would increase the rate of complete registrations. Both the control and treatment groups utilized the same creative elements and call-to-action.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:
1. Incorporating urgency into ad copy can significantly increase completion rates for registrations.
2. The treatment group, which included urgency in the ad copy, saw a 46.8% increase in emails acquired for all traffic compared to the control group.
3. The results were statistically significant with a 96.3% confidence level.
4. This approach successfully instilled a sense of time sensitivity among prospects, emphasizing the relevance of the information provided in the email series in anticipation of the election year.
In future experimentation, we should continue to test the effectiveness of urgency in ad copy across different campaigns and audiences. Additionally, we can explore other ways to create a sense of urgency, such as limited-time offers or exclusive content. It is important to analyze the impact of urgency on different metrics besides email acquisition, such as engagement and conversion rates, to further optimize our marketing strategies.

experiments.title: How a donation interrupter impacts recurring giving during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of the recurring interrupter for Eagle Lake Donation Page donors will achieve an increase in recurring giving because the donor is asked to give less upfront but have a larger impact.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/09/2024 - 05/17/2024
Every year, The Navigators run two high-urgency campaigns focused on raising money for their Christ-centered summer camp called Eagle Lake. We have been actively testing ways to activate their donor file to monthly sustainers, and we wanted to bring these efforts into their Eagle Lake Campaign.
This interrupter only showed to half of the people who selected a one-time gift under $100 on one of their Eagle Lake donation pages.
The copy asked the visitor to convert their gift to a $10 monthly gift in support of Eagle Lake efforts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment show that the addition of the interrupter may have had a positive impact on converting The Navigators audience to become monthly sustainers.
A total of 120 donors were shown the interrupter and out of those 8 donors clicked "Yes" to convert their gift to a $10 monthly gift.
That is 6.67% conversion rate!
While we are unable to validate these results, it is encouraging to see almost 7% of donors willing to commit to a monthly gift and become a Sustainer.
We plan to continue testing the presence of the interrupter in an effort to create a larger Sustainers file for The Navigators.
In future experimentation, it is important to continue testing different approaches to increase recurring giving, such as optimizing the messaging and design of the interrupter, as well as testing different donation amounts and frequencies. Additionally, it is beneficial to analyze the donor file to identify specific segments that are more likely to convert to monthly sustainers and tailor the interrupter messaging accordingly. By continuing to test and optimize these strategies, nonprofits like The Navigators can effectively increase their recurring giving and generate more sustainable revenue for their programs such as the Eagle Lake summer camp.

experiments.title: How yes/no “autonomy” language on a pop-up impacts conversion rate during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a yes and no option for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the autonomy to make a micro-decision allows the visitor to feel in control before being asked for the gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/19/2024 - 04/27/2024
For The Navigators April Eagle Lake High Urgency campaign, we wanted to test using two buttons, both a yes and no option, on their homepage overlay/pop-up.
We hypothesized that providing the visitor with a micro-decision would lead to an increase in the donor conversion rate.
The control features the standard single call-to-action. Whereas, the treatment offers the call-to-action to donate as well as a “No, I’m not interested option.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that providing visitors with a choice between a yes and no option on the homepage overlay significantly increased the donor conversion rate for The Navigators April Eagle Lake High Urgency campaign. The treatment, which featured both options, resulted in a 193.8% increase in donations compared to the control, with a statistical level of confidence of 97.2%. This demonstrates that giving visitors the autonomy to make a micro-decision before being asked for a donation can positively impact conversion rates.
In future experimentation, The Navigators should continue to test and implement strategies that offer visitors a choice or a sense of control in the donation process. This can include using two buttons with different options, adjusting language to frame the call-to-action as a choice rather than a directive, or incorporating interactive elements that engage visitors in decision-making. By leveraging the learnings from this experiment, The Navigators can optimize their donation process and ultimately drive higher conversion rates and engagement with their campaigns.
