experiments.title: How a humanized sticky bar affects donor conversion rates on a CYE donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a humanized sticky bar on a CYE donation page for potential donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because it provides more urgency and a special message from a well-known figure at the organization. because  it provides more urgency and a special message from a well-known figure at the organization.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/29/2023 - 12/31/2023
ACI ran their annual calendar year-end campaign. In the final four emails of the campaign, they wanted to test how an urgent, personalized sticky bar signed by Dr. Ben Carson would perform. Typically, they see higher conversion through funnels and campaigns when messaging is directly from Dr. Carson, so we expected an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that using a personalized sticky bar signed by Dr. Ben Carson did not result in a higher donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment version of the email had a 19.3% decrease in Transactions for All Traffic compared to the control version, with a 95.1% level of confidence. Additionally, the treatment version experienced a significant 237% decrease in One-Time Donations with a 98% level of confidence.
These results suggest that the personalized sticky bar did not create the urgency and special message that was expected. Instead, it may have had a negative impact on donor conversion.
To use this learning in future experimentation, it is important to carefully consider the messaging and language used in fundraising campaigns. While using a well-known figure like Dr. Ben Carson may normally result in higher conversion rates, it is crucial to ensure that the messaging aligns with the organization's goals and resonates with potential donors. Additionally, it is important to conduct thorough testing and analysis before implementing new strategies to minimize potential negative impacts on donor conversion.

experiments.title: How a simplified message that focuses only on impact in the final email of a high urgency campaign affects conversions for lapsed members
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the message and only focusing on the impact in the final email of the campaign will produce a higher conversion rate because the treatment email presents a more straightforward and concise message, which can be easier for recipients to understand quickly.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/06/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was ran for lapsed members to Heritage asking them to reactivate their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how a concise message that only focuses on their impact in the final email of the campaign would affect conversion rates. We believe a clear and simple call-to-action may lead to a better response as it reduces cognitive load for the reader.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that simplifying the message and focusing only on the impact in the final email of the campaign resulted in a higher conversion rate. The treatment email, which presented a more straightforward and concise message, saw a 37.4% increase in clicks compared to the control email with a 96.4% level of confidence. Additionally, we saw a 15.4% decrease in donor conversion with only a 43% level of confidence which tells us that the change in email copy did not affect the motivation to give on the donation page.
Based on this learning, future experimentation should prioritize clear and simple call-to-action messages that highlight the impact of the organization. By reducing cognitive load for the readers and making it easier for them to understand the value of their membership, we can improve the conversion rates and encourage more individuals to reactivate their memberships.
Additionally, it is important to continue conducting A/B testing to validate and refine these findings. By testing different variations of messages, designs, and strategies, we can further optimize our campaigns and ensure that we are effectively engaging our audience.

The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was ran for nonmembers to Heritage asking them to activate their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how a concise message that only focuses on their impact in the final email of the campaign would affect conversion rates. We believe a clear and simple call-to-action may lead to a better response as it reduces cognitive load for the reader.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that simplifying the message and focusing on the impact in the final email of a campaign can lead to a higher conversion rate. The treatment email, which presented a more straightforward and concise message, resulted in a 9.5% increase in clicks for all traffic. Additionally, we saw a 6.2% decrease in donor conversion with only a 38.9% level of confidence which tells us that the change in email copy did not affect the motivation to give on the donation page.

To apply what was learned in future experimentation, it is important to continue to prioritize clear and simple call-to-action messages. By reducing cognitive load for the reader and making it easier for recipients to understand the message quickly, we can improve engagement and conversion rates. It is also important to monitor statistical confidence levels to ensure that the results of the experiments are reliable and can be confidently implemented.

experiments.title: How Yes/No “Autonomy” Language Impacts Clicks on a CYE Exit Intent
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that yes/no autonomy language for users on a CaringBridge visit profile will achieve increase in click-thru because users are more likely to interact with a call-to-action that presents an easy “micro-yes.”.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/11/2024
For CaringBridge’s CYE campaign, we hypothesized that Yes/No language on the call-to-action buttons would improve click-thru rate and send more traffic to the donation page. In the control, the call-to-action was “Donate Now” whereas in the treatment users were given a choice: “yes, I would like to give,” or “no, I’m not interested.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the hypothesis that yes/no autonomy language on the call-to-action buttons would improve click-thru rate and send more traffic to the donation page was proved to be incorrect. The treatment, which implemented the yes/no autonomy language, actually resulted in a 24.2% decrease in clicks for all traffic compared to the control.
This suggests that users were not more likely to interact with language that presents an easy "micro-yes" before being taken to the main donation page. In fact, it seems that the yes/no autonomy language may have confused or deterred users from clicking on the call-to-action buttons.
It should be noted that donor conversion rate wasn't captured for this experiment. It would be interesting to see the impacts downstream, whether less people making the commitment to "Yes, I would like to give" would still result in more conversions at the donation due to those users being more committed/motivated to give due to the micro-yes.

It is possible that this ultimately led the mission to sound more indvidual/Fazale focused rather than broadening to greater impact.

experiments.title: How an in-line appeal that becomes a sticky bar with a small donation CTA and opt-in for an email reminder impacts donor conversion during a Calendar Year-End Campaign

rows.name: Dear reader urgency with Scrolling Sticky Bar

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an in-line appeal that becomes a sticky bar reminding visitors of the value they’ve received from this organization’s articles will increase donor conversion by increasing the appeal and exclusivity of the offer
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/13/2022 - 12/25/2022
Historically, this organization used a combination of sticky bars and slide-outs with messaging around the urgent need of helping youth experiencing homelessness or housing insecurity  and have tied this urgency back to either a donor goal or a revenue goal. During their calendar year-end campaign, they often use a sticky bar with a daily donor goal countdown to convert visitors to their article pages into donors.
They wanted to test this against a design inspired by one of Wikipedia’s tests where there is an in-line appeal that becomes a sticky bar when scrolling. Instead of emphasizing the matching challenge in effect and the donor goal, the in-line appeal would ask the reader to consider the value they’ve received during the past year from their articles and tell them that less than 1% of readers donate to make these articles possible. Once the reader scrolled to continue reading the article, they would be prompted with the following, “If we have given you $5 worth of truth about housing insecurity this year, take a minute to donate.” They would then be asked for a gift of as little as $5 to “keep these exclusive articles possible to provide guidance to transform lives.”
If the visitor clicked the button to give, they would go to the donation page with the amount they selected. They could also click, “maybe later” which loaded an embedded HubSpot form that allowed them to opt-in for an email reminder to give. Those who opted in would receive an email reminder before the end of the campaign to make their gift.
Our hypothesis was that the conversational and value-driven copy on the in-line design and sticky bar, coupled with the option to opt-in for an email reminder would increase donor conversion by increasing the appeal and exclusivity of the offer. We would also be tapping into the donor’s desire to not lose access to these articles by highlighting how few people give to make them possible.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The new messaging and design crushed the control sticky bar with a daily donor goal. What is remarkable is that the avg gift was $29 despite the fact that the CTA was to make a $5 gift. This showed that readers felt that they were receiving much more value from the articles and content and wanted to make sure that it would still be available to them in the new year.

It's also worth noting that the dear reader/slide-up treatment brought in a $10,000 gift. We've pulled this out in the recording of this experiment so as not to skew the average gift or the revenue to give a more accurate picture of the overall results.

111 people opted in to receive follow-up emails to remind them about giving opportunities to this organization. We will be monitoring this segment to see downstream conversion as well as sending specific appeals to them reminding them that they asked to know more about ways to give at a later date.

We will continue to refine the messaging of this bar for future urgent campaigns as well as test into leveraging statistics of how few visitors/pledge signers/readers, etc donate to see if this messaging increases donor conversion on acquisition offer donation pages as well.

We also want to test the opening line, "we humbly ask you, don't scroll away" in other places to see if that conversational approach can improve conversion by keeping people on the page longer. For example, on an instant donation page after someone has requested an eBook or signed a pledge. Will that headline keep someone on the page long enough to read the donation appeal?

experiments.title: How a homepage donation modal impacts donor conversion during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a homepage donation modal will increase donor conversion by reducing friction and increasing the appeal of the donor CTA
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/08/2023 - 05/20/2023
During FamilyLife’s May Recurring Campaign, we wanted to increase donor conversion on the homepage. They were offering both a premium and a matching challenge for new recurring donors. We hypothesized that featuring a “modal” in the center of the page with the image of the premium offer and copy highlighting the matching fund, we could increase the appeal of the CTA and reduce friction by allowing donors to begin their gift right on the homepage.
We split the traffic between our standard homepage during the campaign and the treatment with the mid-page modal.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the homepage donation modal did not result in an increase in donor conversion. In fact, it led to a significant decrease in donations across all traffic sources.
This suggests that the homepage donation modal created friction or detracted from the user experience, leading to a decrease in donor conversion. It is possible that the modal was seen as intrusive or distracting, causing visitors to abandon the donation process. Also, the messaging of the modal may not have been aligned with the motivation of the traffic to the homepage. More testing would be needed to see if a different premium offer or value proposition angle would lead to higher conversion.

experiments.title: How an end of article dear reader emphasizing how few readers donate impacts donor conversion during a high urgency campaign

rows.name: End of Article Dear Reader - premium focused
End of Article Dear Reader - premium with 3% language

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a dear reader message that emphasizes how few readers donate will increase donor conversion during a high urgency campaign
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/01/2023 - 05/20/2023
During FamilyLife’s annual Spring recurring campaign, we wanted to test the best placement for a dear reader message featuring copy that mentions less than 3% of articles readers give each month to keep this content here for them on-air and online.
Traditionally, they’ve relied on an end-of-article dear reader promoting the campaign and offering a premium offer to those who donate. We wanted to test this against a dear reader that added language about how less than 3% of readers donate, believing this would increase the urgency of the CTA. To validate the placement of the message, we would test that same 3% language in a dynamic dear reader that would appear at the top of the page and become a sticky bar on scrolling. Due to size constraints of this placement, we would remove the language about the premium.
We split their article traffic evenly between all three versions of the page and measured the impact on recurring donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We found that emphasizing how few readers donate was compelling, no matter the placement. The best version of the dear reader in the experiment was the one that emphasized how few donate and offered the premium at the end of the article. More testing is needed to see if the above article deployment would have performed better if it too had offered the premium. Due to size constraints of that deployment, we did not get test that this time around.
Going forward we will continue to use language about how few readers donate in our dear reader messages, with additional testing focusing on ways to further refine and optimize that message.

experiments.title: How adding a humanized sticky bar to a campaign donation page affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar to a campaign donation page for prospective donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2022 - 12/03/2022
MAF was running a high-urgency campaign for Giving Tuesday. Due to the previous testing of this concept in other high-urgency campaigns, we hypothesized that adding a sticky bar on the donation page itself reiterates the goal and would increase donor conversion. However, we also wanted to humanize this sticky bar by using Aaron Bear’s (CAO and Vice President) image and name so that it seems like it’s a message from Aaron himself. The added layer of using an authority figure within the organization is expected to increase our conversion rate as well.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall, we saw a 49.8% lift in donor conversion rate with a 99% level of confidence when adding the humanized sticky bar to the campaign donation page. As hypothesized, we believe that adding a humanized sticky bar to a campaign donation page reiterates the goal of the campaign and personalizes the ask since we are using the face of someone that works at MAF.

Moving forward, we will rollout a humanized sticky bar to all campaign donation pages.

experiments.title: How a dear reader appeal for article readers impacts donor conversion outside of a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a dear reader for the article readers will achieve greater donor conversion because it will increase the appeal, clarity, and exclusivity of making a gift to CBN News because it will increase the appeal, clarity, and exclusivity of making a gift to CBN News.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/26/2023 - 02/18/2023
The Christian Broadcasting Network is a Christian news and media ministry that reaches millions of people around the world and provides humanitarian aid and practical resources to those in need. For their December Campaign, they tested a dear reader appeal to see if it would improve donor conversion on their article pages. While we weren’t able to validate a lift in conversion during the experiment, we had a directional lift in revenue which we hypothesized was due to the additional value proposition readers received from the dear reader message.
We wanted to retest this approach outside of the influences of a high-urgency campaign. We decided to keep the same value proposition from the two variants we tested in December and add a 3rd variation that would reference how fewer than 1% of readers give to keep CBN online and on-air.
Dear Reader #1 would emphasize CBN’s messaging and worldview. “CBN News doesn’t bombard you with negative views, secular agendas, skewed statistics, or reporters’ political viewpoints. We’re here to tell you the truth, and to give you hope. And every CBN News story that you read and watch relies on the generosity of readers like you.”
Dear Reader #2 would emphasize CBN’s unique expertise. “Our award-winning journalists give you 24/7 breaking news, U.S. and international news, health, and Christian entertainment news. It’s reporting that you aren’t likely to see anywhere else—information you’ve relied on for many years! And the CBN News stories you read and watch depend on the generosity of readers like you.
Dear Reader #3 would emphasize CBN’s unique expertise AND how few readers give to keep their articles available. “You might be surprised to discover that less than 1% of our readers give to keep CBN News Available.”
They split the traffic four ways and ran the experiment for 20 days.  We also excluded any donations over $1,000 from the results to prevent them from skewing the experiment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Consistently during the experiment, the dear reader approach was winning for donor conversion. However, there was a surge in giving on mobile in particular the last week of the test that caused V3 with the 1% statistic to win out overall. We have now validated that a dear reader is a successful tactic to use during important giving seasons on CBN News' article pages and that language emphasizing the exclusive award-winning journalism of CBN news is highly motivational to donors. Citing how few readers give to keep these articles available also seems to motivate higher generosity.

For our next experiment, we will test a different placement of this messaging using a dynamic popup that becomes a sticky bar when scrolling to see if that placement improves conversion against the winning dear reader.

experiments.title: How increasing the urgency of a banner appeal  during a high urgency campaign affects recurring donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding urgency to a banner appeal during a high-urgency campaign will increase recurring donor rates by more clearly expressing the need for the prospect to respond to the appeal and to do so now.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/15/2023 - 03/30/2023
We wanted to know how adding urgency to a banner appeal that has traditionally been run during high-urgency campaigns would impact recurring donor rates. The control presents an opportunity to begin your donation on the banner but is supported by generic copy that lacks urgency and does not communicate why someone should give and why they should give now.
We wanted to strengthen the appeal with a treatment that conveys the urgency of the problem in the copy and supports that urgency with a dynamic countdown timer that informs visitors how many monthly donors were still needed to meet the goal … and thus confront the problem.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 12 days, we were able to validate our hypothesis that adding urgency to a banner appeal during a high-urgency campaign would increase recurring donations, producing a 34.6% lift (with a 97% level of confidence) over the control.
For mobile visitors, which made up 72% of total users who entered this experiment, we observed a 66% lift in recurring gifts, with a 99% level of confidence.
Over an 11-month period, with outlier gifts over $1,000 removed, the treatment will produce roughly $13,000 additional revenue, showing the importance of using strong, urgent copy during fundraising campaigns—even in supporting elements outside of appeal emails.
We believe that by calling out the problem and indicating how many more gifts were still needed to hit the goal, we were able to make the appeal more tangible and urgent in the minds of potential donors.
Going forward, we will suggest the high-urgency approach as the control and may consider other ways to improve the appeal, such as highlighting a more specific impact or outcomes achieved from the donor's contribution.

experiments.title: How a Wikipedia-style ask performs against a banner ad and a slide-in appeal on article pages during a high-urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a Wikipedia-style banner on article pages will increase recurring donations during a high-urgency campaign because it presents a more compelling appeal in a prevalent location, leveraging urgency and reciprocity to inspire people to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/02/2023 - 03/16/2023
During past high-urgency campaigns, an organization we work with has traditionally run a banner appeal on article pages in an attempt to increase giving from traffic who visit these articles.
Because this organization earns a high volume of traffic to the article pages, we wanted to test the banner appeal against two treatments that we have seen success with in the past to test how they would perform against one another in the context of a high-urgency campaign specifically soliciting recurring donations.
We felt that the banner appeal, while visually attractive and prevalent on the page, lacked a value proposition that was compelling enough to inspire people to make the commitment to give a monthly recurring gift. Both treatments employed nearly identical language both speaking to the problem/solution being conveyed by the appeal and the urgency created by the fact that only 6% of all donors give on a monthly.
The two tactics we wanted to test were a) a Wikipedia-inspired top-of-page appeal that converts to a slide-up appeal when the user scrolls and b) a slide-in appeal that appears from the right side of the screen at about 30% page depth.
For this experiment, we split traffic between the banner appeal (control), the Wikipedia-inspired appeal (treatment A), and the slide-in appeal (treatment B), splitting traffic roughly evenly between each. It was our hypothesis that given its visibility and the appeal it communicates, the Wikipedia inspired ask would perform best.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for two weeks, we were able to validate a 156.9% increase in recurring donations produced by the Wikipedia-inspired banner appeal (99.8% level of confidence). In addition to this encouraging lift in recurring donations, we also observed:
582.2% in desktop recurring donations (34 vs. 5), 99.9% LoC75.5% in mobile recurring donations (14 vs. 8), 80% LoC28.1% in total donations, 89.5% LoC
These results suggest that the Wikipedia-inspired appeal is most effective in engaging visitors and compelling them to donate on a recurring basis.
We believe that the more compelling and thorough language used in the Wikipedia-inspired appeal—detailing the urgency of the problem, conveying the solution the donor can help achieve, and leveraging the persuasion principle of consistency by conveying that only 6% of donors give monthly—led to the increase in recurring gifts as compared to the control (banner).
And we believe that the more prominent location of the Wikipedia-inspired appeal led to the increase in recurring gifts as compared to treatment B (slide-in).
This experiment further validates the effectiveness of the Wikipedia-style banner tactic. Now, future experimentation can broaden this learning by investigating which persuasion principles work best in this context to improve on these results.

experiments.title: How urgency language impacted Facebook ad clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: Which element of urgency will drive response limited time or few left in stock?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/16/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas. Through radio, television, digital resources, and books, Dr. Robert Jeffress delivers bold, biblical truth to equip the saints in piercing a dark culture.
Each year PTV releases a new daily devotional book with 260 days of encouragement and teachings.
To boost response in the final days of the devotional being offered we decided to test whether an urgency of time or an urgency of exclusivity would drive results on Facebook.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment provided key learnings around the use of urgency to drive response on a Facebook campaign. The results showed that creating a sense of exclusivity, or creating an urgency of few left in stock, yielded 30.2 % more clicks compared to the control which only used an urgency of time. This illustrates the value of using scarcity when crafting a message on a digital campaign. Going forward, creating a sense of exclusivity, combined with a sense of limited time can be used across all types of marketing campaigns to further drive response. If a campaign is not time-sensitive, encouraging users to act quickly due to limited availability can be an effective way to increase response rates.
In addition to driving a click response, we saw a significant impact on donations. The few left in stock generated a 49.6% increase in donor conversions with an 87.1% level of confidence (13 to 75 donations)!
For revenue, we saw a 22.6% increase with a 70.9% level of confidence.
While donations and revenue did not yield confidence levels over the required 95%, we did see directional lifts in a positive direction.

experiments.title: How opening a loop of curiosity via the subject line affects open rates during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a less direct subject line will increase open rates by creating an open loop of curiosity that recipients will want to close by opening the emails to learn more.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/14/2022
During a year-end campaign when we know potential donors will be receiving a high volume of emails soliciting donations, we wanted to know whether or not using less direct subject lines would improve open rates by opening a loop of curiosity that the recipient would want to close by opening the email.
Recipients of this campaign were segmented between previous donors and non-donors and roughly half of both segments were sent emails with subject lines that spoke directly to the contents of the email while the other half received emails that opened a loop of curiosity with less direct subject lines … but which (importantly) would still clearly relate to the subject matter of the email once the recipient opened. This test was run across three appeal emails in an effort to ensure the results were not isolated to a single send.
experiments.keyLearnings:
When looking at the results of this experiment a number of interesting observations came to light:
The curiosity-building subject lines did indeed increase open rates by 2.6% (99.9% LoC)However, the curiosity-building subject lines also decreased click-through rates by -8.5% (97.4% LoC)
But because these sends were all segmented between previous donors and non-donors, drilling down into the data may help paint a more complete picture of our results, for instance:
Among non-donors:
Curiosity-building subject lines produced a 2.4% increase in open rates (100% LoC)But they also produced a 16.6% decrease in click-through rates  (100% LoC)
Among previous donors:
Curiosity-building subject lines produced a 4% increase in open rates (100% LoC)While also producing a 33.8% increase in click-through rates (99.9% LoC)
And so we see that not all recipients behave the same, further emphasizing the importance of tailoring communications to the audience that you are targeting, and doing away with the assumption that all audiences behave the same.
Non-donors may be less familiar with (or less bought into) the value American Cornerstone Institute provides—hence their status as non-donors—while previous donors, who better understand (or better align) with the value received from ACI were hooked by the curiosity-building subject line and continued their momentum through the appeal contained inside the email, ultimately clicking through to the donation page at a significantly higher rate.
Based on these results, we will want to send emails with direct subject lines to the non-donor segment while sending curiosity-building emails to the previous donor segments as the results from this experiment have clearly indicated each segment's preference.

experiments.title: How adding a sticky bar to a year-end fundraising campaign donation page affects donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar to the donation page of a year-end fundraising campaign will increase donation rates by humanizing and further conveying the value of the appeal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2022 - 01/04/2023
During a year-end fundraising campaign for a faith-based education organization, we wanted to test how adding a humanized sticky bar to the donation page would affect donation rates.
We set this experiment up as a redirect test with ~50% of traffic seeing the standard donation page, sans sticky bar, and the other half seeing a humanized sticky bar from one of the organization’s staff members (including a photo) making a personal appeal and expressing the value of the prospect’s donation.
We ran this test for 10 days, during the last stretch of the fundraising campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 10 days, the treatment featuring a humanized sticky bar achieved a 14.5% increase in donation rates with an 87.1% statistical level of confidence when compared to the control.
In addition, the treatment including the sticky bar increased revenue per visitor by 151% with a 99% level of confidence, indicating that not only did the sticky bar encourage more people to give, it inspired them to give more generously.
We believe that including a sticky bar featuring a staff member of the organization was able to increase donations among page visitors by humanizing and putting a face to the appeal, reminding people that this organization is comprised of real people, just like them, who are trying to make a difference.
And even though we did not receive a large enough sample size to statistically validate these results, we did observe a directional lift that indicates there is low risk associated with implementing this treatment and we therefore feel confident in recommending the inclusion of a humanized sticky bar to 100% of the traffic during their next high-urgency campaign.

experiments.title: How adding impact language to a direct-ask pop up banner during a high urgency campaign affects revenue – marriages saved V1
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the language on the gift array of a direct ask pop-up to show the impact of the gift will result in greater revenue
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/23/2022 - 12/02/2022
Focus on the Family (FOTF) is a global Christian ministry dedicated to helping families thrive. They provide help and resources for couples to build healthy marriages that reflect God’s design and for parents to raise their children according to morals and values grounded in biblical principles. Every year during the calendar year-end high-urgency campaign, FOTF places a top-of-page pop-up banner that contains a direct ask to support their mission. This banner (known internally as the “wiki-banner”) has been tested into over many years, with experiments to improve donation metrics. Since many people visit FOTF.com during this season to make a year-end gift, the “wiki-banner” serves as a low-friction tactic to get people to the donation page quickly. It is important to note that the visiting audience to FOTF’s website is served different content / experiences based on pages they have consumed in the past…specifically a “marriage audience” and an “in favor of pro-life audience – Santicity of Human Life” (also known as SOHL).
For this specific experiment targeting the “marriage audience”, we were focused on changing the language on the gift array buttons to reflect the impact that a gift would make on the amount of marriages saved. The theory was that if we could directly show how many marriages could be saved with a specific gift amount, the visitor would be moved to give a bigger gift to make a greater impact. As screen real-estate is at a premium for this “wiki-banner”, we designed an experience that showed the number of marriages saved on a button on the wiki-banner, and in a “hover-state” it would flip to the equivalent dollar amount of the gift. We even added a free-form field that would allow you to add how many marriages you wanted to save, and it would calculate that amount in real-time.  After making a selection, the user is directed to the donation form where the amount they chose is auto-populated on the giving form, to reduce friction. It is also important to note that we were testing for revenue lift on desktop only (not mobile due to screen size limitations).
experiments.keyLearnings:
If you notice, this experiment is labeled V1. We launched this pre-Giving Tuesday (11/23/22)....and were seeing very positive results...that is until the experiment was "over-ridden" inadvertently by FOTF due to some internal development directives. The over-ride happened right before Giving Tuesday, so we stopped getting results and ended up manually stopping the experiment on Dec 2, 2022. Prior to that stoppage, we recorded 236% lift in revenue for the treatment version with a 99.2% Level of Confidence. By statistical measures, this was a valid lift. Even when we removed outlier donations over $2k from both the control and the treatment, we still saw a significant lift from the treatment.
Due to the technical over-ride, we did not feel comfortable relying on these results to make any future recommendations other than to re-build the experiment as it was, and quickly re-launch to see if the results continued to show a positive lift in revenue through the remainder of the year-end campaign. Results from V2 will be shown in a future write-up and linked here.

experiments.title: How adding impact language to a direct-ask pop up banner during a high urgency campaign affects revenue – babies saved – V3 relaunch
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the language on the gift array of a direct ask pop-up to show the impact of the gift will result in greater revenue
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/11/2022 - 12/31/2022
Focus on the Family (FOTF) is a global Christian ministry dedicated to helping families thrive. They provide help and resources for couples to build healthy marriages that reflect God’s design and for parents to raise their children according to morals and values grounded in biblical principles. Every year during the calendar year-end high-urgency campaign, FOTF places a top-of-page pop-up banner that contains a direct ask to support their mission. This banner (known internally as the “wiki-banner”) has been tested into over many years, with experiments to improve donation metrics. Since many people visit FOTF.com during this season to make a year-end gift, the “wiki-banner” serves as a low-friction tactic to get people to the donation page quickly. It is important to note that the visiting audience to FOTF’s website is served different content / experiences based on pages they have consumed in the past…specifically a “marriage audience” and an “in favor of pro-life audience – Santicity of Human Life” (also known as SOHL).
For this specific experiment targeting the “SOHL“, we were focused on changing the language on the gift array buttons to reflect the impact that a gift would make on the amount of babies saved. The theory was that if we could directly show how many babies could be saved with a specific gift amount, the visitor would be moved to give a bigger gift to make a greater impact. As screen real-estate is at a premium for this “wiki-banner”, we designed an experience that showed the number of babies saved on a button on the wiki-banner, and in a “hover-state” it would flip to the equivalent dollar amount of the gift. We even added a free-form field that would allow you to add how many babies you wanted to save, and it would calculate that amount in real-time.  After making a selection, the user is directed to the donation form where the amount they chose is auto-populated on the giving form, to reduce friction. It is also important to note that we were testing for revenue lift on desktop only (not mobile due to screen size limitations).
NOTE: This is the third version of the experiment (V3 relaunch) due to technical challenges of the V1 + V2 version not properly returning data.
experiments.keyLearnings:
If you notice, this experiment is labeled V3 relaunch. We launched this one AFTER V1 + V2 did not return any data. As it turns out, the previous versions of this experiment did not include a pivotal piece of code that would identify this target audience as having consumed "SOHL" content in the past. As such, the previous experiments returned no data.
Once we corrected the errors, we re-launched this experiment in parallel with the experiment that was targeting "marriage audiences". Like it's "sister-experiment", this version ran from 12/11 to 12/31. Unlike the "sister-version" of this experiment, the results showed an increase in the revenue by around 82% with a 98.6% Level of Confidence....and thus statistically validating for the treatment.
Like its' sister experiment....This led us to even more questions like...
What would results have looked like earlier in the campaign if the code had been correct?Why does this audience respond more positively to impact language than the marriage audience? Does the timing of the experiment change the results?
There were several key take-aways that came from this series of experiments.
Timing/motivation may play a big role in the effectiveness of impact language, but we cannot be sure due to the technical challenges experienced early in the campaign.Make sure you are always monitoring your experiments for data anomalies, and don't be afraid to end an experiment early and re-launch if the data does not "look right"We cannot say definitively that the impact language led to great revenue during the majority of the high-urgency campaign...but we can say that there is enough data to show a positive trend and learnings that we will take into our next high-urgency campaign experiment

experiments.title: How a homepage takeover affects donor conversion rate at calendar year end
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that taking over the homepage with a donation form for main domain visitors` will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/30/2022 - 01/04/2023
IMB was running their calendar year end campaign around their Lottie Moon Christmas Offering. To reduce friction at a time of the year when conversion rate is highest, they wondered if turning their homepage into the donation page would increase donor conversion by reducing the necessary steps between the visit and a gift.
They set up a treatment where some donors would see a donation page with a link to “go to IMB.org” at the top of the page instead of the homepage.
To mitigate risk, they only included 25% of the traffic in this experiment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment, which showed a donation page on the first step, produced a 30.6% increase in conversion rate. This is significant, as experiments like this are often hard to validate at the end of the year, when motivation to give is highest. However, the reduction in friction showed that donors are still falling out of the funnel, even when coming in with high motivation.
This technique should not be overused, but could also be used with specific campaigns.

experiments.title: How using a humanized sticky bar during a high-urgency campaign impacts donation conversion rates.

rows.name: Sticky Bar - Humanized with Dan's Photo

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a sticky bar for website visitors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/20/2022 - 01/04/2023
During Avila Institute’s year-end campaign, we wanted to capitalize on traffic to one of their most popular websites, SpiritualDirection.com, and run a 3-way experiment to see whether a sticky bar made an impact on donations.
For this experiment, our control was no sticky bar; variant one had a sticky bar with just copy; variant 2 had a sticky bar with a picture of Dan Burke, the founder of Avila. Both variant 1 and 2 had the exact same copy. We ran this sticky bar on all pages on the website.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we expected a sticky bar to outperform no sticky bar, the results of adding in Dan's image were really interesting. The Dan image version of the stick bar had a 92% increase in conversion rates compared to both the control and variant 1. We believe that this is due to Dan's notoriety within the Avila community; everyone knows who he is, what he looks like, and by adding an image we humanized the message a little more, giving donors the perception that it was really Dan asking for urgent help during the campaign.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 89.4% increase with a 94% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
(Other) visitors had a 129.3% increase in donations with an 90% level of confidence.Email visitors had a 57.2% increase in donations with an 86% level of confidence.Organic Search visitors had a 423.5% increase in donations with a 90% level of confidence.Male visitors had a 227.5% increase in donations with an 87% level of confidence.35-44 visitors had a 1.3% increase in donations with a 96% level of confidence.Desktop visitors had a 54.1% increase in donations with an 87% level of confidence.Mobile visitors had a 123.0% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.New  visitors had a 209.2% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a yes/no pop up impacts donor conversion rate during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking  a value-prop yes/no question  for site visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/05/2022 - 12/11/2022
Navigators was running a high urgency campaign at the end of the calendar year. In order to capture more donations from web visitors we ran a test on the CYE popup/overlay. We wanted to see if we could more people to click through and donate on a pop up that was more value prop-centric with a yes/no question. The control for this experiment used campaign-specific language and the treatment asks the site visitor a yes/no question. If they select yes, they’re taking to the donation page. If they select no, the pop up exits out.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a valid 44% decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 95.9%. These results negated our initial hypothesis. However, based on previous experiments we've ran with the Navigators, these results are consistent with their audience's behavior. While the second pop up has the site visitor align with a certain value claim, it's not direct in what we're asking the person to do: donate. Previous experiment results show us that this organization's audience responds to more directs communications.

experiments.title: How using a faux forward on a high urgency campaign impacts donor conversion rates
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a faux forward for giving tuesday donors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2022 - 11/29/2022
Giving Tuesday is generally a big day of giving for many non-profits. In order to cut through the noise, non-profits generally use a variety of tactics for their afternoon and evening resends, the most common of which is a faux forward. But does it really work?
For the Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate, we put that question to the test. For the PM version of their Giving Tuesday emails, across multiple email and donor segments, we tested whether a resend of the AM version (same copy, same subject line) would have as much impact as a faux forward, where the sender is from a different person and it looks like that person is forwarding the original, AM email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For the Oblates, the question of whether using a faux forward tactic, during a time of year when everyone is receiving hundreds of emails, works has been answered. There was a huge increase in donor conversion rates from the faux forward, with a 73% increase and a 99.9% level of confidence.
Additionally, this experiment wielded the following interesting points:
While open rates are not a great metric to use for comparison, one of the main points of a faux forward is to get the reader to open the email. The treatment version averaged a 31% open rate, compared to 27% for the resend. When looking at performance by donor type (donor, recurring donor, non-donor) the lift continues. The donor segments saw a .61% response rate for the faux forward version versus a .5% response rate on the resend. Non-donor response rates also saw similar performance, with a 0.1% response rate on the control versus 0.2% response rate on the faux forward version.

experiments.title: How adding specificity of impact to a high urgency campaign donation page impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding specificity to the donor’s impact for prospective Giving Tuesday donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/02/2022
During Reasons to Believe’s Giving Tuesday campaign they ran a test on the donation page to create more specificity around the impact of the donor’s gift on Giving Tuesday. Instead of generally telling the donor they need to give because it’s Giving Tuesday we highlighted the “why” of their gift, using language like: you will offer someone the chance to have their life changed forever… we need your help to continue providing these opportunities… etc.
The treatment for this donation page was a complete rewrite of the control. Traffic to this experiment was generated primarily through housefile emails and paid social ads.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a 411% valid lift in donor conversion rate. While there are a lot of elements changed in the treatment, this informs us that RTB's audiences are highly motivated by knowing how their gift is being used and what their impact looks like. This treatment was rolled out to the remainder of the campaign to maximize results.

experiments.title: How using a ‘donation interruptor’ affects recurring giving rates during a Giving Tuesday high-urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that interrupting the donation process with a pop-up for donors on Giving Tuesday will improve recurring gift conversion rates by increasing its visibility at the decision-making point and prominently communicating its value.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/28/2022 - 12/03/2022
In an effort to increase recurring donor acquisition for a faith-based client during a Giving Tuesday campaign, we wanted to measure the effect of interrupting the donation process with a pop-up asking the donor to become a monthly supporter instead of making only a one-time gift.
This pop-up was set up as an A/B test that would only fire for 50% of donors who were in the process of completing a one-time gift under $100, reiterating the value of becoming a monthly donor both in terms of impact and exclusive benefits.
We tested this same hypothesis with the same client during last year’s Giving Tuesday campaign, and while we saw an encouraging lift in recurring giving, we were unable to validate our results—so we were eager to see how the same concept would perform in a similar setting during this year’s campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Due to an increase in the number of donors (sample size) over last year's campaign, we were pleased to be able to pull a valid learning from this year's experiment—with our treatment producing a statistically valid 137% increase in recurring gifts with a 98.4% level of confidence.
We believe that several motivating factors came into play in producing this result:
First, the interrupter pop-up dramatically increased the visibility of the recurring-giving option. Second, it clearly and thoroughly laid out the value of becoming a recurring donor in terms of impact and exclusive benefitsThird, by creating a mental interruption, it may have given donors an opportunity to reset the mental process of completing their donation (transaction forms being as prevalent as they are in the digital space may cue a certain degree of auto-pilot during the transaction process) and allowed them the time to consider their options.And finally, the pop-up treatment positioned monthly giving as an opportunity rather than an ask, giving agency to the donor and casting them in the role of someone who is able to do (and get) more than they would by making a one-time gift.
We also observed that the treatment produced a statistically valid increase of 210% in recurring giving among donors using mobile devices, important as we continue to see an increase in mobile giving over time.
These results are a positive indication that a similar treatment may work for other clients in the context of high-urgency campaigns and we will seek to globalize this learning by implementing it for new audiences and new giving contexts, such as content offers or direct appeals.

experiments.title: How subject line urgency impacted email performance
experiments.researchQuestion: Which subject line will have the biggest impact on subscriber engagement (opens and clicks)?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/30/2016 - 01/13/2017
As the year end deadline approached for Illinois Policy Institute’s year end fundraising campaign, they wanted to find ways to communicate that urgency to the subscribers. For the second-to-last email send, sent out on December 30th, they decided to run a subject line test to determine which would have the biggest impact on subscriber engagement.
Both of the subject lines communicated urgency. The primary difference between the two was that one, time is running out, is more direct in its approach and is a more common approach taken by many organizations in their fundraising and marketing-style emails. The alternative, tick tock, was a bit less common and did not give away the purpose of the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We found that the more direct and “sale-y” subject line of time is running out decreased the open rate by 8%. Additionally, this lower open rate led to an incrementally lower click through rate with a 33% decrease (with 94.6% statistical validity). Our hypothesis is that with the time is running out subject line, the subscribers immediately felt marketed to since it is not a subject line most people would use in their day to day communications. As a result, they were less likely to engage.

This is a great example of the concept that people give to people. As fundraisers, we want to communicate urgency but we need to do it in such a way that it still feels genuine.

experiments.title: How visually increasing urgency affects landing page conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which combination of elements creates the greatest sense of urgency that allows for the increase in conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/24/2014 - 12/28/2014
CaringBridge was in the final week of their 2014 year-end fundraising campaign and expected traffic to dramatically increase to their donation pages. They wanted to maximize on-page conversion by adding elements to the page to increase urgency: reminding people of the proximity to December 31st.
We knew that adding elements like a countdown clock and a thermometer would increase urgency, but we tested to see which element (or combination of elements) would maximize landing page conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: The countdown clock was able to increase the urgency of the ask which, in turn, lead to the increase in conversion rate. However, when combined with the thermometer element of the campaign, it negatively affected the conversion rate. This may be due to the large goal seeming out of reach for the average donor. Whatever the case, for large campaigns like this, the countdown clock will be deployed in the future to increase conversion rate.

experiments.title: How emphasizing the value of giving monthly vs incentives impacts click thru rate in a high urgency campaign email
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing on the value of monthly giving rather than the incentives of monthly giving  for prospective donors  will achieve a higher click thru rate and a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/26/2022
Reasons to Believe was running a high urgency campaign to acquire new recurring donors. Most of the campaign copy was geared towards the incentives for a monthly donor (i.e. special perks, exclusive access, etc.). In one of the last emails of the campaign we wanted to test adjusting the language so that it emphasizes the value of giving monthly vs a 1 time gift. Our hypothesis is that prospective donors will be more motivated to give when they understand why monthly giving is valuable.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment resulted in a 125% valid lift in click thru rate with a 99% level of confidence. This informs us that RTB's audience is more engaged in the email and are more likely to click thru when they're given the value of monthly giving. However, as we dug deeper we saw 11 donations come from the control and 9 donations come from the treatment. This translates into a 18% decrease in donations with a level of confidence of 34.5%. This is not a statistically significant decrease but we were expecting an increase in donor conversion rate. This might be because the actual donation page also had a heavy emphasis on incentives and the messaging of value was not consistent and carried through in the next step.
It's recommended that the donation page copy is tested next time to determine if this language truly motivates more donors to take action.

experiments.title: How adding urgency and clarity in the opening line of an ad and relatability through the ad image impacted clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding urgency and clarity in the opening line of an ad and relatability through the ad image for ad visitors would increase click-through rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 09/09/2022
TPPF’s Keep Texas Texan petition was experiencing a low click-through rate, so we tested a few different ad angles to see if they would increase clicks. We wondered if grabbing the reader’s attention right off the bat with a statement that they could “get behind” and clearly understand what was at stake would increase click-through rate. We also wondered if the existing ad image was experiencing user fatigue, and therefore tested two new images to see how they impact click-through rate. We ran an a/b/c test. The copy for the “b” and “c” variants differs from the “a” variant only in the opening sentence of the copy.
The “b” variant has a different image than “a”, using an image with symbols that represent Texas and that almost any Texan would recognize. The “c” variant has a different image as well, showing a person riding a horse in open Texas land – an image that we hypothesized Texans would relate to and distinguish themselves through.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Both treatment 1 and 2 led to an increase in ad clicks. Treatment 1 saw a 38.9% lift in clicks with a 96.9% level of confidence and treatment 2 saw a 83.4% lift in clicks with 99.9% level of confidence. This tells us that users did appreciate the clarity in the opening line of the ad copy, as they learned immediately what the ad was asking of them (to sign a petition) and what the petition was for (to keep Texas Texan). Also, by pointing at this this petition was "critical", users likely felt more of a sense of urgency to click the ad to reach the critical petition. As these variables were not isolated, we don't know for sure whether the clarity or urgency factored more into their decision to click through to the landing page.

Ultimately, treatment 2 experienced the biggest life of the three variants. This tells us that the ad image in treatment 2 resonated the most with users. They're asked to complete a petition to "keep Texas Texan", and the image of a man riding a horse through an open field on a dirt path rightly represents Texas. Users were more likely to click an ad image that offered authenticity than an image that was more "cartoony". That's because users see Texas and Texans as "authentic".

This ad approach is worth testing with other ads and for other offers. The results and learning would be clearer, however, if the ad variants are isolated. We don't know for sure if users were more motivated to click because of the copy change or the image change.

experiments.title: How urgency impacts email acquisition rate on a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a Facebook ad that stresses the urgency of a course launch increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/11/2017 - 10/16/2017
Dallas Theological Seminary was making preparations to launch its new ‘mini’ course on the life and work of Martin Luther. They wondered: If we tell visitors to pre-enroll in this course by stressing the urgency of the upcoming launch date, will email acquisition increase? They launched an A/B test leading up to their launch date to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Dallas Theological Seminary found that the treatment version that stressed the upcoming launch date did, in fact, increase email acquisition by 88%. By using the actual date of the launch (October 16, 2017) and the word ‘Monday’ in their call-to-action, Dallas Theological Seminary was able to signal to viewers that this was an urgent offer with a specific launch date. It likely made viewers feel that they were going to miss out on a particular event, even though the course was still offered after the launch date.
This strategy has prompted further tests, especially as Dallas Theological Seminary builds plans to launch new courses.

experiments.title: How personalizing and removing heavy design affects high urgency campaign emails for donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that personalizing an email for alumni and potential donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/21/2022 - 09/22/2022
Luther Seminary ran their annual 24-hour Give Day high urgency campaign from 9/21 – 9/22. The emails sent in this campaign were heavily designed and had a marketing feel rather than a personalized, conversation feel.
We wanted to test removing the design from the emails and changing the language to be more conversational from an actual person at the seminary.
We made these changes across the 9 emails for the alumni and all other segments since those were the only two segments large enough to test within.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment saw a 23.1% decrease in conversion rate with a 95.4% level of confidence across the 9 emails sent on Give Day. We concluded that Luther Seminary's audience is accustomed to the highly designed, marketing emails therefore the personalized emails did not increase the likelihood of someone giving a donation.
We believe that the timeline and words of inspiration at the top of the control email actually provided a sense of encouragement and urgency to give that day. Whereas the treatment emails were more focused on making a connection with the potential donor through conversation-like language.
Another notable takeaway from this experiment is that the click-through rates on the emails saw 19.7% decrease with a 98.2% level of confidence from the treatment emails.
We would like to further test this concept in another high-urgency campaign that spans a longer period of time. We believe this will provide more accurate information than a campaign that has 9 emails within 24 hours.

experiments.title: How asking for a gift less than a previous contribution impacts a fiscal year end campaign
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that asking for a lower gift than HPC for donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/01/2022 - 06/30/2022
The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate serve poor and needy people in the United States and more than 70 countries around the world.  In June, they announced a rebranding, where they combined 3 of the organizations that operated under the Missionary Oblates umbrella into one- The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate – USA. This rebranding also coincided with the end of their fiscal year, so as part of their fiscal-year end high urgency campaign, we focused on what the new organization would do (increased efficiencies in fundraising).
With the economic uncertainty of high inflation, prices, and potential recession, we ran a month-long campaign where we tested asking for a lower than previous gift for the donor segment, using language in our treatment variant that said :
Specifically, I am looking for << Test Donors Needed Qty >> people to give $<< Test Ask Amount >> right now. << Test First Name >> you have generously given $<< Test HPC Amount >> to us in the past, but with all the uncertainty in the world, continued inflation and high prices, I’m not asking for the same level of support. Instead, your gift of $<< Test Ask Amount >>, when combined with the << Test Donors Needed Qty >> other supporters of the Missionary Oblates, will ensure that we can continue to share the Good News of Jesus Christ to a lost and hurting world.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The overall campaign was a success for the Missionary Oblates, raising 16% more than the previous fiscal-year end campaign. For this experiment specifically, the 'downgrade' treatment version had a 62% higher conversion rate with 100% level of confidence! It also had a 4% higher average gift.
This shows that, even in a tough economic climate, people are willing to support the causes they truly believe in. And folding in the re-branding messaging made the appeal even stronger, as we were able to provide specific value statements that tied the donor closer to the organization.

experiments.title: How using direct language in pop-up copy for a high urgency campaign affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that directly asking if Working Preacher is a helpful resource for people viewing the Working Preacher website will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/10/2022 - 05/26/2022
Luther Seminary runs a high urgency campaign for Working Preacher every year during the fall and spring. Luther Seminary ran an A/B pop-up test during the fall campaign between copy about helping pastors receive the resources they need and copy about supporting the Working Preacher campaign. The copy around supporting the Working Preacher campaign and website saw a 7.9% lift with a 41.9% LoC.
For the spring campaign, they wanted to use copy around supporting the campaign and website, but they tested more detailed copy around what Working Preacher provides for users against shorter copy that more directly asked the user if Working Preacher helped them as an individual.
They crafted an alternate popup and split the traffic to see if it produced a conversion increase.
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we observed was a 49.5% increase in donor conversion rate on the treatment with a 97.9% level of confidence. The results indicated that the Working Preacher audience responds more to a direct and specific ask about whether or not Working Preacher was valuable to them as an individual. Being direct on what results from their gift by helping them specifically grow closer to God and lead others vs being abstract by saying Working Preacher helps people and pastors all over the world will inspire more gifts leading to a higher donor conversion rate.
Another takeaway from this experiment is that mobile visitors had a 229.8% increase in donations with a 95% level of confidence. We believe this to be caused by the treatment having shorter pop-up copy therefore the user was more inclined to click on the "Make your donation" button because the shorter copy caused less friction.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 74.8% increase with a 100% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Mobile visitors had a 229.8% increase in donations with a 95% level of confidence.Returning visitors had a 56.1% increase in donations with a 98% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How removing a progress thermometer to a donation page affects donation rates during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing a progress thermometer on a campaign donation page will result in lower conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/19/2022 - 08/08/2022
In previous high-urgency campaigns, we’ve had success increasing conversion rates by showing a progress thermometer on the top of the donation page once we were nearing our goal. Knowing this, we wanted to see if we could replicate these results during a recent high-urgency campaign for a public policy client. We hoped to increase conversion rates by inspiring prospects to be one of the donors that helped “fill the bar” and help us meet our goal.
This concept attempts to employ the principles of urgency (we only have X to go), social proof (many others found this cause worthy to support), and unity (belonging to a group) in an effort to inspire donors to act.
We initially planned to show the progress thermometer to 100% of traffic, but then we decided to test this concept again and instead split traffic 50/50 between the control (including the progress thermometer) and the treatment (without the progress thermometer), hypothesizing that removing the thermometer would reduce conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Despite previous success using the progress thermometer concept during high urgency campaigns, in this instance removing the thermometer produced a valid lift of 17.1% at a 99% level of confidence.
Interestingly, while removing the progress thermometer increased donations, it also resulted in an 8.7% decrease in revenue. And while we were unable to validate this decrease (75.9% level of confidence), when we combine this finding with the fact that the average gift given by donors increased by 24.8% with the thermometer, it appears that while removing the thermometer increased the amount of gifts received, including the thermometer improved the quality of gifts and ultimately raised more money—presumably by motivating donors to give more so that the goal could be reached and because the amount of money already raised indicated that this was a worthy cause to support.
Because of the mixed results produced by this experiment, it may be worth seeking additional opportunities to test this hypothesis again, focusing on revenue, in an effort to validate the portion of our findings that indicated that including the progress thermometer increased overall revenue during the time this experiment was run.

experiments.title: How a sticky donate button with a campaign specific CTA effects donor conversion during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a CTA on a sticky donate button related to an ongoing high urgency campaign will result in a higher number of donations, than a generic donate button.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/07/2022 - 06/22/2022
Focus on the Family runs a high urgency campaign every year centered around pro-life issues. This year, they wanted to test if adding a sticky donate button to article pages with a call to action mirroring the ongoing high urgency campaign would result in higher donor conversions than a similar button with a generic call to action. It is worth noting that this experiment was limited to a segmented audience that used user data to identify if a user had prior pro-life interests. For the control, a generic donate button (with a pulsing heart icon) was placed on article pages that matched the data segmentation. For the treatment, a sticky button with the words ” Save a Baby” (with a pulsing baby icon) was placed on article pages that matched the data segmentation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
During this experiment that had 91,000 samples over a two week period, we saw a 55% increase for the treatment with a 95.5% Level of Confidence. In this case, this experiment validated for the hypothesis. Key takeaways from this experiment are that matching the CTA of the button to the segmented audience during a high urgency campaign can result in a greater conversion.
Next steps for the experiment would be to test his same hypothesis using the same data segments but do so during a non-high urgency campaign to see if properly segmented users are still converted at a higher frequency with a specific CTA.

experiments.title: How adding social proof and urgency on a landing page impacted survey submissions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding social proof and urgency toward a specific goal for survey takers will achieve an increase in email acquisition.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/11/2022 - 05/12/2022
ACI was running a survey with high volume but a lower than ideal conversion rate. We evaluated the page for opportunities to increase email acquisition. The first treatment we developed included the element of social proof and let people know that over 55,000 other people had already submitted the survey to increase legitimacy of the offer. We visually highlighted this with a live counter at the top of the page. For the second treatment, we added language to let visitors know we had a goal of 100,000 submissions and their survey response would count toward that goal. We also emphasized this with a progress bar at the top of the page and asked for their help in getting closer to reaching the goal.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running for the experiment for one month, when looking at overall traffic, the second treatment had a 3.4% lift in email acquisition with an 81.9% level of confidence. However, when we assessed mobile traffic specifically, we saw a 6.4% directional increase in email acquisition with 94.1% level of confidence on the first treatment and a valid 9.1% increase in email acquisition with 99.3% level of confidence. Additionally, when we isolated traffic by new visitors we also had a 4.9% directional increase in email acquisition with a 91% level of confidence. We believe this was due to the increase in urgency and motivation by highlighting the progress towards the goal on the landing page for page visitors. Because mobile visitors make up almost half of the traffic to the page and neither treatment was showing a decline for overall traffic, we will move forward with rolling out the second treatment to 100% of traffic for future survey takers. For future testing, we could also increase urgency on the landing page by adding a countdown timer or by indicating a specific deadline in the copy to reach 100K responses.
experiments.fluxMetrics:
The Flux Metrics analyze the three primary metrics that affect revenue (traffic, conversion rate, and average
				gift).

experiments.title: How adding a donor countdown on a sticky bar affects donor conversion rate during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a micro-goal and donor countdown on a sticky bar  for site visitors  will achieve a high donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/26/2022 - 05/08/2022
RTB was running a recurring giving (Monthly Partner) campaign. Their goal was 100 new recurring donors by the end of the campaign. In order to bring awareness to the campaign, the used a sticky bar on their website to drive traffic to the Monthly Partner donation page. While they’ve been using “100 monthly donors” in their messaging, we hypothesized that breaking down the goal into a smaller, more achievable goal would drive more donations. We stated that “10 monthly donors” are needed today in order to provide a micro-goal. In addition to that change, we had the button have an active countdown on how many monthly donors were left in the daily goal.
Traffic to this page was primarily driven organically.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment resulted in a 100% increase in donor conversion with a level of confidence of 95%. However through most of the duration, the experiment was targeted to only the homepage (not site-wide), which gave us a smaller sample size than expected. Nonetheless, the experiment did validate and informs us that RTB's audiences are more likely to respond to a smaller, achievable goal in a recurring donor campaign. The larger goal of 100 donors can potentially feel like a daunting goal that the donor cannot realistically contribute to.
After this experiment ran, we launched a site-wide pop up with both the micro goal and the donor countdown. This is worth retesting for a different high urgency campaign to see if we get the same type of response.

experiments.title: How changing a call-to-action on a Facebook ad during a high urgency campaign increased donor conversion rates
experiments.organization: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
categoryNames: The Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing a CTA button on Facebook ads for a digital audience will achieve a higher donor conversion rate. .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/10/2022 - 04/17/2022
Easter is a key fundraising season for the Missionary Oblates of Mary Immaculate. Our Easter/Spring high urgency campaign this year focused on a recurring gift offer, where any monthly gift of $15 or more would have a votive candle lit for the donor’s intentions every month at the Oblate’s main shrine, the National Shrine of Our Lady of the Snows.
For our Facebook ad campaign, we wanted to test whether a ‘donate now’ call-to-action button would drive a higher donor conversion rate than the ‘learn more’ button Oblates traditionally uses. Our hypothesis was that due to the urgent nature of the campaign, a donate now CTA would drive a higher conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
By changing the call-to-action button to 'donate now' during this limited-time, high-urgency campaign, there was a 50% increase in donor conversion rates, with a 98.5% level of confidence.
We believe that this is due to the nature of the campaign-if people are reading the Facebook ad copy, which is asking for a donation, they are more likely to click on the CTA button to donate and then complete their donation than to learn more about what their donation could be. In fact, the treatment version had a slightly lower click-through rate (0.9% versus 1%) than the control, thus reinforcing that people were motivated to donate when they landed on the donation page.

experiments.title: Does increasing the urgency for a match campaign ad increase click throughs for Pathway to Victory?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing the urgency in ad copy for social media audiences will achieve higher clicks to the landing page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/26/2021 - 12/31/2021
Throughout the month of December, Pathway to Victory’s fundraising efforts centered around a premium offer of a 2022 Daily Devotional book, combined with their largest-ever Gospel Strong Matching Challenge of $1.5 million. The existing ad copy for the last 5 days of December focused on the last chance for donors to have their gift matched, but lacked a level of urgency. We tested a different version of copy (image creative remained the same) and headline that increased the urgency and focused on the midnight, December 31st deadline to have your gift matched.
experiments.keyLearnings: By increasing the urgency and having a stronger headline, we were able to drive more clicks to the landing page. For future high-urgency campaigns, we've now shown that social ads need to have as much urgency in the copy as possible in order to perform well.
