experiments.title: Does removing images and buttons and using plain-text on a high urgency campaign increase clicks for Pathway to Victory?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing images for email subscribers will achieve a higher click through rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/31/2021 - 12/31/2021
December 31st is usually the day when digital donors receive the most emails from nonprofits, as that is the day where the majority of them raise the most revenue. In order to cut through the ‘noise’ of December 31 emails, for Pathway to Victory we tested a plain-text email versus their control email which used graphics. The messaging remained the same on both versions; for our test however we made the email look like it came directly from Dr. Robert Jeffress. By removing all images, and removing hyperlinked text, while adding in the full URLs for the landing page, our goal was to increase the number of click throughs.
experiments.keyLearnings: By making the digital donor feel like they were being personally reached out to by Dr. Jeffress, instead of receiving a generic 'marketing' email with graphics, we drove a higher click through rate and engagement with Pathway to Victory. This hypothesis will be useful to continue to test moving forward with not just high-urgency campaigns like a December 31st email, but throughout the year.

experiments.title: How donation page copy impacted donor conversion in a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing value proposition on the need for existing members will achieve an increase in conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/13/2021 - 04/03/2021
When reviewing the donation page for a high urgency renewal campaign, we wanted to test reducing brand references and making the copy more donor-focused. Since visitors to this page were returning members, our hypothesis was they already knew or at least had some familiarity with who The Heritage Foundation is and what they do. For the treatment, we reframed the copy to show instead what the membership gift would accomplish and highlight the need, rather than emphasizing what Heritage was already doing.
experiments.keyLearnings:
One element that was removed from the treatment copy was social proof, which we know is a key factor in donor motivation. The control shares that The Heritage Foundation shares over 500,000 supporters. For future testing, we will look for opportunities to keep elements of social proof while reducing brand mentions to see if there is any notable effect. The control copy also had congruency in the headline and the final ask on the page, making it abundantly clear what the desired action on the page was while the treatment headline leaves some ambiguity. "Defend conservative change in America" - but how? There are multiple ways to do so. In future headline tests, we can draw the connection between renewing membership and defending conservative change in America so the reader is not left guessing. We'd also like to re-test this concept at a later date considering it is an annual campaign and donors may already be motivated to give during this season. Donation page visitors could be inclined to renew their membership regardless of reading through the value proposition on the page, especially if they have been a long-time donor.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 7.2% decrease with a 88% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Desktop visitors had a 7.3% decrease in donations with a 94% level of confidence.New visitors had a 9.0% decrease in donations with a 97% level of confidence.Housefile Email visitors had a 6.3% decrease in donations with a 94% level of confidence.18-24 visitors had a 45.7% increase in donations with an 89% level of confidence. (We'd like to do some extra digging into this directional lift for this younger age segment!)45-54 visitors had a 29.7% decrease in donations with a 98% level of confidence.65+ visitors had a 12.6% decrease in donations with a 93% level of confidence.

experiments.title: Will emphasizing a sale deadline in an email subject line increase email opens?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting the discount deadline for a NIO discounted ticket deadline reminder email will achieve an increase in email opens.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/16/2021
Approaching a NIO Summit discounted ticket deadline we sent out a reminder email that the discount was ending on Friday of that week. We wondered if highlighting the deadline in the subject line would create urgency and increase email opens.
experiments.keyLearnings:
There was an increase of about 9% in the open rate of our treatment email which aligns with our hypothesis. Notably, while the more urgent subject line increased the open rate of the email, the click-through rate to the ticket checkout page was about the same for both versions.
After reviewing these results, we cannot definitively say that the reason for the increase is the highlight of the deadline in the treatment subject line because it is not an isolated variable. The control subject line mentions NIO as "in-person" which we did not mention in the treatment version.
This hypothesis requires further testing with more isolated variables.

experiments.title: How adding urgency and relevancy to a subject line on an appeal impacted open rates

rows.name: A critical year for the future of freedom for Texans

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding relevancy and urgency for housefile recipients of a digital appeal will achieve an increase in open rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/08/2021 - 02/22/2021
When sending a monthly appeal, we wanted to test if adding urgency and relevancy in the subject line would influence open rates. The control subject line “This legislative session” contained an element of mystery but we developed a treatment subject line “A critical year for the future of freedom for Texans” to draw attention to the timeliness and to make a personal connection to the reader, especially if they are a Texan concerned about freedom.
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found was the subject line with additional detail reduced opens by 36.3% while the succinct, mysterious subject line outperformed the treatment with 100% level of confidence. One hypothesis is that "A critical year" indicates the recipient could take action at any time during the year versus "This legislative session" has a more finite timeframe thereby making it more relevant and timely. The element of mystery proved to drive open rates more than clarity in this specific instance. We'll use this finding to develop additional subject line tests in the future.

experiments.title: How increasing urgency affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing urgency and clarifying language increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/04/2017 - 05/08/2017
CaringBridge was running a matching gift campaign to try to meet a match from a generous donor. As they reached the end of the campaign, they wanted to increase the perceived urgency to the reader of their final email. They had seen research that visually increasing urgency by putting a countdown clock in the email would improve response, so they created a treatment that did that. But they also wanted to test another concept that put the donor in the position to be the hero and finish out the campaign. Rather than messaging that they were “just short of their goal”, they quantified that they were “just a few gifts short”, which increased the perception that the donor could actually meet the goal.
Additionally, they tried to clarify language that had tripped up donors before. With a matching gift, they had traditionally used language like “double your gift”. But they hypothesized that this language could actually imply that they wanted to the donor to give twice as much. So, they tested simpler language that said “If you’d like to give (and have our anonymous donor match it),  you can use the link below.” They clarified the language on the link itself, from “Double my gift now” to “Yes, match my gift”.
They split the email file and sent a treatment to each half to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment, with the increased urgency and clarified language, produced a 64.6% increase in donations, along with a directional 24% increase in average gift. Because there were multiple variables in the treatments, further testing must be done to isolate each variable and quantify the lift from each individual variable.

experiments.title: How urgency impacted donor conversion on The Daily Signal donation ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Will including language and a countdown clock about the approaching year end increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2016 - 01/01/2017
The Heritage Foundation had recently discovered a tactic that was able to successfully increase the donor conversion rate of readers of The Daily Signal. This website is a Heritage-owned property that receives over 2 million visitors per month so even minor improvements can lead to significant gains.
In the days leading up to December 31st, we wanted to see if we could utilize the upcoming deadline to increase the urgency of the gift. For this test, we modified the existing value proposition statement to allude to the December 31st deadline for tax-deductibility and then added a countdown clock to visually reinforce this date.
We then launched the experiment with 10 days remaining in the year.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment had two interesting observations. The first, and most important, is that we were able to lift donor conversion rate by 61% by reinforcing the urgency of the December 31st deadline. The second observation came from an analysis of the number of visitors that clicked on the initial ad.
What we found is the year end messaging resulted in a 45.6% decrease in click through rate (statistically significant). That means we were able to achieve a 61% lift in donations from roughly half the number of visitors. The key to this learning is that, while the urgency-based message may not be as appealing to a larger audience, it increases the motivation of those that do click on the link.

experiments.title: How connecting value proposition with urgency on a Facebook ad increases click through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: More value proposition will increase click through rates
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/13/2021 - 04/22/2021
FamilyLife had been running some ads for their Dates to Remember offer, and they asked us to test out some new copy. The original copy stated a potential problem “Maybe you’ve discovered” and then suggested the box as a solution. Since the offer was for dates with your spouse, in our treatment copy, we focused on the value proposition around that concept and explained why it was important. Then we discussed how the box fit that need and added a sense of urgency at the end.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment increased the click through rate by 39%, getting more traffic to the page, which is the first step in optimizing a funnel. We hypothesize this is because we gave the reader more incentive to purchase the item and gave them a reason to do it right away.
We could not sufficiently track purchases from these ads. After we fix the tracking we will continue to test around this concept. But the next iteration for now is testing more engaging creative that matches this ad copy.

experiments.title: How adding specificity and urgency affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing specificity and urgency in the call-to-action increase clickthrough rate and conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/22/2017 - 10/24/2017
Compassion International served Facebook ads to people who had browsed their site that showed them dynamically generated ads based on the children they had viewed. While results were positive for the campaign, they wanted to see if they could increase clickthrough rate and conversion rate by adding specificity to the ads.
They created a treatment that was identical to the first ad with one small change—in the call-to-action, instead of “Sponsor a child today”, they changed the copy to “Sponsor this child right now” to increase specificity and urgency.
They launched an experiment to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment produced a 233.4% lift in clickthrough rate, but resulted in fewer downstream conversions (though this data was not valid). This suggests that while the ad might increase user motivation to click, something on the following landing page is not aligning with that motivation and is causing cognitive friction in the mind of the prospective sponsor. This shows that the experiment needs more data and possibly downstream conversion testing to discover what is causing the depression in conversion.

experiments.title: How a subject line impacted email opens for members in a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a subject line utilizing mystery and personal connection for returning members will achieve an increased open rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/31/2021
For an annual membership campaign, we wanted to look at ways to optimize the email envelope for The Heritage Foundation appeals. We had a set of historically used subject lines and wanted to test using a different subject line to drive email engagement. We set up the control as “Here’s the next big challenge” and developed a treatment subject line using only the word “Reconnecting”. The sender and body copy remained the same for each version.
experiments.keyLearnings:
What we saw was the treatment produced a 30.1% increase in open rate for the member segment with 100% level of confidence. Our hypothesis was the treatment would lift open rate since the language utilizes the element of mystery for the receiver to make them ask - What does the sender want to reconnect with me about?
Beyond the email open rate, we made further observations that are noteworthy. Clickthrough rate on the treatment saw a 25.1% lift with100.0% level of confidence. Additionally, the treatment produced a 93.6% increase in donor conversion with 100.0% level of confidence.
One thing that is important to keep in mind, however, is this email was randomly split so there may be some imbalance in returning member behavior that could have led to these results on donor conversion. Before leaning too heavily or making conclusions on the lift in donor conversion, we would want to do additional analysis on the two segments to verify it was a true and fair split based on time on file and previous giving history.

experiments.title: How an expiring goal and increasing urgency affect donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How does an expiring goal and increasing urgency affect donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/27/2018
CaringBridge tried a new fundraising technique during the year-end campaign called the “Power Hour”, where they would raise money in small hourly campaigns that expired in 60 minutes. As they were running this campaign (via a sticky bar on their site), they wanted to monitor what happened to clickthrough rate and conversion rate as the number of gifts dropped. They analyzed the data with four gifts remaining and the data with two gifts remaining to try to understand how an expiring goal affects donor conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: Interestingly, there was no statistical lift in clickthrough rate—both "4 gifts left" and "2 gifts left" had a similar clickthrough rate, with only a 12% difference and 40% level of confidence. However, there was a statistically valid 56.9% increase in conversion rate once there were only two gifts left—which showed that the declining goal increased motivation and incentive. While this data is not necessarily actionable as progress towards a goal cannot be controlled, it can help direct timed communication efforts in relation to progress towards a goal. When should you send a "final appeal for help?" This experiment indicates that it may be more beneficial the fewer gifts there are left towards the goal.

experiments.title: How a softer call-to-action might increase donor conversion during high urgency campaigns on mobile devices
experiments.researchQuestion: Can we increase donor conversion with a soft CTA of signing the Declaration for Life?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/23/2019 - 05/05/2019
Focus on the Family was running a campaign geared towards their pro-life initiative. To promote the campaign and get people to give, they featured a banner at the top of their site that asked people to give towards it. In an earlier experiment, where they test the call-to-action on the banner from a hard ask to a softer CTA, they found that it had little to no impact on donor conversion. To continue to test into this idea, they hypothesized testing a different soft CTA that would engage users, present an email acquisition opportunity, and give people the option to give. For the new experiment they tested the hard ask against asking people to sign their “Declaration for Life” pledge. They split their mobile traffic and tested this approach.
experiments.keyLearnings: The soft CTA decreased donor conversion by 53.9%. And while we were able to acquire email addresses through the declaration, the decrease in donor conversion and revenue is too great of a loss. We will continue to test into this banner on mobile devices during campaigns to try and better understand the motivation of people for these campaigns. Maybe a value-add content offer would perform better than the options we have already tested and the control which is a hard ask?

experiments.title: How creating a sense of urgency in your ad copy impacted donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will creating a sense of urgency and improving clarity increase the ad clickthrough rate, email signup rate, and/or donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/27/2020
As a part of the SCOTUS 2020 confirmation hearings for (now) Justice Amy Coney Barrett, Americans for Prosperity had designed a digital activism opportunity for citizens to sign a letter and send it to their senators to tell them that they approved this nomination and were urging their elected officials to represent them by voting to confirm the nominee when it came time to officially vote.
The letter-signing page, once completed, would redirect signers to a donation page where they would be asked to make a financial gift to support the continued efforts to confirm the nominee.
In reading the control ads, they lacked a sense of urgency. For this nominee, there was a general feeling that it was a foregone conclusion. Telling people that there was still risk if they didn’t take action now was true, but would it have an impact on results?
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 100% level of confidence, we observed that this treatment increased donor conversion rate by +89.6%.
Subsequently, we observed every step of the funnel with increased results:
Ad Clickthrough Rate increased +30.9% (LoC: 100%)Email Signup Rate increased +14.3% (LoC: 100%)
This meant that so many more people made it through the funnel and onto the donation page, which increased results thereafter (in terms of donor quantities). But the conversion rate was increased in our opinion for two reasons:
As noted, the increased volume of people coming through the funnel, andThe momentum was started at the ad level and carried all of the way through to a cohesive donation page that still cast doubt on the confirmation unless they did more right now.
In terms of breaking down the ads themselves, here are some other observations that led to the massive increases in ad clickthrough rate:
We used trigger-based ads—specifically, by getting a photo of President Trump along with the nominee. This caused more people to pause in their feed, which got more people to read the ad copy (which generated motivation).Using CLARITY to tell people exactly what they can expect post-click, how their information would be kept safe, and that it would take only a minute all worked to mitigate anxiety for the reader to want to take the action today.Ensuring that we communicate that there is still doubt unless they take action right now was a motivating factor, as well

experiments.title: How visually boosting urgency affects conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Does the addition of a countdown timer increase the urgency of the appeal enough to boost the overall conversion of the page?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/19/2015 - 06/28/2015
As part of their fiscal year end campaign, Alliance Defending Freedom wanted to find a way to boost the conversion of their already high performing landing page.
The audience being driven to the landing page was already highly motivated as we discovered in a past experiment. Based upon those results, we didn’t think that adding value proposition language would boost the results so we decided to test a visual element that would increase the urgency. We created a treatment with a timer to the page that counted down to the end of the fiscal year.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In the early weeks of the campaign, the timer had no significant impact on the conversion rate of the page.  However, throughout the final week of the campaign, the timer added an increased sense of urgency that improved the page conversion by 12.6%.
As shown in the graph above, it wasn’t until this final week that the conversion rates diverged enough to reach a statistically significant lift.

experiments.title: How increasing urgency and perceived need affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will increasing urgency and perceived need affect donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/20/2018 - 01/13/2019
Texas Public Policy Foundation was running a campaign to raise support for their Center for the American Future, which defended citizens against government overreach. They were running a petition of support for John Yearwood, a rancher who faced a threat from the government if he disturbed the habitat of a rare spider—one which he had never seen!
The petition was followed by a donation ask, which was not performing very well. They decided to create a treatment that redesigned the page and message to present the need and urgency in a more forceful manner, hypothesizing that it would increase donor conversion. The treatment page added a headline that thanked the signer. They then tried to heighten the stakes by telling the reader that the stakes of this case were high, and could possibly affect them. The next paragraph of copy focused on a single fact—Mr. Yearwood can’t fund this case by himself, and hundreds of Texans are stepping up to help him fund it. Then comes the “but” statement—they are currently short of goal. They put a thermometer on the page that showed how close they were, and provided some social proof.
experiments.keyLearnings: The new donation page produced a 236.1% increase in donor conversion, which showed the importance of communicating not just the cause, but the need for funds. Additionally, social proof and urgency—in this instance, the case moving forward without the necessary funds had an effect.

experiments.title: How subject line urgency impacted email performance
experiments.researchQuestion: Which subject line will have the biggest impact on subscriber engagement (opens and clicks)?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/30/2016 - 01/13/2017
As the year end deadline approached for Illinois Policy Institute’s year end fundraising campaign, they wanted to find ways to communicate that urgency to the subscribers. For the second-to-last email send, sent out on December 30th, they decided to run a subject line test to determine which would have the biggest impact on subscriber engagement.
Both of the subject lines communicated urgency. The primary difference between the two was that one, time is running out, is more direct in its approach and is a more common approach taken by many organizations in their fundraising and marketing-style emails. The alternative, tick tock, was a bit less common and did not give away the purpose of the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We found that the more direct and “sale-y” subject line of time is running out decreased the open rate by 8%. Additionally, this lower open rate led to an incrementally lower click through rate with a 33% decrease (with 94.6% statistical validity). Our hypothesis is that with the time is running out subject line, the subscribers immediately felt marketed to since it is not a subject line most people would use in their day to day communications. As a result, they were less likely to engage.
This is a great example of the concept that people give to people. As fundraisers, we want to communicate urgency but we need to do it in such a way that it still feels genuine.

experiments.title: How urgency in the subject line impacts open and click rates
experiments.researchQuestion: Will adding urgency to the subject line increase the percentage of people the open or click on the message?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/21/2017 - 12/30/2016
World Concern sent a series of 7 fundraising emails as part of their year end campaign. They wanted to ensure each email was getting the maximum impact so they set up different treatments for each email.
For the second email in the series, we wanted to discover if adding urgency to the subject line would impact the percentage of people that either opened or clicked on the message. We decided to test a more vague subject line against one that conveyed the urgency of the December 31st deadline since that was the topic of the email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In the end, the more urgent subject line of “Don’t wait another minute” decreased the open rate of the email by 27.5%. However, we also wanted to monitor how it impacted the click through rate since the ultimate goal of this email was to get people to the donation page. When analyzed, we discovered there was not a significant difference between the percentage of people clicking on a link.
As a rule of thumb, it is a good trade off to get less opens if it means more people clicking on the link but with there being no discernible difference between click rates, we would rather have more people reading the email so they are exposed to the message when emails later in the series are sent.

experiments.title: How not mentioning we’ve met the goal in email copy during a high urgency campaign impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Would removing the copy that reached our goal motivate more people to give?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/28/2017 - 07/31/2017
Towards the end of Live Action’s money bomb, they met their goal. This was great news! They still had two emails left to send in their campaign and we questioned that donors might be less motivated to give if they knew we had already met the goal. We tested this. For the control, we mentioned that we had reached our goal but still needed their support. For the treatment, we didn’t mention we met the goal, but emphasized that time was running out for them to make their gift.
experiments.keyLearnings: When we removed the copy that mentioned we met the goal, we increased donor acquisition by 38.3%! Mentioning that we met the goal in the copy caused people to think that their gift wasn’t needed anymore. By focusing the messaging on urgency, we were able to motivate more people to give!

experiments.title: How relevancy and urgency affect open-rate

During the series of SBA List moneybomb emails, they wanted to see which subject lines would motivate more people to open their emails and in turn click-through to the donation page. In the first email send, they tested the subject line: “The upcoming Supreme Court decision” vs “The pro-life impact of the Supreme Court decision.”
experiments.keyLearnings: The control subject line “The upcoming Supreme Court decision” had a 7.5% increase in opens. This subject line may have created a bigger sense of relevancy and urgency than the subject line “The pro-life impact of the upcoming Supreme Court decision”. While people were initially more motivated to open the control resulting in a better open rate, the treatment had a better click-through. This can be attributed to the subject line of the treatment being more reinforced by the overall message of the email copy. For the resend of this email, they sent the treatment because of the higher click-through rate.

experiments.title: How urgency affects clickthrough rate in website popups
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing clarity of the call-to-action increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/03/2016 - 05/16/2016
Run with Water was raising entry fees before their race event. They used a ‘welcome mat’ pop-up to let visitors know and encourage them to register for the race. However, they hypothesized that time-sensitive language that highlighted the user’s benefit might increase urgency and increase race registrations instead of the cause-based call-to-action. So they created a treatment that highlighted the deadline and presented the benefit to the user, rather than cause-based copy.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment with urgency-based language delivered a 24.8% increase in clickthrough rate. This shows that while cause-based copy might inspire some people (like existing donors or constituents familiar with the cause), clarity trumps persuasion. While we must continually test, research shows that plainly stating the benefits can often increase conversion in place of inspirational copy.

experiments.title: How urgency and clarity affect open rate

rows.name: It's a challenge to be a Christ-follower

experiments.researchQuestion: Which subject line would increase open-rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/10/2016 - 05/11/2016
Each month, Jews for Jesus sends out an eAppeal. The messaging for these eAppeals comes directly from their mail appeal for the month. When they converted the direct mail letter into an eAppeal this month, there seemed to be two different value propositions in the letter. One value proposition focused on how it is becoming more challenging to be a Christ-follower in America today, and the other focused on a specific outreach event happening in Israel.  With having two different value propositions and versions of emails, they tested two different subject lines to see which version would receive more opens.
Then, they split-tested this with their email file, dividing it in half.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment had an increase in open rate of 5.8%.  People responded more to the urgency and clarity of the treatment than the vagueness of the control.
Additional tests of this email continued to prove that the value proposition for the Israel outreach event was more motivating to the audience to click-through and ultimately give to. You can find those tests here:
Click-through – What we learned about reinforcing the value proposition to help increase click-through.
Donor conversion – How two different value propositions for an eAppeal affected donor conversion.

Live Action was running a high-urgency campaign and wanted to test a “dear reader” treatment on Live Action News by prompting readers to donate at the end of each article on the site with urgent copy surrounding the deadline and including value proposition copy around the topic of the campaign- promoting a culture of life.
experiments.keyLearnings: The high-urgency copy saw a 459% increase in donations, which supports the hypothesis that people are more likely to donate when there is a specific urgent goal and deadline in place. In addition, the copy surrounding the campaign supported the main value proposition of Live Action News- which is to promote a pro-life voice in the midst of mainstream media that does not promote that voice. This created a perfect combination of urgency and value that deeply resonated with the donors.

Live Action was running a high urgency email campaign and wanted to test whether adding a countdown clock to the email appeal would increase the sense of immediacy attached to the goal. All other copy in the email remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: While adding the countdown clock produced a 27% decrease in clicks, it saw a 30% increase in donations. Although the donations test did not validate, we can still gather that the urgency of the countdown clock affected people’s motivation to give.

experiments.title: Does a high urgency campaign with a direct ask and goal drive more results than general Christmas gift messaging?
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a high urgency campaign with a direct ask and goal drive more results than general Christmas gift messaging?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2018 - 12/20/2018
World Concern’s Global Gift Guide email strategy has traditionally focused upon messaging donors leading up to Christmas in a very transactional way.
As a part of the 2018 strategy, to test into creating a “High Urgency Campaign” that pulled out 4 of the most popular gifts (with the highest appeal and organizational mission alignment) and created a fundraising goal, with challenge messaging to challenge the donor to help us deliver specific gifts by a deadline.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The high urgency campaign messaging treatment essentially doubled the donor conversion rate (+99.4% increase) when compared to the transactional gift messages that had been traditionally used leading up to Christmas giving deadlines.
Typically, revenue lift experiments require far more than 75 transactions to validate, but we did also achieve a statistical significance in increasing revenue for the campaign through donations received through the treatment vs. the control. With a 96.5% level of confidence, we also saw an increase in the revenue per visitor statistic by 71.3%.
We would recommend further testing (and gathering far more than 75 transactions) to have full confidence with the revenue lift aspect of this type of experiment, but when combining both revenue lift potential with twice as many gifts, leveraging high urgency campaigns over transactional messages within the confines of a holiday gift catalog program appears to drive a dramatic improvement.

experiments.title: How count-based urgency and percentage-based urgency affect conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: How will count-based urgency affect revenue, as opposed to percentage-based urgency?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/10/2018 - 01/02/2019
CaringBridge had been running sticky bar challenges throughout their year-end campaign, and had found a “power hour” method that created recurring urgency by setting hourly goals and inspiring donors to help reach them. As they neared the end of the campaign, they had a $25,000 matching gift they wanted to leverage to generate revenue, but decided to test the “donor-based” goal (with a number of donations displayed) against the traditional “goal-based” thermometer, which showed a percentage of the $25k gift remaining to be matched.
Their hypothesis was that while thermometers have been tested to convey urgency, they might make the goal too abstract or “out of reach” for one single donor. They didn’t want to go about “business as usual” without testing it.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The countdown clock prevailed as the winner, with the thermometer reducing clicks by 19.4%. Looking deeper into the data, the conversion rates downstream were actually the same—24%. However, the reduction in traffic for the thermometer meant that fewer total people made it into that funnel, which meant fewer gifts and less revenue.
This experiment has implications for any campaign—while fundraisers are traditionally percentage-focused as they are thinking about revenue goals which may or may not be visible to the prospect, donors might respond better to a "count-based" goal where their individual gift, no matter how large, can have a significant impact.

experiments.title: How increasing urgency in email copy affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increased urgency increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/01/2016 - 05/02/2016
Harvest Ministries was sending a mid-month update on their fundraising appeal for the month. The offer was a book by Joni Earackson Tada. The control email highly incentivized the click by asking the reader to “find out why” the sender (Greg Laurie) admires Joni, the author of the book. While this might drive a lot of clicks, we’ve seen this approach drive much lower conversion rates, as those who click through are not as motivated to accept the offer.
Our first experiment showed that increased clarity, in this case, decreased clickthrough rate. We devised a new treatment that leaned into the transactional nature of this email and maximized urgency to get their own copy of the book before Mother’s Day.
We split the file and ran a test to see which email won.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment produced a 28.8% decrease in clickthrough rate, which showed that the “tease” copy in the control still provided a greater incentive to click. However, the treatment email produced a 32.6% higher conversion rate on the page, which made revenue almost even between the two treatments. This prompts further testing to find an email that incentivizes the click while providing motivation to complete the donation.

experiments.title: How urgency and a repositioned call-to-action increased donations by 89.4%
experiments.researchQuestion: Will highlighting the urgency of a deadline increase donor conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/09/2015 - 12/10/2015
Harvest Ministries prepared an appeal for their year end campaign that offered a book, “The Biggest Story”, as a premium. They had prepared an email that told a personal story, made a financial ask with a call-to-action button, and then followed up with some information about the premium. On the landing page, there was a callout box that indicated that time was running out to order the premium and receive it before Christmas.
Their team formed a hypothesis that if they moved this callout earlier in the donation funnel, that they would attract visitors with a higher motivation. They developed a treatment that added urgent copy that emphasized the impending deadline, and moved the call-to-action button after this copy.  They then ran an A/B split test to see the results.
experiments.keyLearnings: This tells us that when we have a deadline, we must message it as early in the funnel as possible to make sure the incentive (why you should give now) reaches donors who might be a motivation to give. There are always people who intend to give, but think it can wait. By giving them clarity (and not false urgency) on when the incentive expires, we were able to convert more of these people.

experiments.title: How adding urgency to a repositioned call to action affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will highlighting the urgency of a deadline increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/08/2015 - 12/09/2015
Harvest Ministries prepared an appeal for their year end campaign that offered a book, “The Biggest Story”, as a premium. They had prepared an email that told a personal story, made a financial ask with a call-to-action button, and then followed up with some information about the premium. On the landing page, there was a callout box that indicated that time was running out to order the premium and receive it before Christmas.
Their team formed a hypothesis that if they moved this callout earlier in the donation funnel, that they would incentivize more readers of the email to click through to the page. They developed a treatment that added urgent copy that emphasized the impending deadline, and moved the call-to-action button after this copy.  They then ran an A/B split test to see the results.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This tells us that highlighting urgency on the landing page isn’t enough — we must move it earlier in the decision process if we want to motivate and incentivize the maximum amount of prospects. Because email performance rates decline naturally at each step (from delivery to open rate to clickthrough rate), we can reach more prospects with this message as we move it further down the inverted funnel. However, that doesn’t mean that we should move it to the subject line — when improperly placed, urgency can deceive and disincentivize prospects.

This also gives a valuable lesson about call-to-action placement. In the first email, the button was placed before the premium, which meant that many prospects made the decision before they were fully informed and incentivized. By moving the button to the end of the email (and strengthening the incentive that comes before it), Harvest was able to nearly double clickthrough rate.

The premium was featured on the landing page, but with no information about the deadline to receive it by Christmas. They wanted to test the impact of adding in language with the deadline in a special callout box.

experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment widget actually delivered a 1% increase on the follow-on donation page, but attracted 20% fewer clicks to start the donation process, which resulted in a sharp decrease in revenue. This suggests that personalization alone doesn’t increase overall revenue and provoked another slate of tests.

experiments.title: How natural urgency affects the clickthrough rate on a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Will emphasizing the natural urgency of a limited-time offer increase clickthrough rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/01/2017 - 04/21/2017
After successfully launching their most popular online course, “Constitution-101,” Hillsdale College decided to offer a limited-time only DVD Set version. This was designed for those who enjoyed the course but also wanted to physically own and share it with others. They used this product launch to test whether the natural urgency of the limited-time offer affected the targeted audience’s decision to take action. Using the emotive appeal “Bring Constitution 101 to your living room” as a control, they created a treatment with more direct, urgent language.
Keeping all other elements the same, they launched this A/B test to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment group that emphasized the natural urgency of the limited-time offer produced a 17.6% increase over the control group. This experiment validated Hillsdale College’s hypothesis that their target audience is more inclined to take action when there is an apparent sense of urgency. This is a valuable discovery about the motivation triggers present in the mind of potential donors—one that Hillsdale College can use across other courses.
Whether we realize it or not, most of our daily actions are driven by factors of urgency. Our digital behavior is no exception. That knowledge is powerful. However, it does not permit organizations to manipulate audiences by creating a false sense of urgency. These attempts are easily sniffed out and will only harm the reputation of the organization. But using natural urgency to alert an audience, as we have seen here, can be an effective way to motivate action.

experiments.title: How urgency impacted donor conversion on The Daily Signal donation ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Will including language and a countdown clock about the approaching year end increase donor conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/21/2016 - 01/01/2017
The Heritage Foundation had recently discovered a tactic that was able to successfully increase the donor conversion rate of readers of The Daily Signal. This website is a Heritage-owned property that receives over 2 million visitors per month so even minor improvements can lead to significant gains.
In the days leading up to December 31st, we wanted to see if we could utilize the upcoming deadline to increase the urgency of the gift. For this test, we modified the existing value proposition statement to allude to the December 31st deadline for tax-deductibility and then added a countdown clock to visually reinforce this date.
We then launched the experiment with 10 days remaining in the year.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment had two interesting observations. The first, and most important, is that we were able to lift donor conversion rate by 61% by reinforcing the urgency of the December 31st deadline. The second observation came from an analysis of the number of visitors that clicked on the initial ad.
What we found is the year end messaging resulted in a 45.6% decrease in click through rate (statistically significant). That means we were able to achieve a 61% lift in donations from roughly half the number of visitors. The key to this learning is that, while the urgency-based message may not be as appealing to a larger audience, it increases the motivation of those that do click on the link.

experiments.title: How urgency impacts email acquisition rate on a Facebook ad
experiments.researchQuestion: Will a Facebook ad that stresses the urgency of a course launch increase email acquisition?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/11/2017 - 10/16/2017
Dallas Theological Seminary was making preparations to launch its new ‘mini’ course on the life and work of Martin Luther. They wondered: If we tell visitors to pre-enroll in this course by stressing the urgency of the upcoming launch date, will email acquisition increase? They launched an A/B test leading up to their launch date to find out.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Dallas Theological Seminary found that the treatment version that stressed the upcoming launch date did, in fact, increase email acquisition by 88%. By using the actual date of the launch (October 16, 2017) and the word ‘Monday’ in their call-to-action, Dallas Theological Seminary was able to signal to viewers that this was an urgent offer with a specific launch date. It likely made viewers feel that they were going to miss out on a particular event, even though the course was still offered after the launch date.
This strategy has prompted further tests, especially as Dallas Theological Seminary builds plans to launch new courses.

experiments.title: How adding specificity and urgency affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing specificity and urgency in the call-to-action increase clickthrough rate and conversion rate?

Compassion International served Facebook ads to people who had browsed their site that showed them dynamically generated ads based on the children they had viewed. While results were positive for the campaign, they wanted to see if they could increase clickthrough rate and conversion rate by adding specificity to the ads.
They created a treatment that was identical to the first ad with one small change—in the call-to-action, instead of “Sponsor a child today”, they changed the copy to “Sponsor this child right now” to increase specificity and urgency.
They launched an experiment to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment produced a 233.4% lift in clickthrough rate, but resulted in fewer downstream conversions (though this data was not valid). This suggests that while the ad might increase user motivation to click, something on the following landing page is not aligning with that motivation and is causing cognitive friction in the mind of the prospective sponsor. This shows that the experiment needs more data and possibly downstream conversion testing to discover what is causing the depression in conversion.

experiments.title: How visually increasing urgency affects landing page conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: Which combination of elements creates the greatest sense of urgency that allows for the increase in conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/24/2014 - 12/28/2014
CaringBridge was in the final week of their 2014 year-end fundraising campaign and expected traffic to dramatically increase to their donation pages. They wanted to maximize on-page conversion by adding elements to the page to increase urgency: reminding people of the proximity to December 31st.
We knew that adding elements like a countdown clock and a thermometer would increase urgency, but we tested to see which element (or combination of elements) would maximize landing page conversion.
experiments.keyLearnings: The countdown clock was able to increase the urgency of the ask which, in turn, lead to the increase in conversion rate. However, when combined with the thermometer element of the campaign, it negatively affected the conversion rate. This may be due to the large goal seeming out of reach for the average donor. Whatever the case, for large campaigns like this, the countdown clock will be deployed in the future to increase conversion rate.

experiments.title: How personalization and urgency affects clickthrough rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does increased personalization and clarity in the value proposition lead to more clicks and more gifts?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/01/2015 - 02/17/2015
CaringBridge ran a successful test in their Holiday campaign that showed a significant lift in donor conversion and revenue by adding personalization to their donor tribute form, where the majority of their gifts come in. They wanted to recreate this test outside of the year-end campaign to ensure that it provided a similar lift when it didn’t have the built-end urgency of that campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment widget actually delivered a 1% increase on the follow-on donation page, but attracted 20% fewer clicks to start the donation process, which resulted in a sharp decrease in revenue. This suggests that personalization alone doesn’t increase overall revenue and provoked another slate of tests.

experiments.title: How increasing urgency affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Will increasing urgency and clarifying language increase conversion?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/04/2017 - 05/08/2017
CaringBridge was running a matching gift campaign to try to meet a match from a generous donor. As they reached the end of the campaign, they wanted to increase the perceived urgency to the reader of their final email. They had seen research that visually increasing urgency by putting a countdown clock in the email would improve response, so they created a treatment that did that. But they also wanted to test another concept that put the donor in the position to be the hero and finish out the campaign. Rather than messaging that they were “just short of their goal”, they quantified that they were “just a few gifts short”, which increased the perception that the donor could actually meet the goal.
Additionally, they tried to clarify language that had tripped up donors before. With a matching gift, they had traditionally used language like “double your gift”. But they hypothesized that this language could actually imply that they wanted to the donor to give twice as much. So, they tested simpler language that said “If you’d like to give (and have our anonymous donor match it),  you can use the link below.” They clarified the language on the link itself, from “Double my gift now” to “Yes, match my gift”.
They split the email file and sent a treatment to each half to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings: The treatment, with the increased urgency and clarified language, produced a 64.6% increase in donations, along with a directional 24% increase in average gift. Because there were multiple variables in the treatments, further testing must be done to isolate each variable and quantify the lift from each individual variable.

experiments.title: How visually increasing urgency in an email affects response rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Adding an animated countdown clock to the email will increase the urgency in the mind of the donor which will increase the response rate of the campaign.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/30/2014 - 12/31/2014
December 31st is the biggest giving day of the year for many nonprofits. As the end of the year approaches, organizations often look for ways to increase urgency to inspire additional giving. DTS added some animation to their emails as a way to visually remind supporters of the approaching deadline.
experiments.keyLearnings: Since the countdown clock increased both conversion rate and average gift, this tactic will be used in future campaigns that contain an inherit deadline like calendar year end or fiscal year end. Interestingly, the countdown clock in the email did not actually increase the number of clicks to the landing page but significantly increase the number of donors that subsequently gave a gift.

experiments.title: How a dynamic goal thermometer on a high urgency donation page affects revenue and conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that replacing a single, static $500,000 campaign thermometer with a dynamic thermometer that resets in $5,000 increments will achieve an increase in revenue and conversion rates because smaller, frequently achievable goals make progress feel more attainable, reinforce donor impact, and create a stronger sense of momentum and urgency during the year-end giving period.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/18/2025
This experiment took place during The Heritage Foundation’s annual calendar year-end fundraising campaign, one of their most important giving periods of the year. The campaign had a total goal of $500,000, and the primary donation experience included a progress thermometer meant to signal momentum and urgency to donors as they considered giving.
Because year-end traffic is high and donors are especially motivated by deadlines and progress cues, this was an ideal moment to test whether an alternative framing of the goal and progress indicator could increase motivation and perceived impact.
The test was run directly on the campaign donation page.
Control: The standard donation page displayed a traditional progress thermometer showing the total amount raised toward the fixed $500,000 campaign goal.
Treatment: The donation page used a dynamic thermometer that “reset” once a smaller goal was reached, increasing by $5,000 increments each time the goal was met. This created the perception of frequent wins and ongoing momentum rather than a single, distant finish line.
Both versions were otherwise consistent, ensuring the thermometer behavior was the primary variable being tested.
The motivation for this experiment came from a behavioral insight: large, distant goals can feel abstract and discouraging, especially late in a campaign when progress may slow. In contrast, smaller, rapidly achievable goals can increase donor confidence, urgency, and the sense that their gift meaningfully contributes to immediate success.
We wanted to test whether breaking a large year-end goal into visible, recurring milestones would:
Make progress feel more attainable
Increase perceived donor impact
Sustain momentum throughout the campaign rather than only near the final deadline
This experiment was designed to evaluate whether a dynamic, milestone-based progress indicator could outperform a traditional static thermometer during a high-stakes year-end fundraising push.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The most significant takeaway from this experiment was that the dynamic, milestone-based thermometer meaningfully increased revenue, even without a corresponding increase in transactions. The treatment generated a 30% lift in revenue, with an 89.7% level of confidence, indicating a strong and practically meaningful impact on total dollars raised.
At the same time, transactions decreased by 10.8%, though this result came with only 48% confidence, suggesting the decline is not statistically reliable and may be due to natural variance rather than a true negative effect. Taken together, these results indicate that the revenue lift was driven primarily by higher average gift amounts, not by more donors completing gifts.
This suggests that the dynamic thermometer helped donors feel more confident making larger contributions by reinforcing momentum and the sense that their gift could meaningfully “complete” a near-term goal.
Moving forward, this experiment reinforces the value of breaking large fundraising goals into visible, attainable milestones, especially during high-pressure moments like year-end campaigns. Rather than relying solely on a single, long-term progress bar, future donation experiences can leverage smaller, frequently achieved goals to sustain momentum and encourage generosity.
Specifically, we will:
Continue testing dynamic or segmented goal framing in high-volume fundraising momentsPair milestone-based thermometers with strategic ask array testing to better understand how goal framing influences gift sizeBe cautious about interpreting transaction volume changes when confidence is low, focusing instead on revenue-per-visitor and average gift as primary success metrics
Overall, this test demonstrates that optimizing how progress is framed can materially impact fundraising outcomes—even when surface-level conversion metrics appear flat or mixed.

experiments.title: Can democracy-focused messaging outperform urgency-based copy on a donation page during EOY?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believed that using EOY-reframed, democracy- and community-focused messaging for WUNC donors would increase donor motivation and conversion compared to crisis-focused copy because values-based framing can strengthen intrinsic motivation and reinforce donors’ identity as supporters of public media.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/20/2025 - 11/25/2025
WUNC is a public media organization serving North Carolina with a mission to inform, engage, and strengthen civic life, supported largely by listener contributions. As the organization entered the 2025 end-of-year (EOY) fundraising period, sustaining revenue growth and donor motivation remained key program goals.
This experiment tested donation page copy during a high-traffic EOY moment to understand whether reframing the ask around democracy and community impact could outperform WUNC’s existing urgency-based messaging tied to federal funding cuts. The test was prioritized as part of a broader effort to evolve messaging beyond crisis framing and assess when values-based appeals resonate most with donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings
Big takeaway: The urgency-based control outperformed the democracy-focused treatment on the primary KPI (donations). Secondary metrics show a similar directional pattern, though only one reaches near-significant confidence.
📊 Performance metrics (with confidence comparisons)
Total donations (primary KPI)Control: 25 / 126 (19.8%)Treatment: 13 / 111 (11.7%)Difference: +8.1 pts favoring controlConfidence level: ~92%❌ Not statistically valid at 95%, but directionally meaningfulOne-time donationsControl: 19 / 126 (15.1%)Treatment: 8 / 111 (7.2%)Difference: +7.9 pts favoring controlConfidence level: ~95%⚠️ Borderline statistical validity; strongest secondary signal in favor of urgency messagingSustaining donationsControl: 6 / 126 (4.8%)Treatment: 5 / 111 (4.5%)Difference: +0.3 ptsConfidence level: <10%❌ No meaningful or directional differenceRevenueControl: $4,100Treatment: $880Revenue strongly favored the control, driven by higher conversion volume.⚠️ No statistical confidence calculated, as revenue is not suitable for a two-proportion z-test in this context.
✂️ Segment interpretation
The performance gap was driven primarily by one-time donor conversion, not sustaining behavior.Sustainer response was effectively neutral between variants.
Next Steps
Continue running with the current urgency-based messaging for EOY. Additional messaging tests are planned to determine when donor responsiveness begins shifting toward values-based framing. Timing is a key variable under consideration, as this test ran immediately before Giving Tuesday, when urgency messaging historically performs better.

experiments.title: How a goal thermometer on a high-urgency campaign donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of a goal thermometer for FYE donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it creates a sense of social proof by visibly showing real-time progress toward the goal and highlighting that others are actively contributing, we foster a sense of community and shared purpose.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2025 - 06/30/2025
During Moody Radio’s Fiscal Year-End Campaign, we tested the impact of adding a goal thermometer on donor conversion.
The goal of this test was to leverage social proof by emphasizing a shared, communal objective to meet a specific financial need for the ministry, ultimately driving an increase in both transactions and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are:
The addition of a goal thermometer for FYE donation page visitors did not lead to an increase in donor conversion metrics. In fact, there was an 8.0% decrease in transactions for all traffic, indicating that the control group performed better in terms of transactions.
 	Despite the decrease in transactions, there was a significant increase in revenue by 489% in the treatment group with a 100% level of confidence. This suggests that although fewer people may have donated in the treatment group, those who did contribute gave more on average.
 	The statistical level of confidence for the decrease in transactions is 82.7%, below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for valid results. Therefore, these results should not be considered reliable or used for future decision-making.

experiments.title: How including a gift array in a high urgency campaign overlay affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a gift array on the pop up for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it allows the user to begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/26/2025 - 03/12/2025
The Navigators are running a pop-up on their website as a part of their February Sustainer campaign. They wanted to test how including a gift array on the pop up would affect donor conversion rates.
They believed that more people would choose to give once getting to the donation page because the user has already begun the process of giving by selecting how much to give.
This test ran for the entirety of the campaign and was shown to 50% of the traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a gift array on the pop-up for homepage visitors led to a significant increase in donations. The treatment group saw a 65.9% higher donation rate compared to the control group. Despite the positive outcome, the statistical confidence level of 81.9% is below the standard threshold of 95%, indicating that the results may not be entirely reliable.

Furthermore, the treatment saw a 104.1% lift in revenue at a 67.7% LOC. While neither of these metrics validated, they do suggest that The Navigators audience responded positively to this addition.

It is also important to note that the gift array buttons preselected the donation page with the amount selected on the overlay. Therefore, taking one step out of the process once the user landed on the donation page which may have been perceived as less work and friction.

Moving forward, it is essential to continue testing the effectiveness of the gift array on donation conversion rates, ensuring a robust and reliable methodology to draw conclusive insights. It would be beneficial to conduct further experiments with larger sample sizes and different variations of the gift array to validate the initial findings. Additionally, considering factors such as donor preferences and user experience could help optimize the gift array strategy for better results in future campaigns.

experiments.title: How updating the value prop on a donation page with urgency and relevancy impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding urgency and relevancy to the value proposition for Hoover’s audience will achieve an increase in the instant donation coversion because there will be more urgency and relevancy making the donor feel more connected to Hoover’s mission.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/27/2025 - 02/14/2025
This experiment was conducted for the Hoover Institution, a public policy think tank dedicated to promoting ideas for a free society.
Our hypothesis was that if we update the instant donation form copy to be more related to an immediate need, then the urgency will cause an increase in the instant donation conversion. If we update the value proposition with urgency and relevancy to the times it will encourage more donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that updating the instant donation form copy to include urgency and relevancy to a current need resulted in a significant increase in instant donation conversions for the Hoover Institution. The treatment group experienced a 42.9% increase in transactions compared to the control group, with a statistical confidence level of 87.7%.

Why the Test May Have Won:
Stronger Immediate Relevance & Urgency: 
 	The test opens with, "Before you go, we’d like to ask you one more thing," which feels more immediate and action-oriented than the control’s "While you wait for it to arrive."
 	It emphasizes the present need for scholars like Thomas Sowell to continue their work, connecting the ask to an ongoing, timely cause.
 	Clearer Value Proposition:
 	The test provides concrete examples of what donations will support: "Examine the causes of current crises," "Enable our team of expert scholars," and "Advance a strategic communications program."
 	The control remains more abstract, focusing broadly on "data-driven research" and "an educated citizenry."
 	More Compelling Call to Action:
 	The test includes a clear call to action: "Will you consider making a tax-deductible gift to the Hoover Institution today?" followed by bullet points that break down the impact of a gift.
 	The control’s call to action feels less direct and more passive with phrases like, "Would you partner with us?"
 	Focus on Impact Over Philosophy:
 	The test copy shifts the focus toward what Hoover does and the tangible outcomes of its work.
 	The control talks more about philosophical beliefs, like "the importance of an educated citizenry" and "the American way of life."

experiments.title: How does sending voicemails during a high-urgency campaign affect donor response?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding voicemails to a multichannel high-urgency campaign for active donors will achieve a higher average gift because of the added personal touch points, relationship cultivation, and sense of ownership that a donor will feel.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/22/2024 - 12/31/2024
Human Coalition is interested in expanding their multichannel approach to fundraising, and decided to test the effectiveness of sending voicemails to a portion of their active donor list during a high urgency campaign.
The control group received the standard campaign emails and Facebook ads.
The treatment group received the same communication as the control group along with a series of 4 voicemails that were delivered through Slybroadcast over a period of 4 weeks. The content of these voicemails mirrored the content of emails in the campaign and were most often sent from the same person.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding voicemails to a multichannel high-urgency campaign for active donors can lead to a significant increase in revenue. The treatment group, which received voicemails in addition to the standard campaign emails and Facebook ads, saw a 60.9% increase in revenue compared to the control group. Further analysis of the data showed that the treatment group also increased the number of donations by 37% at a 98% level of confidence.
This result suggests that the personal touch points, relationship cultivation, and sense of ownership that voicemails provide can have a positive impact on donor engagement and giving behavior. While the statistical level of confidence is below 95% for increasing revenue, the strong results warrant further testing of this strategy.
In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing the effectiveness of adding voicemails to multichannel fundraising campaigns, perhaps with variations in the timing, frequency, or content of the voicemails. Additionally, collecting qualitative feedback from donors who received the voicemails could provide insights into why they may have been more effective in driving donations. Overall, this experiment highlights the potential of using voicemails as a valuable tool in donor communication and fundraising efforts.

experiments.title: How a goal thermometer on a high-urgency donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the presence of the revenue goal thermometer  for The Navigators CYE Donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it signals to visitors that others are also donating therefore boosting trust on the page. because  it signals to visitors that others are also donating therefore boosting trust on the page.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2025
To improve donor conversion rates during The Navigators’ year-end campaign, we conducted a test by adding a goal thermometer to the donation page.
The experiment ran from December 27 to December 31, 2025, with 50% of site traffic exposed to the treatment.
Our hypothesis was that the thermometer would positively influence donor conversion by serving as social proof, signaling that others are contributing and encouraging more people to donate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the addition of a goal thermometer to the donation page did not have the intended effect of increasing donor conversion rates for The Navigators' year-end campaign. In fact, there was a 5.1% decrease in donations for all traffic during the experiment period.
Despite these results, the statistical level of confidence was only 81.0%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for making valid conclusions. As a result, these results should not be considered conclusive or used for future decision making but they do suggest that The Navigator's audience had a neutral reaction to the goal thermometer.
Moving forward, it is important to understand that not all experiments will yield the expected results, and it is essential to carefully evaluate the data and statistical significance before drawing conclusions. In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to conduct further tests with different variations or factors to determine what truly influences donor conversion rates for The Navigators. Additionally, considering factors such as timing, audience segments, and messaging may also provide valuable insights for optimizing donation campaigns in the future.

experiments.title: How exclusivity language on a high-urgency pop-up impact donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including exclusivity language for users on the RTB website will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it highlights the donor’s identification with a group, beyond just being a donor..
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/03/2024
For RTB’s first-ever Giving Day, we hypothesized that the inclusion of exclusivity language — highlighting that the donor is joining a unique or exclusive group of donors to the first RTB Giving Day — will improve conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including exclusivity language on the RTB website did lead to a significant increase in donor conversion rate. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 68.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for valid results.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is large enough to produce statistically significant results. Additionally, it would be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period of time to gather more data and increase the confidence level of the results.

While the initial findings are promising and suggest that exclusivity language may indeed impact donor conversion rate positively, we should not make any definitive conclusions or implement permanent changes based on these results alone. Further testing with a larger sample size and longer duration is needed to validate the impact of exclusivity language on donor conversion rate.

experiments.title: How focusing on urgency in the final days of a high urgency campaign affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing on urgency in the final days of a high urgency campaign for potential donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because users will be reminded of the deadline and the importance of giving before then.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/27/2023 - 12/31/2023
The IMB was running their annual Lottie Moon Christmas Offering campaign. On the campaign donation page, they decided to test their standard value proposition which told the story of Lottie Moon against a page that increased urgency to give before the deadline of December 31st. This test ran on the final four emails of the campaign which led up to the deadline.
They split the traffic to determine a winner.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that focusing on urgency in the final days of a high urgency campaign may not necessarily lead to a higher donor conversion rate. In fact, the treatment group, which received the urgency-focused messaging, experienced a decrease of 19% in transactions compared to the control group.

Based on these results, it is important to consider that urgency itself may not be a significant motivating factor for potential donors. Other factors such as the emotional appeal of the story or the credibility of the nonprofit may have a stronger influence on donor behavior.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further explore the impact of urgency messaging on donor conversion rates. This could involve testing different variations of urgency messaging, such as emphasizing specific deadlines or highlighting the impact of giving before a certain date. Additionally, it could be valuable to investigate the potential interaction between urgency and other elements of the value proposition, such as emotional storytelling.

By conducting further testing and refining the messaging strategy, the nonprofit can gain a clearer understanding of what truly drives donor conversion and optimize their campaigns accordingly.

experiments.title: Will adding urgency to an in-line banner increase donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a countdown timer on the Wikipedia banner will increase donor conversion because the countdown to the deadline would increase urgency.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/02/2024
As the campaign deadline draws near, we believe adding a countdown to the Wikipedia banner may insight more donor participation by visually showing time is running out and the time to act — time to save America’s families and make an impact — was coming to a close. Would adding a countdown timer increase donor perception that they have a deadline to make a donation and make a difference?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a countdown timer to the Wikipedia banner may increase donor conversion by creating a sense of urgency. The treatment group saw a 23.9% increase in donations for all traffic, indicating that the countdown timer potentially motivated more donors to take action before the deadline. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 81.0%, falling short of the typically accepted threshold of 95%.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the potential impact of introducing a countdown timer or similar elements that create a sense of urgency. While this experiment provided promising results, the validity is not strong enough to definitively conclude that the countdown timer was the sole reason for the increase in donations. Therefore, further testing with a larger sample size or different variations of the countdown timer may be necessary to draw more reliable conclusions. Additionally, it would be beneficial to explore other strategies to increase donor conversion and engagement to complement the potential effectiveness of a countdown timer.

experiments.title: How using the concept of autonomy and donor volition impacts giving during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that focusing on autonomous motivation for email recipients will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because we focused less on urgency/pressure and more on value .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/09/2024
In an effort to increase donor conversion rate, we wanted shift the messaging of a campaign email to be less urgency/pressure focused and more autonomy driven – giving the donor the choice to give/not give. This is based on autonomous (volitional) motivation – donors give because they personally understand and value the cause; it’s their way to enact and share their values. This test was a complete copy overhaul and was tested across 2 of the last emails of a high urgency campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that focusing on autonomous motivation for email recipients led to a significant decrease in donations compared to the urgency/pressure focused messaging; a directional 46% decrease in donor conversion rate with a LoC of 93%. This indicates that the shift in messaging strategy did not resonate as effectively with donors as intended.

Additionally we experienced a valid 16% decrease in clicks, indicating that the messaging did not resonate enough with recipients to even click through the donation page.

Given that these are the last 2 emails of a campaign, where urgency is what typically drives action, it may be that RTB's audience is primed and expectant of this type of messaging as they approach the deadline.

experiments.title: Does a countdown clock increase urgency and donor conversion for a year end campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a timer on the sticky bar for the fye campaign will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because having a visible timeline of when the campaign ends draws more urgency than a donor countdown.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/18/2024
For Moody’s FYE campaign, we wanted to see if we could increase donor conversion rate by replacing the donor countdown sticky bar with a different tactic to see if we could find out what their audience responds best to.
For this test we wanted to see if a timer would increase urgency more than a donor countdown
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment, which involved replacing the donor countdown sticky bar with a timer for the FYE campaign, resulted in a significant increase in donations for all traffic.
