The 38.3% increase in donations is a promising result, indicating that the timer may have indeed created a higher sense of urgency among donors.

However, the statistical level of confidence of 94.1% falls slightly short of the commonly accepted threshold of 95% for results to be considered valid. This suggests that while the results are promising, further testing is needed to confirm that the timer is indeed more effective at increasing donor conversion rates compared to the donor countdown.

It's also important to note that while this did not validate with transactions. However, there was a $11,285 increase in revenue when a countdown timer was present.

experiments.title: How reducing the length of copy and increasing urgency impacted revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by shortening copy and increasing urgency for email subscribers will achieve a higher conversion rate and revenue because the ask is stronger and less copy to digest.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/05/2024
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Baptist Dallas.
During June and December, PTV conducts matching challenges in order to increase donor conversion.
For the next to last email during their June match campaign we decided to test shortening the email and increasing urgency to see if we could get more donations and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that shortening the email copy and increasing urgency can lead to a significant increase in revenue. The treatment group saw a 38.6% increase in revenue for all traffic, which is a promising result.

In future experimentation, we should continue to test the effectiveness of shorter copy and increased urgency in driving conversions. Further testing with larger sample sizes and different variations of the email content could help confirm the initial findings and determine the best approach for maximizing donations and revenue during matching challenges. Additionally, we should strive to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence to ensure the validity of the results before making any significant changes to our email strategy.

experiments.title: How does focusing on an urgent need rather than a premium in the final email of a campaign impact donor conversion?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of urgent language instead of an incentive for potential donors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because  the sense of urgency and a deadline will act as a motivating factor to give.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/04/2024
For the final email of The Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign, we decided to test urgent language in the email vs the incentive of a premium. For the majority of this campaign, we have focused on the incentive of a book premium. However, for the final email our hypothesis was that by the final email if they have not converted to a monthly donor by this point, then the premium is not incentive enough for them to give.  Therefore, the urgent language may be motivating due to the presence of a deadline.
The control included the premium and the treatment included the urgent language.
This test ran as a 50/50 A/B test to The Navigators housefile audience in an effort to activate more recurring donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that urgent language instead of a premium may be effective in motivating The Navigators audience to give in the final hours of an appeal. The treatment email saw a 47.2% lift in transactions at an 80.8% level of confidence. While this test did not validate, the results are compelling in that, urgent language resonated well with their audience.
While the clicks did not validate for the treatment in this test, it does suggest that those who were compelled to click the link were more motivated to give once they landed on the donation page.
One thing that is important to note is the presence of the premium throughout previous emails. The lack of the premium in the treatment may have been a deterrent to give with the email because they could have received the premium in previous emails.
In future experimentation, it may be worth including the premium and have the addition of the urgent language in the treatment rather than removing the premium all together from the treatment email.

experiments.title: How increasing urgency in a sticky bar impacted conversion rates during year-end
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a visible countdown deadline for the homepage sticky bar will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because it will increase the motivation to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2023 - 12/31/2023
For this experiment, we wanted to see if we could increase donor conversion rate by adding a sense of urgency. To do this we added a countdown timer to a sticky bar to give a deadline.
experiments.keyLearnings: The addition of a visible countdown deadline to the homepage sticky bar resulted in a 21.8% increase in donations for all traffic.

experiments.title: How does focusing on an urgent need rather than a premium in the final email of a campaign impact clicks?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of urgent language instead of an incentive for potential donors will achieve an increase in clicks because  the sense of urgency and a deadline will act as a motivating factor to give.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 03/04/2024
For the final email of The Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign, we decided to test urgent language in the email vs the incentive of a premium. For the majority of this campaign, we have focused on the incentive of a book premium. However, for the final email our hypothesis was that by the final email if they have not converted to a monthly donor by this point, then the premium is not incentive enough for them to give.  Therefore, the urgent language may be motivating due to the presence of a deadline.
The control included the premium and the treatment included the urgent language.
This test ran as a 50/50 A/B test to The Navigators housefile audience in an effort to activate more recurring donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the urgent language in the final email of The Navigators February Sustainers Appeal campaign did not result in an increase in clicks from potential donors. In fact, the treatment group saw a decrease in clicks compared to the control group, with a drop of 13.3% in clicks at a 81.0% level of confidence. While these results did not validate, they do suggest that the urgent language was not a motivation factor for The Navigators audience to click the link, and that they potentially respond better to the presence of an incentive or premium.
In future experimentation, it is important to carefully analyze the impact of urgent language and deadlines on donor behavior. It is possible that in this instance, potential donors may have already been motivated by the premium incentive in previous emails and adding urgent language only served to create confusion or negatively impact their decision-making process.
Moving forward, it may be beneficial to continue testing different incentives and messaging strategies to see what resonates best with potential donors. Additionally, ensuring that experiments are properly powered and have a high level of statistical confidence will help to draw more reliable conclusions for future decision making.

experiments.title: Does running brand ads impact CYE donations on Facebook

rows.name: PTV DA Daily Devotional 2024 CYE 2023 - treatment

experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by running brand ads for Facebook traffic will achieve a higher conversion rate because there will be brand awareness and knowledge of who PTV is.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2023 - 12/31/2023
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
December is always a key month for nonprofit fundraising. PTV is no different. Every December, PTV offers a Daily Devotional for the next year as well as a matching challenge.
For this year, on Facebook, we decided to run brand ads to see if this would increase the number of donations.
For the set up, we did 2 campaigns in Facebook.
The control ran Dec 1-31 with ads promoting the Daily Devotional and matching challenge.
The treatment ran Dec 1-15 with brand ads (no ask) just ads about PTV optimized for reach. Then from Dec 16-31 this campaign ran the same Daily Devotional and matching challenge ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. **Brand awareness can positively impact conversion rates:** The treatment group, which focused on brand ads rather than direct ask ads, saw a 16.1% increase in Purchases for All Traffic. This indicates that building brand awareness through ads can lead to higher conversion rates.

2. **Variations in different metrics:** Clicks experienced a significant decrease, while Complete Registrations saw a substantial increase during the treatment phase. Cost per Click, Cost per Registration, and Cost per Purchase also showed notable changes. Additionally, there were decreases in purchases across different visitor types (65+, Mobile, Tablet, Desktop, Female, and Male visitors).

3. **Statistical confidence levels:** While the changes in metrics were significant, the statistical confidence levels were not high enough (85% or above) to consider the results valid for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider the following:

1. **Consider longer test durations:** Running experiments for a longer duration may help in achieving higher statistical confidence levels, providing more reliable results for decision-making.

2. **Diversify test approaches:** While brand awareness seems to have a positive impact on conversion rates, it is essential to continue testing different strategies to find the most effective approach for fundraising campaigns.

3. **Segmentation analysis:** Understanding the impact of different visitor types on conversion rates can help tailor campaigns to specific audiences, potentially increasing overall effectiveness.

Overall, while the experiment showed promising results in terms of the impact of brand ads on conversion rates, it is crucial to conduct further analysis and experiments with higher statistical confidence levels before making significant decisions based on these findings.

experiments.title: Does running brand ads impact CYE donations on Facebook
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by running brand ads for Facebook traffic will achieve a higher conversion rate because there will be brand awareness and knowledge of who PTV is.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/16/2023 - 12/31/2023
Pathway to Victory is the broadcast ministry of First Dallas.
December is always a key month for nonprofit fundraising. PTV is no different. Every December, PTV offers a Daily Devotional for the next year as well as a matching challenge.
For this year, on Facebook, we decided to run brand ads to see if this would increase the number of donations.
For the set up, we did 2 campaigns in Facebook.
The control ran Dec 1-31 with ads promoting the Daily Devotional and matching challenge.
The treatment ran Dec 1-15 with brand ads (no ask) just ads about PTV optimized for reach. Then from Dec 16-31 this campaign ran the same Daily Devotional and matching challenge ads.
Results are based on December 16-31 as this is the timeframe that would show the fruits of the brand ads.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that running brand ads on Facebook can potentially lead to a higher conversion rate in terms of donations for a nonprofit like Pathway to Victory. The treatment group, which was exposed to brand ads before the Daily Devotional and matching challenge ads, saw a 16.1% increase in donations compared to the control group.

However, the statistical level of confidence was only 81.0%, falling below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be relied upon for future decision making.

In future experimentation, we will test running brand ads alongside offer ads rather than only brand at the beginning.

experiments.title: How posing a lower, $10 ask for non donors impacts email engagement and giving during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that offering a specific, lower ask amount for non donors will achieve an increase in click-through rates and ultimately gifts because having a specific, low amount can reduce decision friction and potential anxiety.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/08/2023 - 12/22/2023
During calendar year end, we wanted to activate more nondonors on RTB’s file. In order to lower the threshold for those who have never given, we posed a $10 ask as a test. The control received no specific amount while the treatment received a $10 ask in their email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that offering a specific, lower ask amount of $10 to non-donors can result in an increase in click-through rates compared to not mentioning a specific amount. The treatment version of the email showed a 20.5% increase in clicks for all traffic with a level of confidence of 87%.

Additionally, only two gifts were generated from this email both of which came from the treatment and  both at an amount of $25 which is higher than the initial proposed amount.

It's suggested that this test is ran again with the $10 ask as the starting point of the gift array that the treatment group receives. That way, there's further congruency in the ask and can potentially activate more donors.

experiments.title: How does addressing someone directly and asking them a personal question affect click-through rate for a high urgency email?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that starting an email with a personal question for potential donors will achieve a higher click-through rate because it keeps the user engaged and helps them feel more relatable to the situation.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/12/2023
The Navigators launched their calendar year campaign with an email that started with a direct statement about how Christians face problems more now than ever before. We decided to test how opening an email with a personalized question to draw them in would affect click-through rate to the donation page.
In previous testing, we’ve seen a more personalized tone perform better because it allows the user to relate to the problem at hand and causes them to engage with the email longer.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that starting an email with a personal question for potential donors can have a positive impact on click-through rates. The treatment group, which received the personalized question at the beginning of the email, saw a 20.3% increase in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control group with an 87.2% level of confidence.

Because of these results, we believe that opening an email with a personalized question rather than a direct statement will keep users engaged with the email causing them to be more likely to click the link to donate.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue testing variations of personalized tones and messaging to optimize click-through rates. Additionally, it would be important to analyze other metrics such as conversions and donations to understand the full impact of the personalized approach. By continuously testing and iterating, we can refine our email strategies to effectively engage potential donors and drive meaningful action.

experiments.title: How a more generalized ask in a sticky bar for Giving Tuesday impacted click-through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that framing the message in terms of a broader impact will resonate with more people and lead to a higher click-through rate because it is more emotionally engaging.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/27/2023 - 11/28/2023
On Giving Tuesday, Dallas Theological Seminary made a concerted effort to acquire more monthly donors, called Cornerstone Partners. They’ve typically taken an approach that outlines the number of new Cornerstone Partners needed before the midnight deadline.
This year, they wanted to see if a more general ask would lead to higher click-through rates because the goal number could be perceived as overwhelming for someone reading.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment version of the message, which framed the ask in terms of a broader impact, did not lead to a higher click-through rate compared to the control version. In fact, the treatment version saw a significant decrease in clicks by 61.1% with a 93.1% level of confidence.
These results suggest that the hypothesis was incorrect and that framing the message in terms of a broader impact may not resonate with more people or be more emotionally engaging. Therefore, it may not be an effective strategy for increasing click-through rates and acquiring more monthly donors.
In future experimentation, it would be necessary to further test different messaging strategies to determine what resonates most with the target audience. It is important to take into account the specific characteristics and motivations of the donors to understand what messaging would be most compelling to them.
Additionally, it would be advisable to conduct experiments with larger sample sizes and run the experiment for a longer period to increase the statistical validity of the results. This would help ensure that the findings are more reliable and generalizable.
Overall, this experiment highlights the importance of testing different messaging approaches and continuously refining strategies to optimize donor engagement and acquisition.

experiments.title: How testing gift images in a dear reader helps influence gifts during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding images of the thank you gifts for potential donors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates because people want to see the gifts they will be getting as a thank-you for their donation..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2023 - 06/04/2023
WUNC is North Carolina’s largest NPR station. During their spring pledge drive, we wanted to run a 3-way test to see whether promoting their premium thank-you gifts using a dear reader style placement would help influence more donations.
We’ve tested dear reader asks in the past, with little lift in direct donations. So this test was designed to see whether having the images, versus not (and no-dear reader at all) would help influence overall online donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did not see a lot of direct donations that can be attributed to the dear readers, we did see a slight lift approaching a valid level of confidence by using the dear reader overall, with a higher lift in donor conversion rates through using the images of potential thank you gifts.

Further testing of this concept would test the placement of the dear reader, as well as strengthening the language and making the connection between those reading the news articles and the 'why' they need to donate to support the work of WUNC.

experiments.title: How personalizing a sticky bar with an on-air personality impacted donations during a high-urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that personalizing a sticky bar with an on-air personality for pledge donors will achieve a change in donor conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2023 - 05/04/2023
WUNC is North Carolina’s largest NPR station. Since a lot of web traffic during pledge drives is driven by offline sources (mainly radio) we wanted to see whether personalizing a sticky bar with a quote/image from a specific on-air personality, who would be on the air during a specific time and day, would make an impact on overall online donations.
We ran this as a 3-way test, with a control, sticky bar with quote but no image, and sticky bar with quote and image of on-air personality.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment aimed to test whether personalizing a sticky bar with a specific on-air personality would impact overall online donations for WUNC, a nonprofit NPR station. The treatment showed a 13.4% increase in donations for all traffic, with an 87.6% statistical level of confidence, indicating that personalizing the sticky bar had a positive effect on donor conversion rates. However, the results are not entirely valid and require further testing to ensure the intended impact.
While results varied from day-to-day, the overall lift that adding a sticky bar to the homepage during the pledge drive was impactful, with an 87% level of confidence that adding one would help increase overall donations.
Interestingly enough, while the image treatment version had a slightly lower conversion rate vis-a-vis the impact on overall online donations, when digging into the directly-attributed results, the treatment version had 6 gifts compared to the no image version of 3 gifts.
Further testing is definitely warranted, as is trying to quantify the connection between offline (radio) and online traffic.

experiments.title: How implementing a goal thermometer on a high urgency campaign donation page impacted donations- attempt #2
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that implementing a goal thermometer for donation page visitors amidst a high urgency campaign will achieve an increase in donations because page visitors will be motivated to help contribute to and reach the end goal of the campaign.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/17/2023 - 05/01/2023
As part of a March high urgency campaign for the Illinois Policy Institute, we want to test the impact of adding a goal thermometer on the campaign donation page. The goal is to reach a $50,000 matching gift. The thermometer syncs with GA to track up-to-date campaign revenue, so donation page visitors will see how much money has been raised towards the campaign goal at the time they’re visiting the page.
We’re curious to see what kind of impact the visible progress and end goal seen through the thermometer will impacted donations.
This is our second try at this test. We extended the deadline of the campaign and will re-run this test.
The initial test resulted in a 7.8% increase with a 34.8% level of confidence to the treatment and 9% increase in revenue with a 65.4% level of confidence.
Let’s see how our second test performs.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the implementation of a goal thermometer for donation page visitors did not result in an increase in donations. In fact, the treatment group saw a significant 46.6% decrease in donations compared to the control group with a nearly valid 83.5% level of confidence.
The treatment went in the wrong direction compared to going in the right direction the first time we ran this experiment. That said, each experiment did produce an increase in revenue through the treatment.
1st test: 9% increase in revenue with a 65.4% level of confidence.
2nd  test: 16.7% increase in revenue with a 56.4% level of confidence.
Oddly, mobile donations through the treatment increased by 58.7% in the first test but saw a 77.5% decrease the second test. The second test did have a significantly smaller sample size, however.
It would be wise to test this again for a future high urgency campaign. An additional hypothesis would be that a more attainable goal would help produce an increase in donations. The goal was not even halfway reached for this campaign, which may have spoiled donor motivation because they felt their gift would not make a big enough dent towards accomplishing the giving goal. Revenue went up in both experiments, so it'd be interesting to see if it would go up the next time this experiment is run as well.

experiments.title: How a lower ask amount affects revenue on a Wikipedia-style article appeal during a high-urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a lower recurring donation ask on a Wikipedia-style in-article appeal banner will achieve greater revenue by offering a lower commitment, thus increasing donations enough to offset the lower ask amount.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/16/2023 - 03/24/2023
During a high-urgency campaign focused on recurring donor acquisition, we wanted to know if lowering the ask amount on a Wikipedia-style in-article appeal would increase recurring donor rates by offering a lower commitment given that donors would be giving on a monthly basis.
This test followed on the heels of a previous experiment (during the same campaign), where we learned that the Wikipedia-style appeal tactic outperformed both a banner ad and a slide-in appeal when presented on article pages being viewed by prospective donors.
We set this up as an A/B test with roughly 50% of traffic seeing the $30 ask and roughly 50% of traffic seeing the $15 ask in the hopes that donor volume would outweigh the lower ask and provide a net increase in revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 5 days, we were unable to validate our hypothesis that a lower ask amount would lift donations enough to overcome the lower ask and produce a net increase in revenue over an assumed 11-month period.
The treatment was only able to produce one additional recurring gift and the 48% decrease in revenue (81.4% level of confidence) did not justify continuing to run this experiment. Based on these results, we elected to run the $30 ask for the remainder of the campaign since it appeared that the decreased ask amount did little to increase the volume of donors while cutting overall revenue nearly in half.

experiments.title: How adding value proposition to a high urgency campaign website sticky bar impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding value proposition to sticky bar copy promoting a key fundraising opportunity for website visitors will achieve an increase in donations because the value proposition will appeal more to potential donors and motivate them to learn more and make a gift through the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/27/2023 - 03/31/2023
The Illinois Policy Institute was running a high urgency campaign through the end of March. To alert website visitors and get more people to the campaign donation page, we’re testing two sticky bars: One that is very brief and simply alerts users of an urgent matching gift and campaign deadline, and the other includes more value proposition copy, appealing to issues that the user likely cares about and telling them how they can learn more about a way to help – while also pointing out the urgency of a deadline.
Which angle will produce more donations?
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding value proposition to the sticky bar copy can lead to a significant increase in donations. The treatment version that included more value proposition copy saw a 304.6% increase in donations, with a statistical level of confidence of 94.4%.

All donations completed in this experiment were completed by desktop visitors and returning visitors. It is clear that the treatment sticky bar (with value prop) spoke more effectively to visitors through appeal and motivation. A "matching gift" does not mean as much to our audience as improving certain issues that they are passionate about.

Future sticky bars run for high urgency campaigns should include value proposition language, as that's what our audience wants to hear. It may also be worth testing variations of the treatment version to further optimize the messaging and achieve an even greater increase in donations.

experiments.title: How increasing urgency by including a dynamic donor countdown to a banner ad affects donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a dynamic donor countdown to the emergency banner for potential donors will achieve increased clicks to the donation page because people respond to an urgent message.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/25/2021 - 05/08/2022
If we added a dynamic donor countdown to the emergency banner, donor conversion will increase because donors respond to urgent messaging. We used an emergency alert banner vs. an emergency banner with a countdown as our variable treatment.
Note – Dynamically counts down from 25. After a gift, the number will decrease to confirm with donors that the counter is valid.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Test stopped due to lack of significant results.
• Unique banner click rate was higher for the variant (5%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (65%).
• Donor conversion was higher for the variant (5%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (65%).
• Revenue per visitor was lower for the variant (-5%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (65%).
• Recommendation is not to add the dynamic counter to the emergency banner.

experiments.title: How adding a micro-goal slider that stresses urgency to a sponsorship page affects conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a micro-goal slider to the sponsorship page that stresses urgency for potential donors will achieve increase sponsorship conversion  because there is a clear CTA. because  there is a clear CTA.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/20/2022 - 03/28/2022
Adding a micro-goal slider to the sponsorship page that stresses urgency will increase sponsorship conversion because there is a clear CTA. When a user enters the Child List Sponsorship page in the variant, a popup will appear after 3 seconds on the page. It presents a variable number of lifeline children that need to be sponsored. The popup scrolls with the page and can be closed by the user.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Desktop donor conversion was higher for the variant (28%) at a statistically valid confidence level (99%).
• Recommendation is to add the slider to all sponsorship-related pages.
• Overall, the variant produced an increase (12%) at a non-statistically valid confidence level (92%).
• RPV was higher for the variant (13%) at a non-statistically valid confidence level (84%).
• Desktop RPV was higher for the variant (34%) at a non-statistically valid confidence level (95%).
• Mobile donor conversion was slightly higher for the variant (2%) at a non-statistically valid confidence level (16%).
• Mobile RPV was slightly lower for the variant (-6%) at a non-statistically valid confidence level (66%).
• Based on 2021 figures (assuming fixed traffic and average gift), we project an incremental lift of $76,000 annually. The net 60-month value of these donors is approximately $1.4M.
• Given the statistical significance in desktop donor conversion (28%) and limited directional downside on mobile, we recommend adding the slider to all sponsorship pages.
• Results may have had an even greater significance if not for the Ukraine emergency. The experiment was close to validating five days in, but highly motivated traffic related to Ukraine evened out the treatments.
• Additionally, we look to test different versions of copy and visual elements employed by the slider to optimize the experience further.

experiments.title: How adding impact language to a direct-ask pop up banner during a high urgency campaign affects revenue – marriages saved V2 relaunch
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the language on the gift array of a direct ask pop-up to show the impact of the gift will result in greater revenue
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2022 - 12/11/2022
Focus on the Family (FOTF) is a global Christian ministry dedicated to helping families thrive. They provide help and resources for couples to build healthy marriages that reflect God’s design and for parents to raise their children according to morals and values grounded in biblical principles. Every year during the calendar year-end high-urgency campaign, FOTF places a top-of-page pop-up banner that contains a direct ask to support their mission. This banner (known internally as the “wiki-banner”) has been tested into over many years, with experiments to improve donation metrics. Since many people visit FOTF.com during this season to make a year-end gift, the “wiki-banner” serves as a low-friction tactic to get people to the donation page quickly. It is important to note that the visiting audience to FOTF’s website is served different content / experiences based on pages they have consumed in the past…specifically a “marriage audience” and an “in favor of pro-life audience – Santicity of Human Life” (also known as SOHL).
For this specific experiment targeting the “marriage audience”, we were focused on changing the language on the gift array buttons to reflect the impact that a gift would make on the amount of marriages saved. The theory was that if we could directly show how many marriages could be saved with a specific gift amount, the visitor would be moved to give a bigger gift to make a greater impact. As screen real-estate is at a premium for this “wiki-banner”, we designed an experience that showed the number of marriages saved on a button on the wiki-banner, and in a “hover-state” it would flip to the equivalent dollar amount of the gift. We even added a free-form field that would allow you to add how many marriages you wanted to save, and it would calculate that amount in real-time. After making a selection, the user is directed to the donation form where the amount they chose is auto-populated on the giving form, to reduce friction. It is also important to note that we were testing for revenue lift on desktop only (not mobile due to screen size limitations).
NOTE: This is the second version of the experiment (V2 relaunch) due to technical challenges of the V1 version. That write-up can be found here.
experiments.keyLearnings:
If you notice, this experiment is labeled V2 relaunch. We launched this one AFTER the V1 version that we ended on 12/2 due to internal FOTF technical challenges. This version ran from 12/2 to 12/11. While this experiment was set up correctly, it was also running in parallel with a similar experiment that was targeted to only the SOHL audience. Unfortunately, challenges with the data capture for that experiment (linked here) were resulting in no returns. The design of the experiment was to run in parallel with the SOHL version, so that we could see the effect on both audiences seperately. We made the decision to end this experiment early on 12/11, so that we could determine what needed to be fixed on the SOHL version, so that we could relaunch both experiments at the same time, and accurately record data on both at the same time.

All of that said, we were seeing similar results (although not as drastic) to the V1 experiment before we ended this experiment. Revunue lift for V2 was around 55% with an 86% Level of Confidence. Even when we removed outlier donations over $2k from both the control and the treatment, we still saw a significant lift from the treatment.

As with the V1 version of this experiment, we did not feel comfortable relying on these results to make any future recommendations other than to re-build the experiment as it was, and quickly re-launch (a second time) to see if the results continued to show a positive lift in revenue through the remainder of the year-end campaign. Results from V3 will be shown in a future write-up and linked here.

experiments.title: How adding impact language to a direct-ask pop up banner during a high urgency campaign affects revenue – marriages saved V3 second relaunch
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the language on the gift array of a direct ask pop-up to show the impact of the gift will result in greater revenue
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/11/2022 - 12/31/2022
Focus on the Family (FOTF) is a global Christian ministry dedicated to helping families thrive. They provide help and resources for couples to build healthy marriages that reflect God’s design and for parents to raise their children according to morals and values grounded in biblical principles. Every year during the calendar year-end high-urgency campaign, FOTF places a top-of-page pop-up banner that contains a direct ask to support their mission. This banner (known internally as the “wiki-banner”) has been tested into over many years, with experiments to improve donation metrics. Since many people visit FOTF.com during this season to make a year-end gift, the “wiki-banner” serves as a low-friction tactic to get people to the donation page quickly. It is important to note that the visiting audience to FOTF’s website is served different content / experiences based on pages they have consumed in the past…specifically a “marriage audience” and an “in favor of pro-life audience – Santicity of Human Life” (also known as SOHL).
For this specific experiment targeting the “marriage audience”, we were focused on changing the language on the gift array buttons to reflect the impact that a gift would make on the amount of marriages saved. The theory was that if we could directly show how many marriages could be saved with a specific gift amount, the visitor would be moved to give a bigger gift to make a greater impact. As screen real-estate is at a premium for this “wiki-banner”, we designed an experience that showed the number of marriages saved on a button on the wiki-banner, and in a “hover-state” it would flip to the equivalent dollar amount of the gift. We even added a free-form field that would allow you to add how many marriages you wanted to save, and it would calculate that amount in real-time.  After making a selection, the user is directed to the donation form where the amount they chose is auto-populated on the giving form, to reduce friction. It is also important to note that we were testing for revenue lift on desktop only (not mobile due to screen size limitations).
NOTE: This is the third version of the experiment (V3 relaunch) due to technical challenges of the V2 version. That write-up can be found here.
experiments.keyLearnings:
If you notice, this experiment is labeled V3 relaunch. We launched this one AFTER the V2 version that we ended on 12/11 due to internal FOTF technical challenges. This version ran from 12/11 to 12/31. This experiment was running in parallel with a similar experiment that was targeted to only the SOHL audience. Unlike the previous 2 versions of this experiment, the results showed a decrease in the revenue by around 9% with an 82% Level of Confidence.
While previous versions of this experiment showed a positive lift (even validating in the V1 version), this one showed a decrease, but not until the final days of the experiment. As we experienced so many technical challenges with launching this experiment, we kept a watchful eye on the results on a daily basis. From 12/11 through 12/29, the treatment was actually leading in revenue….but on the last 2 days of the year, the opposite actually happened.
This led us to even more questions like...
Does the timing of the experiment change the results? Did donors in “non-peak” times get inspired to give more based on messaging?Were the donors in the last 2 days motivated to give regardless of messaging?Was the change in messaging more of a friction point in the last 2 days of the year?Would it be better to change messaging in the wiki based on the urgency and timing of the campaign? (Ex., show more impactful language earlier in the campaign, then make the most urgent times as simple and frictionless as possible)
There were several key take-aways that came from this series of experiments.
Timing/motivation may play a big role in the effectiveness of impact languageMake sure you are always monitoring your experiments for data anomalies, and don't be afraid to end an experiment early and re-launch if the data does not "look right"We cannot say definitively that the impact language led to great revenue during the majority of the high-urgency campaign...but we can say that there is enough data to show a positive trend and learnings that we will take into our next high-urgency campaign experiment

experiments.title: How presenting a daily goal in an email appeal affects donations during a year-end fundraising campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a daily goal in the body of an email appeal will increase donation rates by increasing the specificity of the ask and giving recipients a clear ‘micro’ goal that they can help achieve.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2022 - 12/27/2022
For this experiment, we wanted to test how presenting a daily goal (“today I’m looking for X donors”) in the body copy of an email appeal would affect donation rates for a public policy organization during a year-end fundraising campaign.
For this experiment, both previous donors and non-donors were included in the sample universe with ~50% of appeal recipients receiving the control (containing an ask that did not include a daily goal) and ~50% of appeal recipients receiving the treatment (containing an ask centered around a daily goal. Nothing else about the appeal was changed.
Major donors were excluded from this test and our experiment spanned seven appeal emails.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment over the course of seven emails during the organization's year-end fundraising campaign, we observed that the treatment produced a 12.2% decrease in donations (84% level of confidence).
The sample size of this experiment did not allow us to validate these results and therefore prevents us from drawing statistically valid conclusions about how presenting a daily goal in the body of an appeal email affects donation rates.
However, the results of this experiment do suggest that including a daily goal was not a motivating factor for recipients of this appeal and may even have demotivated them from giving. We also observed that the daily goal did not serve as a significant motivating factor in terms of clicking through to the donation page from the email, producing only a 2.1% lift with a 53.6% level of statistical confidence.
This may be because a daily goal does not add tangible value or a reason to give to the appeal. It is also in essence an organizational-focused goal that lacks a specific outcome that the daily goal will help achieve.
Based on these results, this public policy client may want to pursue a different method for increasing engagement and donations on the next high-urgency campaign or consider including a specific outcome that the daily goal will achieve. In either case, the organization should not assume that including a daily goal, as structured in this experiment, will increase donation rates during a high-urgency campaign.

experiments.title: How an exit intent impacted donor conversion during year-end
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding an exit intent for web visitors will achieve higher donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/03/2023
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry with a mission to pierce the darkness of the world’s culture with the light of God’s truth.
For previous high-urgency campaigns, we have tested exit intent popups but have not been able to see a valid response. We decided to test this again with the calendar year-end match campaign.
Previous tests, https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/4829 and https://app.winstonknows.com/experiment/4417
For Giving Tuesday through December 31st, PTV offered a matching campaign of up to $525k for any donation made.
The exit intent ran on the entire website for anyone that visited a site to make a donation but did not complete the donation.
experiments.keyLearnings:
For this experiment, we were very close to achieving a valid level of confidence. We saw a 4.8% increase in donor conversions with a level of confidence of 92.9%.
Compared to the 2 other times we have run this experiment, this one achieved the highest level of validation for donor conversions.
Due to these results we will move forward with rolling out the exit intent popup during high urgency campaigns and begin testing into copy, CTA and strategy within the popup itsself.

experiments.title: How shortening the value proposition copy on a high urgency donation page affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that condensing the value proposition for potential donors on a monthly appeal page will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/21/2022 - 10/01/2022
The Boy Scouts of America ran a monthly appeal campaign in September that consisted of one email and one follow-up to that email. The first email only received two donations therefore we needed to test changing the language on the donation page to see if this would increase donor conversion rate.
We felt like the donation page copy on the original page was too long to read therefore causing friction when someone landed on this page to potentially convert.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment page saw a 43.7% decrease in donor conversion rate with a near-valid level of significance of 84.9%.
After sending the follow-up appeal that had more urgency within the copy and was shortened compared to the first email, we believed that the issue actually laid within the email content of the first email, not the original donation page. Therefore, by removing a lot of the value proposition from the treatment donation page, users coming from the second email did not have enough value given to them at this step in the funnel in order to convince them to make a donation.
Next, we would like to test keeping the email copy shorter that way we're more likely to get users to the donation page where they can be presented with more value proposition.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Returning  visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with an 87% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How humanizing a sticky bar impacts donor conversion rate during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that humanizing a sticky bar for site visitors  will achieve a high donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/04/2022 - 05/21/2022
The Navigators were running a high urgency campaign for Eagle Lake camps. In order to drive more traffic to the campaign’s donation page, we wanted to implement a site-wide sticky bar. Their team member, Abby Anderson, would be the sender of these emails so we wanted to user her likeness (image and signature) to humanize the sticky bar message. People would be hearing from her from both emails and the site which would create consistency, familiarity, and would would make site visitors feel like they’re hearing from an actual person. Our hypothesis is that the humanized sticky bar would result in an increase in donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a 50% directional decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 89%. Though this experiment did not validate, we have sufficient data to hypothesize that The Navigators' audience are not motivated to give by seeing a humanized sticky bar. It's also difficult to discern what percentage of site visitors were actually receiving the campaign emails. That means that particular group of people would not have been with familiar with Abby as an email sender. For future tests, it would be beneficial to retest this concept but use the picture of a student who needs to be sponsored for Eagle Lake Camps. Giving the prospective donor a visual of who they would actually be helping (or someone like this student), would humanize the content further without needing to know a specific Navigators staff member.

experiments.title: How a Personal Introduction at the Beginning of an Appeal to Donors Impacts Donor Conversion During a High Urgency Campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a personal introduction at the beginning of an appeal will increase donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/26/2022 - 04/26/2022
KCBI is a leading Christian radio station and ministry in Dallas, Texas. For their spring fundraising campaign, they wanted to see if beginning an appeal with a personal story and introduction by the sender would increase donor conversion. When testing with another organization, we found that beginning an appeal like this increased engagement by humanizing the copy and building rapport. We know from research that donors give to people, not organizations, so we wanted to see if this would increase conversion during an urgent campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: Unfortunately, we had a small group of people open the email, so we could not achieve statistical reliability. We will keep testing into more conversational and personalized intro copy to see if we can validate this approach with their audience.

experiments.title: How a yes/no pop up with value proposition language impacts donor conversion during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a value prop based pop up  for site visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate during calendar year end.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/27/2021 - 01/04/2022
During calendar year end, Avila wanted to try an exit intent pop ups in lieu of a homepage takeover for the last few days of the campaign. There was concern that constituents who come to their site for content, may not want to contribute to a campaign. So we decided to test the language in the yes/no exit intent pop up. The control asked the site visitor if they wanted to donate to Avila to support their on-going efforts. While the treatment would have value proposition based language, asking the site visitor if they want to bring spiritual guidance to those looking for truth.
Site to their page was organic and driven by the blog content and resources they continuously provide to their constituents.
experiments.keyLearnings: The results indicate that Avila's site visitors were more likely to give to their calendar year end campaign when presented with a yes/no pop up that identified Avila's key value proposition. Without having any ask attached to the copy, that audience segment produced a 3.1% conversion rate resulting in a 125% increase in donor conversion rate with level of confidence of 94.8%. This tells us that Avila's audience responds to their core mission and cause over just being asked for a donation. It is recommended that this concept is retested outside of calendar year end in order to determine if this is also true in a different context.

experiments.title: How increasing urgency by presenting a micro goal on a child sponsorship funnel impacts conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing urgency and a need for people consider child sponsorship  will achieve an increase in conversion (sponsorships/donations).
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/22/2022 - 03/28/2022
Save the Children give people the opportunity to sponsor a child and provide for their needs. There is a lot of choices that a prospective donor has to make when they land on the child sponsorship page. From deciding if they want to sponsor a child, to deciding what child they need to sponsor, it can be overwhelming to a user. And while there is urgency in a need to sponsor a child, that message isn’t adequately communicated on the page and in the funnel.
To test into this and see if we could increase the urgency within the funnel, we created a slide in banner that would appear after 3 seconds on the page. This banner/pop-up would present a variable number of children (a micro-goal) that needed to be sponsored “by midnight tonight”. The popup scrolls with the page and has the ability to be closed by the user. We showed this variation to 50% of site users and ran the experiment for five weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall, we increase child sponsorships with the micro-goal urgency banner by 12% at a 90% level of confidence. Additionally, the greatest impact on conversion was for desktop devices. Desktop devices saw a 27.5% increase in conversion with a 97% level of confidence. There was no positive or negative impact on mobile devices.
The largest lift was related to Lifeline Sponsorship purchases, where the treatment saw a 151% lift over the control (46 vs 18). This was to be expected, as engaging with the slider added a Lifeline child to the cart, but was confirmation that if we guide donors to this product, they will respond positively.
There was no statistical significance between the treatments by channel, location, or new vs returning visitors.
What all of this tells us is that by presenting this micro-goal to site visitors (and telling them the deadline to meet this goal was midnight tonight), increase the urgency of the need which increased the motivation of the user. We know urgency drives conversion, but to see this urgency used during a season where urgency isn't naturally present is good to know that we can create urgency in our funnels and that donors are, in fact, highly driven by a need with a goal and deadline.
Next steps: While we hypothesize the increase in conversion was caused by the urgency that the micro-goal slide in banner created, the slide-in banner also was a totally different and new experience from the control. To isolate some of the variables used in this experiment and better understand and optimize this experience, continued testing of these different elements is the logical next step. Specifically, the copy being used, the functionality of the slide-in of placing a child "in the cart" vs taking users to a learn more page, the visual elements of the slide-in, etc.

experiments.title: How adding urgency to an instant donation page impacted conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding urgency  for instant donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/08/2022 - 03/17/2022
We were seeing an overall decline in donor acquisition response and became curious if the “standard” page messaging we had been using for video series offers had been in rotation too long and needed a refresh. When we reviewed the copy, we realized that if returning donors had already signed up for a separate video series, they would have already seen this messaging and had already been told why they should give…however, the main thing that was lacking was the answer to the question: why should I give right now? We built a treatment keeping this component in mind that we wanted to create a sense of urgency and clearly outline the problem that this video series is designed to address. We incorporated language explaining why “Praying Scripture over your Children” is especially needed and important in today’s culture and emphasized we must not wait until it’s too late! We weaved in elements like Scripture and donor alignment and shortened the copy from 5 important things to 3 important things.
experiments.keyLearnings:
We ran the treatment for a month and saw some valuable results. Overall there was a 29.9% lift in donations with 80% confidence so we did not reach statistical significance. If we drill down into DESKTOP traffic, we did validate a 226.5% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence for this page, but note that it is only based on 22 total transactions.  Similarly, returning visitors had a 482.3% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence. This is a huge learning for our other video series donation pages as this matches up with our theory that the old donation page copy may have been stale and glazed over by returning visitors who have read similar messaging copy on multiple video series over the past year.
--Moving forward, we will work this sense of urgency into our copy as it specifically relates to the video series topic at hand as it seems to be a huge motivator for our traffic that is already familiar with the ministry.
Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 38.9% increase with a 93% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Tablet visitors had a 5.2% increase in donations with a 98% level of confidence.Referral visitors had a 677.8% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.(Other) visitors had a 7.0% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.Male visitors had a 151.8% increase in donations with an 88% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How adding clarity to sticky bar messaging impacted donations for a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity and seasonally relevant messaging for website visitors will achieve more calendar year-end donations.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/30/2021 - 01/04/2022
During a calendar year-end campaign, we wanted to test various messaging on a sticky bar to see what would most resonate with donors to get them to the donation page to make a gift. We had a general control message that prompted a gift to ‘share resources with believers’ and mentioned the upcoming deadline. The first treatment added clarity around what the donor’s gift would accomplish – highlighting that each dollar would impact 10 people. The second treatment posed a question to introduce donor autonomy – will you help us? The third treatment was the same message, but appeared at the bottom of the screen rather than the top of the page. We split traffic to all 4 versions for site visitors during the last week of the year.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did not reach statistical significance since the experiment was time-bound and only ran for a short period of time, what we did find is that the language that clearly outlined the impact of each dollar given seemed to be moving more donors to conversion. This will need to be tested again outside of a high urgency campaign window for more clear results, but we can find additional ways to incorporate this language into the value proposition for future donor communications.

Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
(Other) visitors had a 4.3% increase in donations with a 92% level of confidence.
 	Email visitors had a 5.1% increase in donations with a 98% level of confidence.
 	Mobile visitors had a 163.7% increase in donations with a 98% level of confidence.
 	Returning visitors had a 78.0% increase in donations with a 96% level of confidence.
 	65+ visitors had a 5.8% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a radical redesign impacted donor conversion during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that humanizing the donation page with experiential language for fiscal year end donors will achieve an increased donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/28/2021 - 08/13/2021
During a fiscal year end campaign, we were seeing lower donor conversion compared to the previous year so we decided to develop a treatment to the donation page for the remainder of the campaign to see if we could improve performance. We modified the headline from “Help DTS reach the world with solid biblical training” to “You can help DTS train students to reach the world with the gospel of Jesus Christ!” We also removed body copy that seemed organization-centric that referenced enrollment trends and their accredited degree programs and instead of featuring the high-caliber quality of the training gave examples of vocations that students pursue after their education and training (counseling, pastoring, etc.) to humanize the institution and presented this as an experiential outcome for how they would “share the good news and strengthen believers” which aligns with what the donor wants to accomplish through their gift. We also introduced copy that highlighted the option to give monthly.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Since the experiment only ran during the final 2 days of the campaign, we would need to run an additional test with similar language to truly test the concept without the existing urgency of the deadline that could be influencing results. The treatment donation page had a 19.1% increase in conversion rate with 93% level of confidence. We want to take this directional lift in gifts on the page and see where else we could apply this learning.
While we did not reach statistical significance to validate the experiment, we did observe some notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment that are outlined below:
Revenue experienced a 47.3% decrease with a 97% level of confidence.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
New  visitors had a 39.8% increase in donations with a 99% level of confidence.Email visitors had a 23.9% increase in donations with a 98% level of confidence.Tablet visitors had a 100.0% decrease in donations with an 87% level of confidence.55-64 visitors had an 88.5% increase in donations with a 93% level of confidence.Female visitors had a 70.9% increase in donations with a 96% level of confidence.25-34 visitors had a 76.4% decrease in donations with a 96% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How increased urgency using a countdown clock affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a countdown clock near the call-to-action for landing page visitors will achieve more ebook downloads.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/16/2020 - 01/06/2021
In a previous experiment, we used a sticky bar to try and create a heightened sense of urgency around our ebook outlining strategies to increase giving during the last week of the year. But the sticky bar made no significant difference in conversions.
As a next step, we wondered if using a countdown clock lower on the page ticking down to the last week of the year would be a better means of conveying urgency and lead to more downloads.
experiments.keyLearnings: The countdown clock led to a 10.6% increase in conversion, although it didn't quite reach our desired level of confidence. However, it's a strong indicator that a countdown clock is an effective motivator to increase downloads in this context. We'll continue to use countdown clock on acquisition pages when there is a relevant deadline - in particularly around year-end and the last week of the year.

experiments.title: How increased urgency using a countdown clock affects email acquisition
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a countdown clock near the call-to-action for landing page visitors will achieve more ebook downloads.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/16/2020 - 01/06/2021
In a previous experiment, we used a sticky bar to try and create a heightened sense of urgency around our ebook outlining strategies to increase giving during the last week of the year. But the sticky bar made no significant difference in conversions.
As a next step, we wondered if using a countdown clock lower on the page ticking down to the last week of the year would be a better means of conveying urgency and lead to more downloads.
experiments.keyLearnings: The countdown clock led to a 10.6% increase in conversion, although it didn't quite reach our desired level of confidence. However, it's a strong indicator that a countdown clock is an effective motivator to increase downloads in this context. We'll continue to use countdown clock on acquisition pages when there is a relevant deadline - in particularly around year-end and the last week of the year.

experiments.title: How communal language on a high-urgency campaign sticky bar affects revenue
experiments.researchQuestion: Communal language will increase revenue during a high urgency campaigns.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/07/2021 - 05/30/2021
FamilyLife was running a matching gift campaign with a focus on recurring giving. Since communal language—copy that shows the reader that they are united with the organization in the most important beliefs and causes that they care for, all spoken with a familiarity of friends—works well for other faith-based organizations. We’d seen lifts of donations and revenue during high-urgency campaign and wanted to test if this would benefit FamilyLife as well. We tested a 50/50 split in traffic on their website.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Overall, there was a directional lift in revenue. This did not fully validate because of the 23% decrease in revenue for new visitors that pulled down the full validation of a 59% increase in revenue for returning visitors.
Our hypothesis is that communal language, invoking the tone of friendship and care, works very well with returning visitors because they are more closely acquainted with the organization. Whereas a intimate tone with a new acquaintance will not resonate.
Observations of the impact on other visitor segments in the experiment:
Organic Search visitors had a 21.5% increase in revenue with an 86% level of confidence.Paid Search visitors had a 57.7% decrease in revenue with a 98% level of confidence.Referral visitors had an 89.5% increase in revenue with an 88% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How the “fear of failure” at a campaign’s deadline impacted donor response rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe the fear of failure will increase donor response rates.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/08/2021
As a part of American’s for Prosperity’s year-end fundraising campaign, they had a final email appeal reminding the donor of an upcoming deadline (midnight) so as to inspire them to give.
We decided to experiment with a positive message (implying that as the deadline approached, that the reader’s gift right now could “put us over the top” of our goals) vs. a more negative tone that focused on the fear of loss (implying that “we, collectively, will not succeed” without the reader’s gift right now as the deadline is approaching).
experiments.keyLearnings:
With a 93.9% level of confidence (just shy of a significant result), we observed an increase of +374% in the donor conversion rate when using a negative tone (eg a message that emphasizes the fear of not hitting our collective goals without the donor's gift right now).

Subsequently, we also observed an increase in revenue from the treatment (negative tone) message of +898% (LoC: 85.8%).

This revenue increase was achieved by a combination of a +52% increase in the average gift size, as well as the increased response rate—which multiplied together to drive the revenue lift seen within the study.

Overall, the samples are pretty light and we'd like to further experiment with this approach again in future studies, but the results seem to indicate that the "fear of loss" within the treatment message drove a higher response and better generosity.

experiments.title: How adding urgency into Facebook ad copy affects conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: Does adding urgency to an ad through body copy increase conversion rate?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/26/2018 - 12/03/2018
Buckner International was offering a free Advent devotional to be delivered each week of December. They wanted to test if adding urgency around the start of the Advent season would increase conversion rate. The “December 2” deadline allow for a clear deadline that would necessitate the need for signing up sooner rather than later. We decided to emphasize that date and include some copy early in the ad to introduce that concept prior to Facebook’s “Read More” link that would hide the bottom portion of the ad.
experiments.keyLearnings: While we did not reach the desired 95% level of confidence needed to statistically validate the experiment, we did see a directional decrease of 18%. Since we had reached the Dec 2 deadline mentioned in the ad, we had to stop the ad before reaching confidence. Urgency is a factor that has often proved to increase conversion; in this case, however, it is our hypothesis that the added urgency went against the purpose of Advent. An Advent devotional is intended to be used as a way for an individual to slow down during a hectic season.

experiments.title: How the ask approach in the body copy affects donations in a high-urgency appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: Does a fear of loss motivate more donors than a high-urgency ask?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/29/2018 - 08/01/2018
Buckner International was nearing the last days of their summer match campaign. Given the tie-in with the direct mail, the actual deadline for the campaign was the next month but we could not wait that long in the online channel before moving on to the next campaign.
Since there was no hard deadline, we wanted to see if the “fear of losing the match” would be as high of a motivator as the urgency with no actual deadline. This approach was tested in the second and third paragraphs of the body copy, and the rest of the email remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings: Although we did receive a 100% increase in gifts with the treatment, this experiment did not reach validity. We should test this approach again in future high-urgency campaigns with a more radical approach to effectively measure whether fear of loss truly motivates donors to give.

experiments.title: How acknowledging listeners impacts engagement with our web asset during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that leading with a thank-you to Radio Player listeners will increase engagement and downstream donor metrics because it acknowledges their presence, deepens their connection to Moody Radio, and invites generosity at the moment they’re already engaged.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 01/23/2026 - 03/09/2026
To increase engagement on Moody Radio’s player, we tested a more direct, listener-focused headline. We believed that showing gratitude upfront would build connection and boost engagement and giving.
Thanking listeners upfront recognizes their presence, reinforces their role, and prompts generosity. This approach meets the audience while they’re already tuned in, making it a good time to invite deeper support.
This test ran in the lead-up to Spring Share. We evenly split traffic across variations to ensure a reliable comparison.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key takeaway from this experiment is that leading with a thank-you message for Radio Player listeners did not produce a measurable lift in engagement or downstream donor metrics.
While the treatment version saw a 4.8% decrease in Content Offer CTA clicks compared to the control, the result was not statistically significant (33% confidence vs. the 95% threshold). This means we cannot reliably attribute the performance difference to the headline change.
However, looking further down the funnel provides important context. Both the control and treatment groups generated 11 gifts each. This suggests that although the thank-you message drove fewer clicks, the traffic it did generate may have been more qualified or motivated to give.
Therefore, these results should not be used to make any definitive conclusions or changes based on this experiment alone. In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence.
Additionally, it may be beneficial to further test variations of the messaging or explore other strategies to increase engagement and donor metrics among Radio Player listeners.

experiments.title: How a donor-centric question impacts engagement on a high urgency campaign slide up
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the treatment language for Moody Radio website visitors will increase clicks and downstream donor metrics because leading with a donor-focused question creates immediate emotional resonance and curiosity in the viewer.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/02/2026 - 03/14/2026
To optimize our web assets, we tested a donor-centric question in the lower third of Moody’s Spring Share campaign. The control used our standard approach; the treatment asked, “Do you love Moody Radio?”
This test builds on proven elements we’ve already implemented, adding an additional layer of optimization focused on emotional engagement.
Our hypothesis was that the treatment would drive more traffic to the donation page because the short, donor-focused question creates immediate emotional resonance and curiosity.
By inviting listeners to respond internally before fully processing the ask, the message reduces friction and increases the likelihood of a click.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key takeaway from this experiment is that the donor-focused question in the treatment drove a 15.8% increase in clicks compared to the control. However, the result did not reach statistical significance (81.1% confidence vs. the 95% threshold), so it should not be used to inform definitive decisions.

More importantly, this increase in clicks did not translate into increased giving. The treatment actually saw a 5.6% decrease in gifts (22.7% confidence), suggesting that while the question sparked curiosity and drove more engagement, it did not attract users who were ready to give.

This indicates a gap between engagement and conversion. The question may have lowered friction for clicking, but it did not sufficiently reinforce motivation to take the next step.

Moving forward, it would be valuable to continue testing donor-centric language while strengthening the connection between the initial hook and the giving opportunity. Refining the message to more clearly tie emotional engagement to impact—and testing across different placements or audience segments—can help determine whether this approach can drive not just clicks, but meaningful donor action.

experiments.title: How the addition of a goal thermometer and countdown clock influence donor conversion during a high-urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of a goal thermometer and countdown clock to the CYE Donation page will increase overall donor conversion, as these elements reinforce urgency and visible progress, helping donors feel both the timeliness and the collective impact of their gift at the moment of decision.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/25/2025 - 01/01/2026
At the tail end of IMB’s year-end campaign, we tested the isolated impact of adding a countdown clock and goal thermometer to the donation page. The test ran for approximately five days, with traffic evenly split between the two variants.
All copy and value proposition remained consistent across both pages; the only variables introduced were the countdown clock and goal thermometer. This approach enabled us to directly assess the impact of urgency and progress indicators on donor conversion, without confounding changes to messaging.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results indicate that adding a countdown clock and goal thermometer to the IMB year-end donation page had no meaningful impact on donor conversion. While the treatment showed a 2.7% decrease in conversion rate, the result carried a low level of confidence (24.2% LOC) and does not suggest a true negative effect.

Under different circumstances, this test would have benefited from a longer run time to achieve greater statistical clarity. However, given the limited window and high stakes of the year-end campaign, extending the test was not feasible. As a result, we conclude that urgency and progress indicators alone were not sufficient to materially influence donor behavior during this phase of the campaign.

experiments.title: How a slide-up versus a slide-down performs during a Giving Tuesday high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a different slide-up/down tactic for potential donors will lead to an improvement in donation conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/26/2025 - 11/30/2025
This test aimed to assess the impact of implementing a slide‑down banner instead of a slide‑up banner during a peak giving moment. We hypothesized that the slide‑down banner would be more visually prominent, capture user attention more effectively, and increase conversion. The slide-up banner was the control in this instance, as we had previously tested it during other time periods, and it proved to be the winner.
In this experiment, the slide-up appeared after the user started scrolling down the page, and the slide-down appeared once the visitor went to the homepage, remaining at the top of the page as the user scrolled. Users could minimize the slide-down banner if they wished; even when minimized, a donation array continued to display at the top of the page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results show a directional decrease in overall conversion and RPV. This suggests that the slide‑down banner may have been perceived as distracting and less visually appealing, ultimately leading to reduced conversion.

Key Results:

-       -10% non-statistically significant decrease (71%) in overall conversion rate.

-       -2% non-statistically significant decrease (54%) in overall RPV.

Overall analysis:

As show in the statistics above, the slide down treatment version had a 10% decrease in overall transaction conversion rates, with a 70% level of confidence. The treatment also had a slightly lower revenue per visitor, showing a 1.8% decrease with a 54% level of confidence.

 

Desktop versus mobile analysis:

Desktop:

Control had a 6.4% conversion rate

Treatment had a 6.1% conversion rate, which is only a 4.6% decrease with a 27% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor increased slightly on the treatment version, with a 10% increase and a 67% level of confidence in that result.

Mobile:

Control had a 4.6% conversion rate

Treatment had a 3.7% conversion rate, which is a 19% decrease with an 80% level of confidence. Moreover, revenue per visitor also declined on the treatment version by 39%, with a 97% level of confidence.

 

Recurring versus one-time analysis

Recurring:

Control: 0.69% conversion rate

Treatment: 0.71% conversion rate, which is a 3% increase with only an 8% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also was marginally impacted, with a 1% increase but only a 50% level of confidence.

 

One-time

Control: 4.8% conversion rate

Treatment: 4.2% conversion rate, which is a 12% decrease with a 75% level of confidence. Revenue per visitor also marginally decreased by 1% with a 51% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a Percentage-Based Goal Thermometer Drives Donor Conversion in High-Urgency Campaigns
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of a percentage-based goal thermometer to the CYE Giving page will increase donor conversion. By showing progress without emphasizing the total dollar amount, the thermometer helps donors see their gift as a meaningful contribution to a shared goal, motivating them to join in and take action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/10/2025 - 12/14/2025
During Moody Radio’s Year-End campaign, we tested adding a percentage-based goal thermometer. Previous tests with a dollar-based thermometer on giving pages showed less impact on donation lift than expected.
We hypothesized that this was due to the size of the overall goal—individual donors may have felt that their gift wouldn’t make a meaningful impact on progress.
This approach aimed to increase donor engagement, and early results indicated it was successful.
experiments.keyLearnings:
