The key learning from this experiment is clear. Adding a percentage-based goal thermometer to giving pages increased donations by 20.8% for all traffic. This indicates that displaying progress as a percentage can enhance donor conversion rates.
However, the statistical confidence level was 75.3%, below the 95% threshold. This means the results may not be reliable for future decisions, though they are still directional. Future studies should further investigate the impact of the percentage-based thermometer, possibly by modifying its design or placement. It is also important to ensure that experiments are well-powered and have high statistical confidence to draw valid conclusions.

experiments.title: How defaulting the monthly tab on an acquisition offer as apart of a high urgency campaign affects recurring conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that defaulting to the monthly tab will achieve an increase in recurring conversion rates because it reduces friction by aligning with the donor’s default behavior, subtly encourages sustained giving through visual anchoring, and reframes the donation as an ongoing commitment rather than a one-time gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/14/2025 - 07/19/2025
The Heritage Foundation is running their annual President’s Match Challenge campaign to members and non-members of Heritage. In this experiment, they wanted to test how defaulting to the monthly tab on the acquisition donation page of an ebook offer as apart of the campaign would affect the rate of recurring giving.
50% of traffic was randomly assigned to each variation (Control vs Treatment). The primary goal was to determine whether defaulting the donation format to monthly giving (rather than one-time giving) would influence the recurring conversion rate.
The idea to run this test stemmed from two core insights:
Behavioral Economics Research:
People tend to go with the default option presented to them. When monthly giving is presented as the default tab, it subtly frames that behavior as the norm or recommended path.
Organizational Goal: Boost Recurring Revenue:
Like many nonprofits, Heritage is invested in growing their base of recurring donors. Monthly gifts provide more predictable, long-term revenue.
By making “Make a Monthly Gift” the default selection (as seen in the treatment version), the hypothesis was that donors would be more likely to choose that option simply because it required no extra clicks or decisions to get there.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. Defaulting to the monthly tab on the donation page resulted in a 0.2% increase in Transactions - Monthly for All Traffic, indicating a potential positive impact on recurring conversion rates.

2. The increase in Revenue by 134% with a 100% level of confidence suggests that while the primary metric did not meet the desired level of significance, there was a significant improvement in the total amount of revenue generated.

3. The results should be cautiously interpreted due to the statistical level of confidence being below the recommended threshold of 85%, indicating that the findings may not be 100% reliable.

4. Future experimentation should consider adjusting the duration of the experiment or increasing the sample size to ensure more robust results.

5. Despite the inconclusive nature of the primary metric, the significant increase in Revenue implies that there may be value in further exploring the impact of defaulting to the monthly tab for donation offerings.

6. It is essential to continue testing this hypothesis in future experiments to validate the findings and determine the true impact on recurring conversion rates.

In conclusion, while the experiment did not provide a definitive answer regarding the impact of defaulting to the monthly tab on recurring conversion rates, the increase in Revenue suggests that there may be potential benefits to implementing this strategy. Further testing and optimization are recommended to gain a deeper understanding of the effects and to make more informed decisions in the future.

experiments.title: How including matching gift language in a high urgency campaign overlay impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including matching gift language in the EL2 overlay for website visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because donors will perceive their gift as having twice the impact and feel a heightened urgency to give within the limited-time match window.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/10/2025 - 07/29/2025
During the Navigators’ Eagle Lake 2 campaign, we ran an A/B test to evaluate the impact of matching gift language on donor conversion.
Specifically, we tested whether highlighting a matching opportunity in the overlay would further motivate web visitors to give.
The experiment was live for approximately three weeks, with traffic evenly split between the control (no match mention) and the treatment (match highlighted). Our goal was to determine if this added urgency and incentive would result in more gifts to support Eagle Lake.
experiments.keyLearnings:
In this experiment, adding matching gift language to the Eagle Lake 2 website overlay led to an 8.3% increase in donations across all traffic. While the treatment group outperformed the control group, the statistical confidence level was only 27.8%, falling well short of the standard 95% benchmark needed to validate results.

Notably, the treatment group also saw a slight decline in revenue of 3.4%, with a confidence level of 53.6%. These low confidence levels indicate that neither the lift in donations nor the revenue change can be considered statistically significant.

The data suggests no conclusive evidence that the presence of matching gift language in the overlay had a positive or negative effect on donor behavior. As such, these results should not be used to inform future strategy or implementation.

Note: Donations over $500 were excluded from this analysis.

experiments.title: How a gift array vs a single button call-to-action in a high urgency campaign footer bar affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the gift array version of the footer for Moody Radio website traffic will achieve an increase in engagement and donor conversion metrics because it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page. because it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2025 - 06/30/2025
During Moody Radio’s Fiscal Year End campaign, we tested into a gift array within the lower third footer vs the standard one-button call-to-action.
While the gift array version had been used in previous campaigns, there had not been a direct test none on the gift array portion alone to understand the motivation of donors to Moody Radio.
This test ran for almost a full month, with equal traffic seeing the control and the treatment.
We believe that using a gift array on a sticky bar for website visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment testing a gift array in the lower third footer of Moody Radio's website are as follows: The one-button version resulted in a 4.6% increase in Transactions for All Traffic compared to the standard one-button call-to-action. The statistical level of confidence for this increase was only 13.3%, which is below the standard threshold of 95% for validity.

While we were unable to reach validity on this test due to the limited time frame, the results do tell us that Moody Radio's audience may be more motivated to give when there is only a single call-to-action button. On the other hand, the results suggest that the gift array version of the footer bar led to a higher average gift.

*Development team added parameters to custom transaction and revenue metrics based on the "src" associated with each variant.

experiments.title: How a gift array vs a single button call-to-action in a high urgency campaign footer bar affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the gift array version of the footer for Moody Radio website traffic will achieve an increase in clicks because it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2025 - 06/30/2025
During Moody Radio’s Fiscal Year End campaign, we tested into a gift array within the lower third footer vs the standard one-button call-to-action.
While the gift array version had been used in previous campaigns, there had not been a direct test none on the gift array portion alone to understand the motivation of donors to Moody Radio.
This test ran for almost a full month, with equal traffic seeing the control and the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key takeaway from this experiment is that the gift array version of the footer for Moody Radio did not lead to an increase in clicks, as initially hypothesized. In fact, we observed a 4.2% decrease in clicks across all traffic, with a statistical confidence of 36.3%. This is well below the accepted 95% threshold for validity.

Additionally, downstream metrics showed a 4.4% decrease in transactions, with a confidence level of only 13.3%.

These findings suggest that the gift array version of the footer did not effectively drive more users to the donation page and may have resulted in less motivated traffic. While these metrics did not meet the validity criteria, they point to the possibility that there may be more effective ways to encourage donations from Moody Radio listeners beyond the gift array format.

In future experimentation, it is important to consider that what may seem like a logical approach to increasing user engagement, such as allowing users to select a gift amount before reaching the donation page, may not always lead to the desired outcome. It is essential to test all changes thoroughly before implementing them on a larger scale. Additionally, it is crucial to ensure that experiments are statistically sound and reach a high level of confidence before drawing conclusions.

experiments.title: How a gift array vs a single button call-to-action in a high urgency campaign footer bar affects clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that he gift array version of the footer  for Moody Bible website traffic will achieve an increase in clicks because it allows the users to already begin the process of giving by selecting a gift amount before they even arrive on the donation page.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/02/2025 - 06/30/2025
During Moody Bible’s Fiscal Year End campaign, we tested into a gift array within the lower third footer vs the standard one-button call-to-action.
While the gift array version had been used in previous campaigns, there had not been a direct test none on the gift array portion alone to understand the motivation of donors to Moody Bible.
This text ran for almost a full month, with equal traffic seeing the control and the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the gift array version of the footer for the Moody Bible website traffic showed a 4.3% increase in clicks compared to the standard one-button call-to-action. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 21.1%, which is below the 95% threshold needed to consider the results valid.
Furthermore, the treatment saw a 26.1% lift in transactions at a 43.7% LOC, meaning that the treatment led to more people engaging with the footer bar as well as led to more motivated traffic to make a gift.
However, the treatment actually resulted in a 20.7% decrease in revenue at a 56.7% level of confidence (pulled out of the number of transactions)
Control: 11 gifts, $3747
Treatment: 14 gifts, $3780
These results indicate that the treatment was more effective at getting people to engage and make a gift, but ultimately, the control produced a higher average of revenue per donor.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that a statistically significant level of confidence is achieved before making decisions based on the results. This means conducting the experiment for a longer duration, increasing the sample size, or making adjustments to the experiment design to improve the reliability of the results.

experiments.title: How a goal thermometer on a high-urgency campaign donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of a goal thermometer for FYE donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it creates a sense of social proof by visibly showing real-time progress toward the goal and highlighting that others are actively contributing, we foster a sense of community and shared purpose.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2025 - 06/30/2025
During Moody Bible’s Fiscal Year-End Campaign, we tested the impact of adding a goal thermometer on donor conversion.
The goal of this test was to leverage social proof by emphasizing a shared, communal objective to meet a specific financial need for the ministry, ultimately driving an increase in both transactions and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment is that the addition of a goal thermometer on the FYE donation page led to a 2.4% increase in Transactions for All Traffic, but with a low statistical level of confidence at 19.1%. This means that while there was a positive impact, the results cannot be considered reliable for future decision-making.

However, revenue saw a substantial 400% decrease with 100% confidence, indicating a clear negative impact from the goal thermometer. These results suggest that Moody Bible’s audience may be more inclined to make smaller donations when a goal thermometer is present, potentially due to the high fundraising target set for the FYE campaign. Further testing is needed to refine these insights and draw more definitive conclusions. It may also be worth exploring the use of a daily campaign goal, which could help reduce the perceived pressure of reaching a large target and encourage more frequent contributions.

Moving forward, it is important to take into consideration the statistical significance of the results when evaluating the effectiveness of different strategies. While the idea of leveraging social proof through a goal thermometer showed some promise in increasing donor conversion metrics, further testing and optimization are necessary to draw valid conclusions. It is important to refine the messaging and design of the goal thermometer to ensure it effectively communicates progress towards a communal objective without negatively impacting revenue. Future experimentation should focus on testing variations of the goal thermometer to find the most effective approach for driving donor conversions without compromising financial outcomes.

experiments.title: How a goal thermometer on a high-urgency campaign donation page impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the addition of a goal thermometer for FYE donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because is creates a sense of social proof by visibly showing real-time progress toward the goal and highlighting that others are actively contributing, we foster a sense of community and shared purpose..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2025 - 06/30/2025
During Today in the Word’s Fiscal Year-End Campaign, we tested the impact of adding a goal thermometer on donor conversion.
The goal of this test was to leverage social proof by emphasizing a shared, communal objective to meet a specific financial need for the ministry. Ultimately, the goal was to drive an increase in both transactions and revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that while the treatment version with the goal thermometer led to a decrease in transactions for all traffic, it resulted in 1.5% decrease at a 10.8%level of confidence, which is below the threshold of 95% for validity. These results indicate that Today in the Word's audience didn't respond negatively or positively to the presence of the thermometer.

However, revenue saw a 9.6% increase at a 100% level of confidence, indicating that the goal thermometer was effective at motivating donors to give larger amounts. Therefore, we should find ways to incorporate thermometers during other Today in the Word campaigns.

In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further explore the impact of the goal thermometer on revenue and donor behavior. It may also be helpful to consider other factors that could have influenced the results, such as the messaging and design of the goal thermometer. Additionally, conducting the experiment for a longer period of time and with a larger sample size could provide more reliable insights.

Overall, the key takeaway from this experiment is that the goal thermometer can be a valuable tool for driving increased revenue and encouraging larger donations, even if it does not directly impact the number of transactions.

experiments.title: How aligning donation page copy with giving level impacts average gift during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an impact bullet to the donation page for a larger amount for April Eagle Lake donation page visitors will achieve an increase in average gift because the donation page copy calls out a higher impact bullet that is more in line with their previous giving levels.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/02/2025 - 05/04/2025
Every spring The Navigators run an April Eagle Lake campaign. At the beginning of this campaign, we ran a gift array test based on the donor’s lifetime revenue, but didn’t quite see the results that we anticipated.
In an effort to get a valid learning, we tested adding an additional impact bullet to the donation page for the two higher segments of lifetime donors to try to boost their average gift. The idea behind the test is that adding a higher level of impact bullet would lead to larger gifts from a segment of people who are financially dedicated to The Navigators.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment group, which included adding an impact bullet at a higher level to the donation page for higher segments of lifetime donors, saw a significant increase in revenue for all traffic by 67.3% as well as a 300% increase in transactions at an 89.3% LOC. However, the average gift for the control was $150 and $63 for the treatment. This indicates that The Navigators audience was more likely to give with the higher impact bullet amount added but they were more likely to give smaller gifts.

Overall, the experiment provided valuable insights into the potential impact of emphasizing higher donation levels to significant donors. By refining the experiment design and testing different variables, we can continue to optimize our donation page and enhance donor engagement and revenue in future campaigns.

experiments.title: How impact callouts on a high urgency pop-up impacts donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding impact callouts to the April Eagle Lake overlay for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it offers the tangible difference each donation amount can make.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 05/05/2025
For The Navigators’ April Eagle Lake campaign, we tested adding impact callouts to each gift array amount on the overlay. When a donor selected a gift amount, that amount was automatically pre-selected on the donation page, where the corresponding impact message was reinforced in the copy—creating a more seamless and compelling giving experience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding impact callouts to the April Eagle Lake overlay for homepage visitors resulted in a small increase of 0.4% in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only at 1.1%, which is below the standard threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

However, after further investigation, the treatment had a negative impact on revenue. The treatment saw a 35.6% decrease in revenue at a 61.8% level of confidence. While these results did not validate they do suggest that the impact callouts were negatively trending.

In future experimentation, we should ensure that we have a sufficiently large sample size to achieve a higher level of statistical significance. Additionally, we should consider refining the impact messaging in the callouts to potentially make a larger impact on donor conversion rates. It is also important to continue testing and iterating on different strategies to optimize the donation experience for our visitors.

experiments.title: How impact callouts on a high urgency sticky bar impacts click rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including impact callouts on the April Eagle Lake pushdown for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in click rate and engagement with the pushdown because it offers the tangible difference each donation amount can make. because it offers the tangible difference each donation amount can make.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/17/2025 - 05/05/2025
For The Navigators’ April Eagle Lake campaign, we tested adding impact callouts to each gift array amount on the pushdown. When a donor selected a gift amount, that amount was automatically pre-selected on the donation page, where the corresponding impact message was reinforced in the copy—creating a more seamless and compelling giving experience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including impact callouts on the April Eagle Lake pushdown for homepage visitors resulted in an 18.4% increase in Clicked CTA button for All Traffic. While this result is promising, the statistical level of confidence was only 53.1%, which is below the generally accepted level of 95%. These results indicate that the impact callouts did in fact get more people to engage with the pushdown.

Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making. In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical confidence level is high enough to draw reliable conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to explore different ways of including impact callouts or other strategies to improve donor conversion metrics.

experiments.title: How a gift array personalized by giving level impacts donor conversion metrics during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that raising the gift array for April Eagle Lake recipients with lifetime revenue between $100-$499 will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because it better aligns with their giving capacity therefore prompting larger gift amounts.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/29/2025
For The Navigators, April Eagle Lake campaign this year, we chose to test into gift arrays that correlated to the users giving level.
Therefore, for individuals with lifetime revenue between $100-$499, we split the recipients in half and tested the following gift arrays. Our goal was that prompting the individuals with a higher gift array would lead to an increase in donor conversion metrics.
Control: $25,$75,$150,$300
Treatment: $75,$150,$300,$450
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that raising the gift array for April Eagle Lake recipients with a lifetime revenue between $100-$499 resulted in a 43.8% increase in donations for all traffic. While this indicates a positive impact on donor conversion metrics, the statistical level of confidence was only 43.0%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95% for validity. These results did not validate, but they are directional.

In terms of revenue, the treatment saw a 243.9% lift in revenue at a 92.6% level of confidence. This metric did not quote reach validity, but it does show that donors were willing to make larger gifts with a higher set gift array. Furthermore, the control had an average gift of $42.50 and the treatment had an average gift of $101.67.

In the future, it is important to ensure that experiments have a higher statistical level of confidence to draw valid conclusions, but due to the small list sizes for this campaign, we were unable to reach validity. Additionally, it may be beneficial to conduct further testing with different gift arrays or segments of donors to better understand the impact of varying gift amounts on donor conversion metrics. By conducting experiments with higher levels of confidence and testing different variables, we can gather more reliable insights to inform future fundraising strategies.

experiments.title: Will adding a timer on the donation page increase urgency?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a timer to the campaign donation page for the end of the campaign will achieve more transactions because because the added element will increase urgency.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/03/2025
Moody Radio recently wrapped up their Annual Spring Share campaign.
This campaign drives a lot of traffic to the donation pages from on-air, paid social and paid search, as well as organic. This campaign is about a month long which can seem like a long time for a donor to have the opportunity to give to where they can push it off.
We wanted to see if we could increase the urgency to give on the donation page instead of waiting by adding a countdown time to the end of the campaign. To make sure this was the most effective, we waited to launch this until the last 4 days of the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a timer to the campaign donation page for the end of the campaign did result in a 6.7% increase in donations for all traffic. While this is a positive outcome, the statistical level of confidence was only 42.8%, which means that the results cannot be considered valid or reliable.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is sufficiently large to generate statistically significant results. Additionally, it may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period of time to capture more data and allow for more conclusive findings.

Overall, while the initial results are promising, it is important to take caution in interpreting the results and to continue conducting rigorous experiments to drive decision-making in the future.

experiments.title: Is increasing urgency the best way to convert donors on a lower third?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a countdown timer on the lower third banner for website content pages will achieve more transactions because adding the countdown timer to the end of the campaign will increase the urgency for a user to make a gift.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 04/03/2025
Moody Radio just concluded its annual Spring Share campaign.
During this campaign, we tested adding several elements across all parts of their website.
For this specific test, we wanted to see if we could increase the urgency on the lower third banner that shows on the content pages of moodyradio.org
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a countdown timer on the lower third banner for website content pages did not achieve the desired outcome of increasing donations during the Spring Share campaign. The treatment actually resulted in a 24.3% decrease in donations for all traffic, which indicates that the countdown timer may have had a negative impact on user engagement and conversion.

It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for this experiment was only 57.6%, which means that the results are not statistically significant and should not be used for future decision making. It is crucial to always ensure a high level of confidence (at least 95%) in the results of A/B experiments before making any conclusions or implementing changes based on those results.

In future experimentation, it is important to carefully design and implement A/B tests with a focus on achieving a high level of statistical confidence. It may also be beneficial to consider conducting qualitative research or usability testing to gather more insights into user behavior and preferences before implementing changes on the website. This will help ensure that any future experiments are more impactful and effective in achieving the desired goals.

experiments.title: Will a dynamic sticky bar convert donors better for Giving Tuesday?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a rotating message in the sticky bar for the Giving Tuesday homepage sticky bar element will achieve a higher donor conversion because the rotating message is eyecatching and can attract more attention to the space rather than having a static wiki. It also contains different value proposition that may entice a donor to give. because the rotating message is eyecatching and can attract more attention to the space rather than having a static wiki. It also contains different value proposition that may entice a donor to give. because the rotating message is eyecatching and can attract more attention to the space rather than having a static wiki. It also contains different value proposition that may entice a donor to give. because the rotating message is eyecatching and can attract more attention to the space rather than having a static wiki. It also contains different value proposition that may entice a donor to give.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/18/2025
For CYE 2024 we wanted to test a different approach to have on the homepage for Giving Tuesday, which in turn would inform the control that we would need to have for the next test.
For this test, we wanted to see if having a sticky bar with a rotating message would increase donor conversion, rather than having a static sticky bar with a donor countdown. This test would only show on the homepage.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing a rotating message in the sticky bar for the Giving Tuesday homepage did not lead to an increase in donor conversion. In fact, the treatment resulted in a 34.5% decrease in donations for all traffic.

It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only 75.2%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. Therefore, these results based on transactions cannot be considered valid for future decision making.

However, similar to the Radio test we did see a massive increase in revenue from the dynamic sticky bar. In fact, we saw the same $4k lift in revenue. We're still digging into outlier gifts with the moody team but, it is interesting that this test is following suit.

experiments.title: Will a Dynamic sticky bar work better than a wiki for Giving Tuesday?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that having a rotating message in the sticky bar for the Giving Tuesday homepage sticky bar element will achieve a higher donor conversion because the rotating message is eyecatching and can attract more attention to the space rather than having a static wiki. It also contains different value proposition that may entice a donor to give.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/18/2025
For CYE 2024 we wanted to test a different approach to have on the homepage for Giving Tuesday, which in turn would inform the control that we would need to have for the next test.
For this test we wanted to see if having a sticky bar with a rotating message would increase donor conversion rather than having a wiki bar at the top of the page. This test would only show on the homepage.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment with the rotating message in the sticky bar did not perform better than the control with a static wiki in terms of donor conversion. Despite seeing a 4.9% decrease in donations for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was only 22.1%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95% for validity.

However, while this test did not validate on transactions, it has a significant increase in revenue. In fact, the revenue for the treatment was $4,200 more than the control. Based on the revenue being significantly higher we went ahead and rolled out the dynamic sticky bar to be our control for the next test. We are working with the Moody team to see if there was a major gift included in the results to make the jump in revenue that drastic.

experiments.title: Will adding urgency to web elements increase transactions?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that increasing urgency for the lower third banner will achieve more transactions because the added urgency will motivate people to give.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/28/2024 - 01/01/2025
During CYE we went throughs several versions of web elements to see which one would be the best to show on content pages. We found that adding what we’re calling a ‘lower third’ banner to the bottom of the page increased transactions compared to a dear reader.
Our next step was to find out how to optimize the lower third to present it in the best way possible. For this test, we wanted to see if adding urgency to the lower third element would increase transactions. Below is what we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding urgency to the lower third banner can potentially increase transactions. Although the treatment group saw a 4.5% increase in transactions for all traffic, the statistical level of confidence was only 19.9%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%. This means that the results cannot be deemed reliable for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the sample size is adequate to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to further refine the concept of urgency and test different variations to see which one resonates most with the audience. It is also valuable to continue testing and iterating on different elements of the lower third banner to identify the most effective strategies for increasing transactions on the website.

experiments.title: Will adding a countdown timer to a donation page effectively increase urgency?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a countdown timer to the donation page for the last week of the camapgin will achieve more transactions because the countdown timer will reinforce that time is running out and your donation is needed now.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/28/2024 - 01/01/2025
For CYE this year we wanted to see if we could increase urgency on the donation page by adding a countdown timer to when the campaign ends.
We started this test the last week of the campaign when urgency should be at it’s peak.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a countdown timer to the donation page during the last week of a campaign can potentially increase revenue for all traffic. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was at 0.0%, which means that the results cannot be considered valid.

In future experimentation, it is essential to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to yield statistically significant results. It may be beneficial to run similar experiments with a larger sample size to accurately determine the impact of adding a countdown timer on donation page conversions.

Overall, while the initial results are promising, it is important to use caution in interpreting the findings and to prioritize statistical significance in making data-driven decisions for future campaigns and optimizations.

experiments.title: How impacted gift array links in email impacts donor conversion during a high urgency appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that impacted gift array links for email users during a holiday campaign will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate and overall revenue because it eliminates friction, pre-filling the selected amount on the donation page, while also reinforcing that their gift is being matched in the copy.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/21/2025
During CaringBridge’s holiday campaign, we hypothesized that using impacted gift array links to drive urgency and eliminate friction during the 2-day match would increase donor conversion rate and overall revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that using impacted gift array links for email users during a holiday campaign led to a decrease in donations for all traffic. Additionally, the treatment saw a dropoff in average gift with $146 in the control versus $65 in the treatment.

Ultimately, the level of confidence was 78.9%, meaning these results are not valid for future decision-making. It would also be worth re-testing this experience for validity outside the window of a high-urgency appeal. Potentially, the treatment may have resulted in too much decision friction at the top of the funnel, whereas the links would have proved more effective simply by increasing clicks and greater traffic to the donation page.

experiments.title: How a standard overlay compares to an overlay with a gift array during a high-urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that an urgency overlay with an interactive gift array for site traffic during the final day of the holiday appeal will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate and overall revenue because the array prefills the selected amount onto the donation page, eliminating friction and providing users who are likely coming to the site in order to make their gift with a first step on page load.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/31/2024 - 01/01/2025
During the final day of CaringBridge’s holiday campaign, half the traffic received a standard urgency overlay whereas the other half received an overlay with an interactive gift array. The frequency and amount the user selected would then be prefilled onto the donation page, eliminating friction and helping to encourage a higher gift amount. We hypothesized that the treatment would increase donor conversion rate and overall revenue, providing users with a gift array on page load – assuming that much of the traffic will be coming to CaringBridge.org on the final day of the year in order to make their year-end gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the treatment, which included an urgency overlay with an interactive gift array, resulted in a 5.3% increase in donations for all traffic on the final day of the holiday campaign. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 48.8%, which means the results cannot be considered valid.

It's important to note that this experiment was conducted during the final day of the year, where urgency is extremely high and uniquely motivated. As seen in the little to no difference between control and treatment, it is possible that users on the site at this time already know that they intend to make their gift – meaning that whether encountering version 1 or version 2 it doesn't significantly sway that decision negatively or positively.

experiments.title: How a humanized sticky bar on a high-urgency campaign donation page impacts donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that the presence of the humanized sticky bar on the CYE Donation page for email 16 visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the personalized message boosts trust and creates a more personalized experience for the potential donor.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/30/2024 - 12/30/2024
During IMB’s CYE campaign, we implemented a humanized sticky bar on the donation page as part of the strategy for Email 16.
This sticky bar ran for a full day, with the test limited to users who accessed the donation page via a link embedded in Email 16.
The sticky bar treatment was shown to 50% of the page’s traffic, allowing us to evaluate its effectiveness in driving engagement and conversions in this specific segment of our audience.
The control featured no sticky bar.
The treatment sticky bar featured Chuck Anderson, sender of Email 16, and had the following copy: “There’s only one day remaining to support IMB missionaries through your year-end gift to the Lottie Moon Christmas Offering®. As a reminder, your gift will play a crucial role in sharing the joy and peace of Jesus with those who have yet to hear His name. – Chuck Anderson”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the humanized sticky bar featuring Chuck Anderson on the donation page for Email 16 visitors did not result in an increase in donor conversion metrics. In fact, the treatment saw a 15.0% decrease in transactions for all traffic with a statistical confidence level of 25.6%, indicating that the control group performed better.
It is important to note that the statistical confidence level is below the threshold of 95%, which means that these results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it is important to consider the impact of humanized elements like sticky bars on donor behavior. It may be beneficial to further test different variations of humanized elements or personalize them based on donor preferences to see if this can lead to an increase in conversions. Additionally, ensuring a longer experiment duration and a larger sample size can help in obtaining more reliable and valid results for future decision making.

experiments.title: How yes/no “autonomy” language on a pop-up impacts conversion rate during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a yes and no option for homepage visitors during CYE will achieve an increase in the donor conversion rate because the autonomy to make a micro-decision allows the visitors to feel in control before being asked to make a gift.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2025
For IMB’s CYE campaign, we decided to test into donor autonomy on the homepage pop-up. We have had success with this concept with other clients, so we anticipated this concept would also perform well for IMB.
The hypothesis for this test is that having an option to say “yes” or “no” gives the potential donor a micro-decision to feel as if they are in control of the situation.
The control featured a single CTA: “Make my gift now »”
The treatment offered two options: “Yes, I’ll make a gift” and “No, not at this time” (this option closed out the pop-up)
We ran the pop-up from Dec 9 – Dec 27, 2025 with 50% of traffic receiving the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that providing visitors with the autonomy to make a micro-decision on the homepage pop-up can lead to an increase in donor conversion rate. The treatment, which included a "Yes, I'll make a gift" and "No, not at this time" option, resulted in a 25.0% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only at 82.7%, falling below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for validity.

Despite the statistical level of confidence falling short, the experiment still provides valuable insights. In future experimentation, it would be beneficial to continue exploring the concept of donor autonomy and how it influences visitor behavior. Additionally, it may be helpful to further refine the language and options presented in the pop-up to see if different variations can yield even better results.

Overall, the learnings from this experiment can inform future testing strategies and emphasize the importance of giving visitors a sense of control in their decision-making process on the website. This insight can be applied to optimize donation processes and potentially improve conversion rates in future campaigns.

experiments.title: How testing a dual action slide-out on article pages impacts donor conversion during FamilyLife’s high urgency calendar year-end campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that splitting the donation ask into two steps will increase conversion rates. By first asking whether FamilyLife has provided value and then following up with a donation request, we reduce cognitive load and create a smoother, less overwhelming user experience, which will lead to higher engagement and more conversions.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/08/2025
FamilyLife, a nonprofit dedicated to strengthening families through biblically-based resources, conducted this experiment as part of their high urgency calendar year-end campaign. FamilyLife uses a sticky donation slide-out that is always visible on article pages. This slide-out prompts users with the message: “Has FamilyLife given you and your family $25 worth of practical resources and biblical insights this year? If so, can you take a moment to donate to ensure that we’re always here for you when your family needs encouragement and support?” Users can then click “Yes” to be directed to the donation page, or “No” to close the element.
For this campaign, we wanted to test whether splitting the question into two parts would lead to higher conversion rates. In the Treatment, when the user first clicks on the sticky slide-out, they would only see the first part of the question—“Has FamilyLife given you $25 worth of practical resources and biblical insights this year?” If they click “Yes,” the second part of the message would appear, asking for a donation. If they click “No,” the element would close without prompting for a donation.
We believe that splitting the ask into two steps could improve engagement and conversion for several reasons. First, by breaking down the question, we reduce the initial cognitive load on users, making the first interaction feel less like a commitment and more like a simple, low-effort engagement. This might encourage more people to click “Yes.” Once they’ve answered affirmatively, they’ll then be presented with a clearer, more straightforward ask for a donation, which could feel less transactional and more natural. Additionally, by limiting the ask to one step at a time, we avoid overwhelming users with too much information or too big of a request at once, which could increase the likelihood of a conversion.
The Control group used the standard, single-message approach, while the Treatment group implemented the dual ask. This experiment was set up as a split A/B test with the traffic split 50/50 between the Control (standard slide-out) and the Treatment (dual ask). The goal was to determine if breaking the ask into two steps would create a smoother, more engaging donation flow, leading to higher conversions. Both the Control and Treatment groups used the exact same language in their messaging and was tested on the article pages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for 21 days. The Treatment (dual ask) led to a 50.9% increase in conversions, but with a 68.90% level of confidence, which means it didn’t fully validate. Despite not reaching the typical 95% confidence threshold, we decided to roll out the Treatment early due to the high urgency of the calendar year-end campaign.
The Control (standard sticky slide-out) generated 10 transactions, while the Treatment (dual ask) resulted in 15 transactions.
We believe the dual ask led to more conversions because it reduced cognitive overload by presenting a lower-effort, initial question. This likely made users more inclined to engage with the content, as the first interaction felt less like a commitment. Additionally, by breaking the ask into two steps, users had more time to process the request, leading to a higher likelihood of converting once they expressed interest in donating. The treatment also benefited from the fact that the second ask for a donation only appeared after the user clicked "Yes"—a step that could have made the donation request feel more natural and less transactional.
We recommend retesting the dual ask in FamilyLife’s next high-urgency campaigns to gather more data and validate its impact on conversions. Additionally, there may be an opportunity to explore this format as a permanent feature, especially if future testing confirms its effectiveness over a longer period and across various campaigns.

experiments.title: How adding a signer’s image in an email footer signature in a high urgency appeal impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including Chitwood’s image in the email footer for CYE Email 12 will achieve an increase in clicks because the image will enhance the personal connection between the organization and the reader and humanize the message. because  the image will enhance the personal connection between the organization and the reader and humanize the message.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2025
In an effort to drive higher email engagement during IMB’s CYE campaign, we conducted a test incorporating an image of Paul Chitwood, IMB’s President, in the email sign-off. The hypothesis was that featuring Chitwood’s image would enhance the personal connection with readers, allowing them to associate a face with the name and fostering a stronger sense of trust and rapport. This, in turn, was expected to increase the likelihood of readers making a donation.
We implemented this test in Email 12 of the CYE campaign, with 50% of the traffic receiving the version featuring Chitwood’s image to assess its impact on engagement and conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that including Paul Chitwood's image in the email footer for CYE Email 12 did not result in the expected increase in clicks. In fact, the treatment group experienced a 3.8% decrease in Clicks for All Traffic compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 64.7%, which is below the recommended threshold of 95%.
Based on these results, it is clear that the impact of including Chitwood's image on email engagement is inconclusive. Therefore, it is not advisable to make any definitive conclusions or decisions based on this experiment alone. In future experimentation, it would be important to conduct further tests with a larger sample size and ensure that the results reach a higher level of statistical confidence before implementing any changes to the email strategy.
Furthermore, we saw a 24.5% decrease in transactions at a 61.4% LOC. While this metric did not reach 95% validity, it does suggest that the presence of Chitwood's image reduced the motivation to give once those who clicked landed on the donation page.
Finally, it may be beneficial to explore other ways to enhance personal connections and trust with readers, such as through compelling storytelling, personalized content, or highlighting the impact of donations. By continuously testing and refining different strategies, IMB can better understand what resonates with their audience and optimize their email campaigns for improved engagement and conversions.

experiments.title: How highlighting the impact of a gift impacts click rate in a high urgency appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting the impact of the donor’s gift for Email 5 recipients will achieve an increase in the click through rate because it allows the donor to connect their action directly to meaningful outcomes.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2025
For The Navigators CYE campaign, we tested into highlighting the impact of a donor’s gift within the email copy.
We tested this concept on Email 5 with 50% of email recipient’s getting the treatment version.
Our hypothesis was that doing so would connect their action (the gift) directly to meaningful outcomes, such as discipling young believers and strengthening faith communities. We thought the emotional appeal would be likely to resonate with recipients, inspiring a sense of purpose and urgency to give, compared to the practical framing of simply checking off a to-do list.
With this change, we expected to see an increase in the click through rate and as a result an increase in donor conversion metrics.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that highlighting the impact of the donor's gift in the email copy for The Navigators' CYE campaign did not result in the expected increase in click-through rate. In fact, the treatment version saw a 10.7% decrease in clicks compared to the control version at a 59.6% LOC.

It is important to note that the results of this experiment are not statistically significant, as the confidence level is below 95%. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision-making.

Furthermore, we wanted to know whether this language produced more motivated clicks. The control produced 25 donations out of the 107 clicks and the treatment produced 19 donations out of the 115 clicks. Therefore, the treatment resulted in a 29.3% decrease in transactions at a 79.8% LOC. While again, these results are not valid, they do suggest that this type of language reduces the motivation to give for The Navigators audience.

In future experimentation, it is crucial to continue testing different messaging and approaches to determine what resonates best with donors. It may be worth exploring a combination of emotional and practical appeals to see what drives engagement and donations. Additionally, conducting experiments with a larger sample size and ensuring a statistically significant confidence level will provide more reliable results to inform future campaign strategies.

experiments.title: How gift array options in a high urgency sticky bar impact donor conversion metrics
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding the gift array to IMB’s CYE sticky bar for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because the gift array minimizes the number of steps that need to take place on the actual donation page therefore minimizing friction and decision making resulting in more gifts.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2025
In an effort to boost IMB’s conversion rate from their CYE Sticky Bar, we opted to test into including a gift array on the sticky bar.
The gift array options were fully functional, and when clicked would auto fill the donation page with the applicable amount selected. We had three gift array options available on the sticky bar: $25, $75, and $250.
This test ran from Dec. 11 – Dec. 27th, 2024 with 50% of the traffic seeing the treatment version (gift array).
We hypothesized that adding the gift array would reduce friction on the donation page by minimizing the steps needed to complete a donation, ultimately resulting in more gifts.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that the addition of a gift array on the CYE sticky bar for homepage visitors did not result in an increase in donor conversions for IMB. In fact, the treatment version of the sticky bar with the gift array saw a 23.4% decrease in donations compared to the control group.
Despite the decrease in donations, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence was only at 66.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%. This means that the results of this experiment are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In terms of overall revenue, the control produced $15,686 and the treatment produced $10,930. Resulting in a 30.3% decrease in revenue at a 74.8% LOC.
We also looked further into the gift array amounts to see if one greatly outperformed the others.
$25 Button5 gifts$120 in Revenue$75 Button2 gifts$200 in Revenue$250 Button5 gifts$1,500 in Revenue
The remaining gifts came through the "More options" button that did not preselect a gift amount in the form meaning about half of users chose the "more options" button.
While these results are inconclusive, they do suggest that iMB's audience may react more positively to a standard sticky bar rather the gift array options.
In future experimentation, it would be important to conduct further testing with a larger sample size and potentially tweak the gift array options or placement on the sticky bar to see if different configurations could yield more positive results. It is also crucial to ensure that statistical significance is reached before making any conclusions or implementing changes based on A/B test results.

experiments.title: How including compelling images in a high urgency appeal impacts clicks
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including compelling images in a high urgency appeal for CYE Email 14 will achieve an increase in clicks because the presence of the images allows the reader to visually connect withe the mission of the IMB.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/06/2025
To increase email click rates during IMB’s CYE campaign, we tested the impact of adding compelling images to Email 14. The treatment was presented to 50% of the recipients of this email.
Our hypothesis was that incorporating these visuals would help readers establish a stronger emotional connection with IMB’s mission, thereby increasing the likelihood of clicking the donation link.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that including compelling images in high urgency appeal emails may not necessarily lead to an increase in clicks for donation links. The treatment group, which received emails with images, actually saw a 2.9% decrease in clicks compared to the control group. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 51.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 95%.

Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making. In future experimentation, it would be important to thoroughly analyze the data and ensure that the findings are statistically significant before drawing any conclusions. It may also be beneficial to test different types of images or messaging to see what truly resonates with recipients and drives them to click on donation links.

experiments.title: How campaign-related copy on the Tribute donation page impacts revenue during a holiday appeal
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that campaign-related copy for users on the Tribute donation page will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the value prop is expanded upon, greater urgency is tied to the campaign, and the donor has a sense of how their gift fits into the December goals.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/17/2024 - 12/18/2024
During CaringBridge’s holiday appeal, the Tribute Donation page largely remains the same. Aside from brief mentions of the match, the copy is stripped down, short, and doesn’t tie into the larger goals of the organization – instead focusing more on a donor’s gift to the individual. We believed that by using the campaign donation page on Tribute, tying to the larger December goals and reinforcing the match more, it would increase average gift and donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing campaign-related copy on the Tribute donation page led to a 3.6% increase in revenue for all traffic as well as a slight bump in donations overall. While this result is promising, the statistical level of confidence was only 70.7%, below the 85% threshold for validity.

However, it is an encouraging first step to continue testing on the Tribute donation page, which historically, has been treated very separate from the rest of CaringBridge's campaign and goals – because users are there for a specific user. To have this broadened and more general appeal copy not result in a decrease, but also perhaps have a slight positive increase, could open up door for continued testing.

experiments.title: How using a countdown clock on a donation page in the final days of a high urgency campaign affects donor conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a countdown clock to the donation page will increase the donation conversion rate because it creates urgency by highlighting the time remaining until the deadline.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/27/2024 - 01/01/2025
The American Cornerstone Institute ran a high-urgency campaign for the final month of the year. Within this campaign, they wanted to test the impact of including a countdown clock on the donation for the final 4 days of the campaign.
They conducted an A/B test, split 50/50, with two versions of the donation page: the control version without a countdown clock and the treatment version with a countdown clock. Their hypothesis was that the countdown clock would create a sense of urgency, encouraging more users to make a donation before the deadline (When the countdown clock ran out).
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the inclusion of a countdown clock on the donation page did not have the intended impact of increasing donation conversion rates. In fact, the treatment group, which had the countdown clock, experienced a 6.6% decrease in transactions compared to the control group.

It is crucial to note that the statistical level of confidence for this result was only 59.0%, which is below the standard threshold of 85% for results to be considered valid. Therefore, these results should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is essential to consider the statistical significance of the results before drawing any conclusions. Additionally, it may be beneficial to test the effectiveness of different strategies to create urgency or incentivize donations, such as limited-time offers or matching donation challenges, to see what resonates best with donors. Conducting further A/B tests with a larger sample size and varying durations may provide more conclusive insights for future fundraising campaigns.

experiments.title: How adding a donor goal thermometer to a year-end donation form impacts donor conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor thermometer for year-end donors will achieve a change in donor conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/05/2024 - 12/30/2024
Avila is an organization that aims to draw Christians worldwide into deeper union with Christ through mystagogically oriented spiritual education and formation.
For their year-end campaign, we wanted to test whether using a donor goal thermometer on the year-end donation form will impact donor conversions. In order to hit the $320,000 goal, Avila needed 500 donors to step up and make a donation. In this experiment, the control had no thermometer; the treatment automatically updated based on gifts to that page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a donor thermometer for year-end donors resulted in a 19.0% increase in Transactions for All Traffic. While the treatment group did outperform the control group, the statistical level of confidence was only 79.0%, falling below the typical threshold of 95% for valid results.

Moving forward, it is important to note that these results should not be used for future decision-making as they do not meet the standard threshold for statistical significance. However, the experiment does provide some insights into the potential impact of using a donor thermometer on donor conversions.

For future experimentation, it may be beneficial to continue testing the use of a donor thermometer but with adjustments to the implementation or design in order to potentially achieve statistically significant results. Additionally, considering a longer duration for the experiment and a larger sample size may also help in obtaining more reliable and conclusive results.

experiments.title: How a humanized slideup impacts donor conversion rate during a high-urgency match
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a humanized slideup for users on CaringBridge profiles will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because it humanizes the ask, and the letter-style sign-off presents the match opportunity from a person.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/02/2024 - 12/03/2024
For CaringBridge’s Giving Tuesday match, we hypothesized that an iteration of the slide-up where the ask is presented by a person, with a letter-style sign-off would increase donor conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that introducing a humanized slideup with a letter-style sign-off on CaringBridge profiles resulted in a 25.0% increase in non-tribute donations. While this result is promising, the statistical level of confidence is only 51.2%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85% for statistical significance.
Ultimately, this experiment was ended early and should be re-tested before these results can be used for decision making

experiments.title: How a premium offer chat CTA on podcast pages impacts donor conversion during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that utilizing a premium offer chat widget with a conversational tone and engaging design will increase donor conversion rates during the high urgency campaign by enhancing the visibility of the call-to-action and creating a more personal appeal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 08/01/2024 - 08/22/2024
FamilyLife is a nonprofit organization committed to strengthening families through various resources, including their engaging podcast series. As part of their Fiscal Year End (FYE) campaign, we are testing two different messaging approaches on the podcast pages to encourage donations.
Historically, the “Dear Listener” message has proven successful in driving donations. However, we want to explore whether a more dynamic approach, utilizing a chat widget, might enhance engagement and boost donor conversion rates. This experiment follows a successful test conducted in July with the “Seeing Beautiful Again” campaign, which achieved a 187.9% increase in donor conversion. We aim to see if a similar impact can be replicated with a different premium during the FYE campaign.
We believe that the conversational and eye-catching design of the chat message widget will increase donor conversion during the FYE campaign by improving the visibility of the call-to-action (CTA) and humanizing the appeal.
The control version of the podcast page featured the traditional “Dear Listener” premium message, which included an image of the premium offer, Unoffendable. The treatment version showcased the premium message chat widget, featuring an image of a FamilyLife member. Most of the language remained consistent between the two versions, and traffic was split 50/50 to measure differences in donation conversion rates accurately.
We also tested this on their Article Pages.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment results indicated that the treatment group featuring the premium offer chat widget achieved a conversion rate of 0.39%, compared to the control group’s rate of 0.32%. This represents a relative difference of 24%. However, with a level of confidence of only 43.6%, these results were not statistically significant.

Despite the positive trend in donor conversion rates observed with the chat widget, the lack of robust statistical significance suggests that more data is needed to draw reliable conclusions. The test ran for 21 days, which may have limited the ability to fully assess the widget's effectiveness.

Next Steps:
Extended Testing Duration: Run the experiment for a longer period to gather more data, especially during different times of the month or year to account for seasonal variations in donor behavior.
 	A/B Test Variations of the Chat Widget: Experiment with different designs and messaging variations for the chat widget to identify elements that resonate better with the audience. Consider testing language, tone, and imagery to enhance engagement.
 	Analyze User Interaction: Utilize analytics tools to monitor how users interact with the chat widget (e.g., clicks, time spent, engagement) to identify any drop-off points or issues affecting conversion rates.

experiments.title: How additional cultivation and text appeals amid a High Urgency campaign impact donor giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that sending cultivation and appeals in an additional channel amid a high urgency campaign for Human Coalition’s housefile will achieve an increase in donor giving and revenue because it diversifies the communication and creates an additional reminder/opportunity to connect with the donor — beyond just their inbox. because  it diversifies the communication and creates an additional reminder/opportunity to connect with the donor — beyond just their inbox.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 10/08/2024
As Human Coalition expands into multi-channel efforts with Facebook retargeting and text appeals, we hypothesized that including additional touchpoints in text for the Back to School high urgency campaign will improve donor conversion rate and revenue. Our goal was to observe the impact on giving, not just from the text directly, but whether these additional touchpoints made donors more likely to give or more generous in their gift amount in other channels throughout the campaign.
The additional touchpoints sent by text were a launch day appeal, a cultivation piece with video, and a final day appeal – three texts in total.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Key Learnings from the Experiment:
1. The treatment group, which included additional touchpoints through Facebook retargeting and text appeals, saw a significant increase in revenue for all traffic by 35.0%. This suggests that incorporating multiple channels for communication can have a positive impact on donor giving during high urgency campaigns.
2. Despite the increase in revenue, the statistical level of confidence was only at 75.9%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. This means that the results are not considered statistically significant and should not be relied upon for future decision making.
The key lesson learned is that incorporating multiple touchpoints in fundraising campaigns may have a positive impact on donor giving, but it is essential to gather more data and ensure statistically significant results before implementing any changes based on experiment outcomes.
Ultimately, a helpful takeaway is that receiving an additional communication in text does not negatively impact Human Coalition's audience, it doesn't appear to be an annoyance or a distraction. Rather, it would be worthwhile testing additional pieces in text that are "bonus" to the campaign. For example, rather than resending the video already included in email. What if readers were sent a new cultivation or video piece that they hadn't received.

experiments.title: How using a video on a high urgency campaign donation page impacts giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using the transcript of a video on a donation page for email recipients will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/17/2022
Reasons to Believe launched their fiscal year end campaign and in their launch email they were leading their email recipients to a donation page with a video. Past experiments with other organizations have shown that a video on a donation page can actually add friction to the giving experience. The reason being that many people are unlikely to watch through a whole video and they’re more likely to read through some copy.
For this email we sent half of their list to a donation page with the video (the control) and the other half of the list to a donation page with a transcript of the content of the video (the treatment). Our hypothesis is that more people would convert on the donation page with the transcript of the video.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment resulted in a 56% decrease in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 78%. Something to note is that on the email itself, RTB used different copy on the CTA button. The control had "Watch my video" and the treatment had "Read my note." When looking at the click thru rate between each button we saw a valid 28% decrease in clicks with a level of confidence of 99%. In a sense, 2 variables were tested in this experiment: the call to action and the donation page itself.

It's likely that RTB's audience is conditioned to watch videos for their content and throughout their high urgency campaigns. However it seems as more traffic was pushed to the video donation page because of the different CTA. Watching a video may sound more appealing than to read something.

It's recommended that this is retested in a channel that gets more traffic and that has the same CTA button, like a sticky bar. This will isolate one variable and get us more definitive results.

experiments.title: How adding social proof to a high-urgency donation page through a slide out affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding social proof through a slideout for Eagle Lake 2 donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion metrics because seeing that others are actively engaging and donating will create a sense of urgency and validation, encouraging more people to contribute.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 07/11/2024 - 07/26/2024
Every year, The Navigators run a high-urgency campaign called Eagle Lake 2 during June and July to raise money for their Eagle Lake camps.
We have seen success running social proof-based tests for other clients, so we were anxious to run it for The Navigators.
For this test, the treatment version displayed a pop-out of a statement that read “FirstName from State just donated $GiftAmount”
All the information displayed was data pulled from donors to the campaign. It is also worth noting, that these contacts were pulled manually due to our lack of ability to install a pixel to track conversions on their donation pages.
Our hypothesis was that the pop-out would increase the amount of social proof experienced by the visitor on the page which would then lead to an increase in our donor conversion metrics by increasing a sense of urgency and need to donate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding social proof in the form of a slideout for the Eagle Lake 2 donation page did not lead to an increase in donor conversion metrics. In fact, the treatment version saw a decrease in transactions for all traffic compared to the control version.

It is important to note that the results of this experiment are not statistically significant, with a confidence level of only 34.4%. Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid, and it would be unwise to make any decisions based on them. However, we can assume that the addition of social proof may have a negative consequence in terms of donor acquisition metrics. Therefore, we will need to test this concept again to come to a clear conclusion.

In future experimentation, it is essential to ensure that the sample size is sufficient to achieve a statistically significant result. Additionally, it might be beneficial to conduct qualitative research to understand why the  slideout did not have the desired effect. This could provide valuable insights that can be used to design more effective experiments in the future.

experiments.title: How yes/no “autonomy” language on a sticky bar impacts conversion rate during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a yes and no option for homepage visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the autonomy to make a micro-decision allows the visitor to feel in control before being asked for the gift. because the autonomy to make a micro-decision allows the visitor to feel in control before being asked for the gift.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/19/2024 - 04/27/2024
For The Navigators April Eagle Lake High Urgency campaign, we wanted to test using two buttons, both a yes and no option, on their homepage pushdown/sticky bar.
We hypothesized that providing the visitor with a micro-decision would lead to an increase in the donor conversion rate.
The control features the standard single call-to-action. Whereas, the treatment offers the call-to-action to donate as well as a “No, I’m not interested option.”
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that providing visitors with a yes and no option on the homepage pushdown/sticky bar can lead to an increase in the donor conversion rate. The treatment version, which included both options, saw a 19.5% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 23.2%, which is below the accepted threshold of 95%.

While these results are not considered valid, it does suggest that The Navigators audience responds well to having autonomy and micro-decisions in the donation process.

In future experimentation, we should continue to explore the use of two-button options on our homepage pushdown/sticky bar to gauge how it impacts donor conversion rates. It's important to conduct further tests with a larger sample size to ensure statistically significant results that can be used for future decision-making. Additionally, we should consider refining the language used in both the control and treatment versions to see how messaging impacts visitor responses. Ultimately, the key takeaway is that providing visitors with the autonomy to make a micro-decision can positively influence their willingness to donate.

experiments.title: How Adding Urgency to a Dear Reader Affects Conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a donor countdown for the dear reader will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because adding the extra urgency on the page will also increase desire to give.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/17/2024
Today in the Word is a ministry of Moody bible Institute. Their mail mission is to spread the gospel through daily devotionals. Because they are a devotional ministry the majority of the contents on its website are article pages with previous daily devotionals.
We only ran this test on the article pages to ensure that we received enough traffic to be able to perform the test. Their audiences have seen multiple dear readers before, and their response has been overwhelmingly positive. For this experiment, we wanted to see if increasing the urgency on the dear reader would increase conversion rate.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The A/B experiment conducted on the article pages of Moody Radio's Today in the Word website showed that adding a donor countdown for the dear reader resulted in a 1.0% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 2.8%, which is below the recommended threshold of 85%. Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid for future decision making.

Key learnings from this experiment include:

1. Adding urgency to the dear reader message may have a positive impact on donor conversion rates.

2. A 1.0% increase in donations is promising, but the results need to be statistically significant to draw definitive conclusions.
