In the future, we should consider testing this approach again to see the validity of the test can increase.

experiments.title: Will a countdown clock on the homepage increase urgency and lead to higher donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a timer for the homepage sticky bar will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because seeing a deadline of when the campaign ends increases urgency more than a donor countdown.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/18/2024
Moody Bible Institute runs a FYE campaign each year that drives a ton of traffic to their website.
For this campaign, we wanted to see if we could test into which tactic works better to increase urgency: donor countdown or timer.
Below are the results that we found.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding a timer for the homepage sticky bar led to a 47.0% increase in donations for all traffic. Despite the positive outcome, the statistical confidence level was only 68.9%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results should not be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that a higher level of statistical confidence is achieved before implementing any changes based on the results. It may be beneficial to run the experiment again with a larger sample size or for a longer duration to obtain more reliable and conclusive results. Additionally, it is essential to continue testing different tactics and elements to optimize donation conversion rates effectively.

experiments.title: Will Adding a Give Monthly Option to the Site Navigation Help with Monthly Donor Conversion During Calendar Year End?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a give monthly button to the main navigation on the website for people who land on the homepage will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because there will be less friction in the process.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/21/2023 - 12/06/2023
For Giving Tuesday we wanted to test having monthly ask set up as a mini campaign within CYE.
To make it easier to make a monthly gift we wanted to test adding a monthly giving button in the main navigation next to the original give button.
experiments.keyLearnings: In conclusion for this experiment, adding a give monthly button decreased donor conversion by 34% with a 78% LOC. This could be due to several reasons. The button copy itself could use further testing between give and donate. Another possibility is people saw the monthly and that was too big of a commitment so instead of giving monthly, they gave a one-time gift.

experiments.title: How using a year-end specific sticky bar on acquisition pages impacted conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a sticky bar with year-end messaging for content offer downloaders will achieve a change in conversion rates because the urgency behind year-end giving deadlines.. because  the urgency behind year-end giving deadlines..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/20/2023 - 01/01/2024
The Hoover Institution is the public policy think-tank for Stanford University.
During the crucial year-end fundraising season, we wanted to see whether we could impact conversion rates on content offer instant donation pages by using a year-end specific sticky bar.
We ran this on all content offer instant donation pages during the last 10 days of the year, as that is when Hoover sees the most revenue come in online in December.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a sticky bar with year-end messaging for content offer downloaders did not achieve the desired change in conversion rates. The treatment group actually saw a decrease in transactions by 12.2% compared to the control group.
It is important to note that the statistical level of confidence for these results is only 79.8%, which is below the generally accepted minimum threshold of 85%. This means that the results are not considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
While transaction conversion rates were lower for the treatment group, when looking at the impact on revenue, we see that the treatment version had a 104% higher average gift. Given the time of year this experiment was run, this is most likely due to a larger gift skewing results, but it is still interesting that the sticky bar with year-end messaging motivated that donor to make that large gift.

experiments.title: How adding an image of a thank-you gift impacted donations during a one-day fund drive.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding an image of a thank-you gift for fund drive donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2024 - 02/08/2024
WUNC is one of North Carolina’s largest NPR stations. Most of their fundraising online comes through fund drives, which they have every other month.
February’s fund drive features some great thank you gifts, which we’ve seen through past testing help motivate donors to give to support WUNC. On a specific day during this drive, WUNC offered a box of chocolates and a WUNC-branded mug as the thank you gift, delivered in time for Valentine’s Day. This was a one-day only offer, so we wanted to test whether adding an image of the gift on the donation page made a difference in conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding an image of a thank-you gift for fund drive donors resulted in a 13.6% increase in transactions for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 33.2%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision-making.

In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that a sufficient sample size is used to achieve a higher level of statistical confidence. Additionally, it may be beneficial to run the experiment for a longer period of time to gather more data and improve the reliability of the results. This will help in making more informed decisions based on the outcomes of the experiments conducted.

experiments.title: How Urgency Language impacts clicks on a website popup during a year-end campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding urgency to a website popup during the year-end campaign will increase clicks by making the appeal more timely.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/27/2024
For Buckner International’s Year-End campaign, we wanted to test the impact of urgency language on a popup we were running to drive website visitors to the year-end donation page. Both popups would feature an image of a mother and child in need and invite users to make a year-end gift. The difference would be in the urgency of the appeal. The control would ask if the reader could “support a family in need this season?” The treatment stated, “Time’s running out to support a family in crisis.”
We hypothesized that the added urgency of the treatment would increase engagement by making the appeal more timely. To ensure that people didn’t see the popup every visit, we set it up to show once every two visits.
experiments.keyLearnings: Unfortunately, website traffic was far too low during the campaign to validate this experiment. We had only 174 sessions that saw the popups and a total of 4 clicks. While both had a healthy CTR, there wasn't enough traffic. Future tests will need to either fire every visit and/or target higher-traffic pages on the site to be able to validate.

experiments.title: How adding a countdown timer to a slideout during a one-day fund drive impacted conversion rates.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a countdown timer for fund drive donors will achieve a change in donation conversion rates because of the urgency associated with the offer..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/07/2024 - 02/07/2024
WUNC is one of North Carolina’s largest NPR stations. Most of their fundraising online comes through fund drives, which they have every other month.
February’s fund drive features some great thank you gifts, which we’ve seen through past testing help motivate donors to give to support WUNC. On a specific day during this drive, WUNC offered a box of chocolates and a WUNC-branded mug as the thank you gift, delivered in time for Valentine’s Day.
This was a one-day only offer, so we wanted to test whether adding a countdown timer to a slideout featuring the image of the thank you gift would have an impact on donation conversion rates.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a countdown timer to a slideout featuring the image of the thank you gift (a box of chocolates and a WUNC-branded mug) resulted in a 13.6% increase in donations for all traffic. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 35.5%, which is below the generally accepted threshold of 85%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid, and should not be used for future decision making.

In future experimentation, it would be important to consider testing the effectiveness of countdown timers in a more controlled setting, or perhaps with a larger sample size, to determine whether they do have a significant impact on donation conversion rates. It is also important to ensure that the language used in the treatment version aligns with the branding and tone of WUNC to maintain consistency and authenticity in communication with donors.

experiments.title: How adding a “Support Honest Journalism” Sticky Button to TDS during EOY
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding a “Support Honest Journalism” sticky button for new visitors to The Daily Signal will achieve a lift in conversion rate and average gift because media consumers are hungry for trustworthy news and will support a media outlet who represents independent and reliable journalism.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/29/2024
We tested three different sticky buttons on The Daily Signal during EOY 2023. Two button’s were direct solicitations and one was a content offer for our Guide to the Constitution. The first button asked the visitor to “Support Honest Journalism” and the other asked them to “Donate”. Part of the experiment was to determine whether a more defined CTA like supporting honest journalism would create a lift in revenue over a tried and true content offer and a regular donate button. The donate button and Guide to the Constitution buttons resulted in a lower conversion rate and average gift, but the support honest journalism button saw a significant lift of lift in avg. gift and a slight lift in conversion rate. Looking at subsegments we identified that the primary lift came from New Visitors to the site which had an 18.77% increase in conversion rate, and a 98% increase in avg. gift. Returning visitors for the journalism button saw a net -58% drop in revenue.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are as follows:

1. A more defined call to action, such as "Support Honest Journalism," can result in a lift in both conversion rate and average gift.

2. The "Support Honest Journalism" button specifically resonated with new visitors to the site, leading to a significant increase in conversion rate and average gift.

3. However, returning visitors did not respond well to the "Support Honest Journalism" button, resulting in a net loss in revenue.

4. The overall increase in revenue for all traffic was substantial but did not reach a statistically significant level of confidence.

Based on these learnings, it is clear that the "Support Honest Journalism" button has potential as a strong call to action for new visitors. However, it is important to consider the impact on returning visitors, as the negative response from this segment had a significant impact on the overall revenue.

For future experimentation, it would be beneficial to further explore how to tailor the messaging and call to action for different visitor segments. Additionally, it is crucial to conduct experiments with a larger sample size to ensure statistical significance and validate the results before making any impactful decisions.

experiments.title: How does adding a more personalized introduction affect email revenue on Giving Tuesday?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a personalized introduction with rhetorical questions framed from personal experience relating to the project or topic being discussed for our email housefile will achieve a lift in revenue and an increased conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 12/15/2023
This test was run to our email housefile on Giving Tuesday 11/28/23 and was our first morning email. We tested a personalized introduction that featured rhetorical questions framed from the donors perspective and text specifically calling out bureaucrats and unlected agencies vs. a control without those additions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The results recorded here reflect cumulative results for members (168K emailed) and nonmembers (790K emailed). Below are results for subsegments.
Member: treatment created a 58% increase in revenue with a 62.3% level of confidence
 	Increased conversion rate by 13.7% with a 64.1% level of confidence
 	Nonmembers: treatment created a 8% increase in revenue with a 52.3% level of confidence
 	Increase conversion rate by 24.8% with a 58.1% level of confidence
 	LC: Treatment created a 8.2% increase in revenue with a 52.2% level of confidence
 	No statistical difference between conversion rates
 	Lapsed Members: Treatment crated a -61.1% decrease in revenue with a 71.8% level of confidence
 	Decreased conversion rate by -26% with a 33.6% level of confidence

experiments.title: How a simplified message that focuses only on impact in the final email of a high urgency campaign affects conversions for members
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that simplifying the message and only focusing on the impact in the final email of the campaign will produce a higher conversion rate because the treatment email presents a more straightforward and concise message, which can be easier for recipients to understand quickly.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 02/06/2024
The Heritage Foundation is running thier annual Kickoff Campaign to acquire members. You can become a member by giving a gift of any size, one-time or recurring. This experiment was ran for members to Heritage asking them to renew their membership. For this test, we wanted to see how a concise message that only focuses on their impact in the final email of the campaign would affect conversion rates. We believe a clear and simple call-to-action may lead to a better response as it reduces cognitive load for the reader.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment email, which focused solely on the impact of The Heritage Foundation, did not have a significant impact on conversion rates compared to the control email.

The treatment email saw a .35% decrease in clicks for all traffic, but the statistical level of confidence is only 3.4%. This means that the results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.

Although there was no difference in clicks, we did see an 8.9% decrease in donor conversion rate with a 77.4% level of confidence. This tells us that using concise copy for the members-only segment does not motivate them as much to renew their membership when compared to other segments like lapsed donors and non-members.

experiments.title: How Urgency Language and a Countdown Impacts Conversion Rate on a CYE Pop Up
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using urgency language and countdown for users on the homepage will achieve an increase in conversion rate because it underscores the limited window of time available for the donor to make their gift..
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/04/2024
For RTB’s CYE Pop-Up, we hypothesized that an added line of copy for urgency and a countdown time would increase donor conversion rate. The treatment iteration added “We urgently need your help.” to the copy as well as the dynamic countdown. Aside from these changes, the body copy remained the same.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding urgency language and a countdown to the homepage did not increase donor conversion rate, however the level of confidence was far below the amount needed for validity.
It should be noted that originally this popup was supposed to a include an end-of-year deadline for a matched gift opportunity. This was met shortly before the popup launched and the line about the match had to be removed. With this tangible deadline missing, it does prompt the question "What is urgent?" Yes, the mission stated in the copy is undoubtedly important, but does having a countdown on that mission of just 7 days end up seeming overreactive to the user?
In future experiments, it would be beneficial to run a countdown for a longer period and in a context that still has a clear deadline for the user to correlate alongside the timer.

experiments.title: How the placement of a call-to-action impacts donation conversions
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that placing the CTA up higher for website visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because  the CTA is more easily visible.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/15/2023 - 12/31/2023
America Needs Fatima has previously tested a slide out vs. a dear reader to feature different content offers. For this test, we wanted to test the slide out method for a direct appeal ask vs. a traditional sticky bar to see if the appeal placement impacted overall donation conversions.
For this test, both web elements contained the same copy and button CTA.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that placing the CTA higher on the website, using a slide out method, resulted in a 10.8% increase in one-time donations compared to a traditional sticky bar. However, the statistical level of confidence for this result is only 64.5%, which is below the accepted threshold of 85%. Therefore, these results cannot be considered valid and should not be used for future decision making.
In future experimentation, it is important to ensure that the statistical level of confidence is above 85% to have valid and reliable results. Additionally, it may be worth exploring other elements or factors that could impact donor conversions, such as the design or color scheme of the CTA button, the messaging used in the appeal, or the timing of the slide out or sticky bar. By testing these different elements and analyzing the results with a higher level of statistical confidence, the organization can make more informed decisions on how to optimize the website for increased donations.

experiments.title: How using a more concise donation page in a high urgency campaign affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding clarity and being more concise for potential donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate because repeated information on a donation page is added friction to the donation process which can deter people from giving.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 11/21/2023
This ministry organization ran a high-urgency campaign to their full housefile with the goal of acquiring more recurring donors. The original donation page re-explained most points that the emails had already described, so we asked ourselves the questions:
Are subscribers still needing this much information by the time they get to the donation page? Or are they just ready to give, and we are getting in their way by proposing more hypothetical questions?
That’s why we proposed a second, short, to-the-point donation page that focuses on clarity and is more concise.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that a more concise and clear donation page may lead to a higher donor conversion rate. The treatment, which focused on eliminating repeated information and reducing friction in the donation process, saw a 0.9% increase in donations. However, the statistical level of confidence was only 4.8%, which is below the recommended 85%.
In future experimentation, it is important to continue exploring the impact of clarity and conciseness on donor conversion rates. The results of this experiment suggest that potential donors may prefer a streamlined and simplified donation process without excessive information repetition. Further testing with larger sample sizes and higher statistical power could help validate these findings and provide more reliable insights for decision making.
Additionally, it would also be valuable to explore other potential factors that could impact donor conversion rates, such as the design and layout of the donation page, messaging and storytelling techniques, and ease of use. By combining multiple variables and testing different combinations, a deeper understanding of the most effective strategies for acquiring recurring donors can be gained.

experiments.title: How adding an urgent countdown clock pop-up to a page impacted conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that by adding an urgent countdown pop-up for potential donors will achieve an increase in conversion rate because the potential donor’s motivation would increase to want to give now rather than wait.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/06/2023 - 07/02/2023
This ministry client supports international prisoners by giving them biblical resources. The page we wanted to test is a secondary page to the main supporting prisoner page. We wanted to add an urgent countdown clock pop-up to this page to drive the conversion rate. This countdown clock included a message of how the potential donor could help be the one to complete the goal of the number of prisoners that are wanted to be supported by the end of the night. The pop-up includes urgency language by proving words like “Urgent”, “time is running out”, “by tonight” to make the potential donor have a higher motivation to give now. The control page was the original secondary page and the treatment page had the urgent pop-up.
This test was run as a 50/50 A/B test.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learnings from this experiment are that adding an urgent countdown pop-up can have a positive impact on donation conversion rates. The treatment group saw a significant increase in donations compared to the control group. However, it is important to note that the statistical level of confidence is below the recommended threshold of 95%. This means that the results of this experiment cannot be considered validated.

In future experimentation, it is recommended to run a larger sample size or run the experiment for a longer duration to increase the statistical power and achieve a higher level of confidence. Additionally, it may also be worth considering testing an urgent pop-up compared to a normal support pop-up to see if the urgent pop-up would still produce higher conversion rates.

experiments.title: How adding a gift array to a home page banner impacts donor conversion during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a gift array to the home page sticky banner will lead to an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/19/2023 - 06/29/2023
This test is a follow-up test to the “How adding a gift array to a home page banner impacts donor conversion” test recorded earlier this year.
We wanted to retest this concept because this topic ended up being an integral part of their Fiscal Year End campaign.
Historically, Care Net has typically run a sticky bar that dynamically populates the number of donors needed across their website during high-urgency campaigns. This year during their fiscal year end campaign, we wanted to test and see how adding a gift array to this would impact donor conversion to have the amount selected happen at this level instead of when they are redirected to the donation page.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a gift array to the home page sticky banner resulted in a decrease in donor conversion. However, the statistical level of confidence for this result is below the threshold of 85%, which means these results cannot be considered valid. But because this was at the end of Care Net's fiscal year, we ended up ending the experiment early and rolling out the control to ensure 100% of the traffic saw the better-performing variant.

Based on this, it is not recommended to use the gift array in the home page sticky banner for future campaigns. Instead, it would be worthwhile to explore other strategies or elements to improve donor conversion. Future experimentation should focus on testing different approaches and measuring their impact on donor conversion to identify the most effective strategies for increasing donations.

experiments.title: How a sticky bar with a daily donor goal countdown impacts donor conversion on a high urgency campaign donation page
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a sticky bar with a daily donor goal countdown on the donation page will increase donor conversion during a high urgency campaign
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/01/2023 - 05/20/2023
During FamilyLife’s Spring Recurring Campaign, they rely on a daily donor countdown in sticky bars and website calls to action to increase urgency and drive traffic to their donation page. We hypothesized that reinforcing this countdown on the page itself would add urgency and increase donor conversion.
The message would indicate how many new monthly donors were still needed before midnight that night.  When the number was reached, it would change to say, “We still need new monthly partners before 11:59pm to be here for you and other families in need.”
We split traffic evenly between both versions of the page and ran it for almost three weeks.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that adding a sticky bar with a daily donor goal countdown on the donation page did not increase donor conversion during the high urgency campaign. The treatment group actually saw a decrease in FamilyLife - Recurring Donations compared to the control group.
While we could not validate this decrease, it was a sharp enough decline in conversion that we ended the experiment early so we wouldn't harm donor conversion during the critical campaign. It is possible that the message was more of a distraction than provided additional urgency to the page.

experiments.title: How adding urgency in a pop up during fiscal year end impacts donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding language that communicates urgency for site visitors  will achieve an increase in donor conversion rate because the time-bound offer will drive donors to give now as opposed to later .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 07/11/2023
During RTB’s high urgency campaign they wanted to drive urgency on the site by implementing a pop up to give during the last few days of the campaign. In order to drive the urgency we developed a treatment that used urgency language to drive fast action among prospective donors.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that implementing language that communicates urgency did not lead to an increase in donor conversion rate for the nonprofit's high urgency campaign. On the contrary, the treatment actually resulted in a decrease in donations. There was a 37% decrease in donor conversion but only with a level of confidence of 59%.
Because this only ran for a few days and only generated 13 donations, it's recommended that this retested in a longer time frame.

experiments.title: How urgency-focused value proposition copy impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that urgency value proposition language for donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donations because visitors will feel a sense of urgency to give and make an impact that they otherwise wouldn’t.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/06/2023 - 06/23/2023
DTS is amid a high-urgency fiscal year-end campaign. As our org is pushing a large number of users to our fiscal year-end donation pages, we want to test into urgency-focused donation page copy.
As part of our FYE campaign, we launched a free content offer where users can sign up to receive the eBook, and once they do, they’re redirected an instant donation page. Our existing donation page deploys standard value proposition messaging through problem/solution copy and reasons to give; however, it does not include important FYE messaging including urgency language, the campaign deadline, campaign goal, or matching gift challenge associated with the campaign. The only urgent element it includes is a countdown clock, but it does not explain what the clock is counting down to.
We want to test urgency-focused messaging that does include these elements and mentions, and that grabs the user’s attention immediately, alerting them of this urgent opportunity to make an impact.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The urgency-focused language did not produce the results that we expected. After running for 17 days, the treatment resulted in a 9.6% decrease in donations with a 40.4% level of confidence. It also produced a 4% decrease in revenue with a 56.7% level of confidence.
The key learning from this experiment is that urgency-focused language does not always lead to more donations. It can provide the urgency, the flash, and the untraditional that could inspire someone to give. Though, in this case, I wonder if the supporting value proposition copy was not as strong as the control. The control includes strong problem/solution copy and highlights specifically how a donor's gift will be used, while the treatment includes more general value proposition copy that never really explains the problem(s) that a donor's gift might help solve and how the gift would be used to solve them.
For a future iteration, it would be wise to blend the value proposition copy from the control with the urgency elements used in the treatment. This could be the formula for increase donor conversion and revenue.

experiments.title: How an urgent message presented as a sticky bar impacted donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that highlighting relevancy and urgency for instant donation page visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion because we increased the motivation to give by explaining in a relevant way why they should give their money to accomplish something specific to an area they care about.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/21/2023 - 04/23/2023
On a post-survey instant donation page, we wondered if we could increase the relevance and urgency of the donation page ask by calling the reader’s attention through a sticky bar at the top of the page. The first treatment was a general urgency around the need to fight against inflation due to a recent worsening of the problem. The second treatment took a more specific approach by highlighting current events in the newscycle that increased the need for action in this policy area. We split traffic 3 ways and ran the test for one month.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The experiment aimed to increase donor conversion by highlighting relevance and urgency on the instant donation page through a sticky bar. The results showed an 11.6% increase in donations for all traffic, but with a low statistical level of confidence of 36.2%, indicating that the results cannot be considered valid. However, the experiment did show significant increases in donations for Paid Social visitors, Male visitors, 45-54 visitors, and visitors over the age of 55.

The key learnings from this experiment are that highlighting relevance and urgency can increase donations, but there is a need for further experimentation to validate the results before implementing changes. Additionally, it is important to segment visitors based on demographics and traffic source to understand which groups respond positively to the treatment. Future experimentation could focus on refining the message and delivery of the relevance and urgency to boost donations across all visitor segments.

experiments.title: How adding a high-urgency sticky bar to a recurring donor campaign page affects recurring donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a high-urgency sticky bar on the donation page of a recurring donor campaign will achieve higher recurring donation rates by defining a tangible goal and more clearly communicating the problem being solved.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/10/2023 - 03/31/2023
During a recurring donor campaign, we wanted to know how adding a high-urgency sticky bar to the donation page would affect recurring donation rates. The sticky bar combined high-urgency language with a dynamic countdown which automatically refreshed to display how many donors were still needed to reach the campaign goal. We set this experiment up as an A/B test with traffic split roughly 50/50 between the control and the treatment.
experiments.keyLearnings: After running this experiment for 21 days, we observed a 5.6% increase in recurring donations produced by the treatment but with a low statistical level of confidence (36.1%). Despite the modest lift in recurring donations, we also observed that the treatment produced a sizable 21% decrease in overall revenue (with a 75.5% level of confidence), indicating that by putting more emphasis on the number of donations, the treatment may have unexpectedly had a negative impact on the amount that donors were willing to give. It may therefore be worth testing this tactic against a monetary goal in the future.

experiments.title: How stating the “deadline” in a subject line for a high urgency campaign impacted open rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that giving clarity to an email subject line amid a high urgency campaign for email recipients will achieve an increase in email open rate because recipients will know their exact window to take action.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/30/2023 - 04/13/2023
The Illinois Policy Institute is amid a high urgency fundraising campaign. The campaign deadline is tomorrow, and we want to test how making that clear in the subject line of today’s email impacts open rate.
The control variant (subject line A) is “Urgent reminder” while the treatment variant (subject line B) is “Deadline tomorrow”. We’ve split these variants to where 50% of our recipients will experience one subject line and 50% will experience the other.
We want to find out how our recipients respond to each subject line in terms of choosing to open the email. We expect “Deadline tomorrow” to produce the most opens because it clearly communicates the deadline to recipients and the fact that the current opportunity will expire after tomorrow.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The key learning from this experiment is that the treatment variant, which had the subject line "Deadline tomorrow", did not perform better than the control variant, which had the subject line "Urgent reminder". The treatment variant saw a 4.8% decrease in Opens for All Traffic with a 70.5% statistical level of confidence, indicating that these results cannot be considered valid. Therefore, we cannot conclude that giving clarity to the subject line in a high urgency fundraising campaign leads to an increase in email open rate.
It's also worth noting that the treatment subject line produced a 32.3% drop in click rate with an 82.2% level of confidence.
The "Deadline tomorrow" subject line may have been less effective because recipients didn't know what the deadline was for. Instead of opening the email to find out, they may have decided it wasn't worth their time to find out. The "Urgent reminder" SL on the other hand, tells the recipient that they've been told about the opportunity before and they may then feel more inclined to open the email see what they need to be "reminded" about.
From a psychological standpoint, there is also the possibility that the word "deadline" is a turn off to recipients. Humans often view deadlines in a negative context because they're used to hitting deadlines at work or with tasks in life. They may not take kindly to the mention of another deadline.
This angle is worth testing again for a future high urgency email to get more data behind it.

experiments.title: How implementing a goal thermometer on a high urgency campaign donation page impacted donations
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that implementing a goal thermometer for donation page visitors amidst a high urgency campaign will achieve an increase in donations because page visitors will be motivated to help contribute to and reach the end goal of the campaign.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/22/2023 - 03/31/2023
As part of a March high urgency campaign for the Illinois Policy Institute, we want to test the impact of adding a goal thermometer on the campaign donation page. The goal is to reach a $50,000 matching gift. The thermometer syncs with GA to track up-to-date campaign revenue, so donation page visitors will see how much money has been raised towards the campaign goal at the time they’re visiting the page.
We’re curious to see what kind of impact the visible progress and end goal seen through the thermometer will impacted donations.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment (showing the thermometer) resulted in a 7.8% increase in donations but with a level of confidence of only 34.8%. The treatment produced 64 donations to the control's 54. Revenue saw a 9% increase through the treatment with a level of confidence of 65.4%. The increases cannot confidently be rolled out for future high urgency campaigns, but it's definitely worth testing again to see if the experiment can reach a confident result.
It'd be interesting to see how the campaign goal factors into results. Would a campaign that nearly reaches the campaign goal be more motivating for users to give? In this case, the total revenue of the campaign was less than half the matching gift goal. So perhaps users weren't as motivated to give because they felt the goal was out of reach. The goal for the next campaign could be adjusted based on historical results to be more achievable.
Interestingly, the experiment produced contrasting results through desktop and mobile. Desktop saw a 24.3% decrease in donations from the treatment (86% loc) compared to a 58.7% increase in donation on mobile (91% loc). We should test this concept again, hopefully during a campaign that lasts longer than 11 days.

experiments.title: How a sticky bar with a “donors needed” countdown affects donation rates during a high-urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a sticky bar with a “donors still needed” countdown timer during a recurring donor high-urgency campaign will achieve donation rates because of increased urgency.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/02/2023 - 03/11/2023
In the context of a high-urgency campaign focused specifically on obtaining recurring monthly donors, we wanted to know how a sticky bar that dynamically populated a “donors still needed” countdown timer to increase urgency would affect donation rates.
The countdown was designed to automatically update donor counts (recurring donors only). We wanted to know how this would perform against the “wiki-banner” containing a simplified donation form that they have traditionally used.
We set this test up as a multi-variant experiment testing to versions of copy in the “countdown sticky”, with the control and both treatments all receiving roughly 1/3 of the traffic.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for eight days, treatment A produced an 8.7% decrease in recurring donations (with a 30.7% level of confidence) and a 2.1% increase in overall donations (14.7% level of confidence); while treatment B produced a 7.2% increase in monthly donations (with a 24.6% level of confidence) and a .78% increase in overall donations (5.5% level of confidence). Because of these flat results and the unlikelihood of this experiment validating, we decided to end the experiment after just over a week.
We believe the low level of confidence was likely a result of running two treatments, while the flat results indicate that the wiki-banner's ability to produce a micro-commitment by having the prospective donor begin the process before landing on the donation page was motivating more or at least as many people to give.
However, we did make a significant observation while analyzing these results: for direct traffic—which accounted for the majority of traffic—treatment A did produce a 50.7% lift in overall donations (86.6% level of confidence) while treatment B produced a 26.4% lift in donations (58.6% level of confidence).
Because of this observation, we do believe that the urgency created by presenting a countdown of 'donors still needed' does play a role in inspiring people to give by at the very least prompting them to make a lesser commitment in the form of a one-time gift.
Therefore, we are planning to follow this experiment up with an A/B test that tests the current wiki-banner w/ donation form against a treatment with the higher-urgency copy and countdown functionality to see if combining the two will produce a significant lift.

experiments.title: How increasing urgency by adding a countdown timer to a slide-out affects conversion rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that if we add a count down clock to the slide out for potential donors it will achieve a greater response rate and higher conversions because we are evoking a sense of urgency.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/10/2022 - 07/07/2022
We decided to test a slide out variant on the child list page on all devices to determine if adding a countdown clock to our original # of kids still needing to be sponsored would increase the conversion rates with our audience.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Test stopped due to lack of significant differences in donor conversion and RPV.
• Donor conversion was slightly higher for the variant (+7%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (48%).
• Revenue per visitor was slightly higher for the variant (+5%) at a non-statistically valid LoC (61%).
• We recommend not changing the sponsorship slider to include a countdown clock.
• We could potentially run this test during a high urgency campaign to see if it pulls a more valid response.

experiments.title: How increasing urgency by including the word “now” to a donate button affects click-through rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the donate button copy to emphasize urgency for potential donors will achieve greater conversions because donors need to act immediately.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 03/29/2022 - 04/19/2022
If we change the donate button copy to emphasize urgency, there will be an increase in conversions because donors need to act immediately.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Click rate on the donate button was higher for the variant (11%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (77%).
• Overall donor conversion was higher for the variant (24%) at a statistically valid level of confidence (95%).
• RPV was slightly higher for the variant (1%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (53%).
• One-time donor conversion was higher for the variant (15%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (79%).
• One-time RPV was lower for the variant (-4%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (58%).
• Recurring RPV was higher for the variant (40%) at a non-statistically valid level of confidence (85%).
• Recurring donor conversion was higher for the variant (69%) at a statistically valid level of confidence (96%).
• As the hero already uses "Donate Now", we recommend not changing the main donate button despite the significant life in donor conversion.

experiments.title: How changing donate button copy to convey greater clarity and urgency affects donation rates
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that changing the donate button copy to emphasize urgency for potential donors will achieve an increase in click rate because donors need to act immediately.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 02/03/2022 - 02/23/2022
If we change the donate button copy to emphasize urgency, clicks will increase because donors need to act immediately. We examined how different CTA’s affected the click rate on the main donation button. There were four variants with differing language; testing the verbiage of donate v. give v. help in addition to temporal urgency now v. today. This test exclusively measured click rate and did not delve into conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Notable Results & Recommendations:
• Test stopped for lack of significant results after 2 1/2 weeks.
• Lowest performing treatment was "give now" (-1.6%) with a confidence of (9%).
• Best performing treatment was "donate today" (12%) at a non-statistically valid confidence of (64%).
• Further tests will examine the impact on donor conversion and click rate.

experiments.title: How a forward style email in a high urgency campaign affects giving
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a forward style email  for prospective donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 01/17/2023
In an effort to continue to humanize communications during a high urgency campaign, we wanted to use a “forward” style email in one of our email appeals. This email would refer to a previous email and make it seem as if the email was forwarded to the recipient. The control for this experiment had the intended, original email (a message from the President). The treatment was also a message from the President but it asked the email recipient to refer to the previous email sent.
In both of these emails previous donors were suppressed so as not to accidentally include those who may have given in the previous email. The email tested was an evening email on the last day of the campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings: This experiment resulted in a 3.6% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 6.69%. This means that this experiment had almost no effect on donor conversion rate. The control seemingly has a higher average gift that is skewed by a $3k gift. There is also no noticeable change in click thru rate. Our hypothesis is that because this was tested in an email that's in the "high urgency" phase of the campaign, motivation to give before the deadline is already high. It's recommended that this is retested at a different phase in a campaign to more clearly discern motivation.

experiments.title: How personalizing a Dear Reader during a year-end campaign impacted conversion rates.
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that humanizing a dear reader for potential donors will achieve a change in conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2022 - 01/04/2023
During Avila Institute’s year-end campaign, we wanted to capitalize on traffic to one of their most popular websites, SpiritualDirection.com and their blog pages, which were being promoted through a 3-times-a-week enewsletter, and see whether adding a Dear Reader donation ask would help drive more converisons.
For this experiment, we ran a 50/50 test to see whether a ‘Note to Readers’ versus a more personalized ‘Note from Dan (Burke, the founder of Avila) made a difference in conversion rates. The only difference between the two was the change in headline and adding the image and signature of Dan Burke.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we had previously tested whether adding a Dear Reader had an impact on donations, this test was to see whether a more personalized approach would increase conversion rates. And it did: the Note from Dan version, which read like a personal letter from Dan, had a 19% increase in donation conversions with a 72% level of confidence. While not reaching the level of validity to decisively call a winner in this test, the higher level of confidence does provide a directional result.
Our hypothesis is that because Dan is so visible and tied into the founding of Avila, readers of the blog posts were more likely to recognize him and respond to a personal appeal from Dan to support Avila during their year-end campaign.

experiments.title: How does adding a personalized sticky bar to the donation page affect donation rates during a year-end campaign?
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a humanized sticky bar featuring a public personality to the donation page of a year-end fundraising campaign will increase donation rates by increasing credibility and humanizing the appeal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/05/2022 - 12/28/2022
During a year-end fundraising campaign, we wanted to know how adding a humanized sticky bar to the campaign donation page would affect donation rates for a public policy organization, especially considering that the personalized message on the sticky bar would be coming from a public personality who is well-known and trusted by the organization’s target audience.
For this experiment, we created a treatment featuring a personal message and photograph of a public figure asking users to ‘stand with me’ by making a donation to the year-end campaign. We set this experiment up as an A/B test wherein ~50% of traffic would see the control (no sticky bar) and ~50% of traffic would see the treatment (including the sticky bar). The experiment ran for 23 days, throughout the bulk of the year-end campaign.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 23 days, the treatment featuring a humanized sticky bar achieved a 12.7% increase in donation rates (with a 79% level of confidence) when compared to the control.
We believe that including a sticky bar featuring a well-known public figure who is widely trusted by the target audience was able to increase donations among page visitors by humanizing and building credibility into the appeal. This may also have increased the believability of the outcomes being promoted on the donation page.
And though we did not receive a large enough sample size to validate this increase, we know that there is a relatively low risk associated with implementing a treatment that has shown an increase in targeted metrics, so we decided to roll this treatment out to 100% of traffic to wrap up the campaign.

experiments.title: How a countdown timer on a donation page affects donation rates during the end of a year-end fundraising campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a countdown timer to the donation page for the last few days of a year-end fundraising campaign will increase donation rates by increasing the urgency for the prospect to respond to the appeal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/29/2022 - 01/04/2023
During a year-end fundraising campaign for a public policy organization, we wanted to know how adding a countdown timer to the donation timer during the final four days of the campaign would affect donation rates. We hypothesized that the countdown timer would increase urgency and fear of missing out and encourage more prospects to give before time ran out.
For this brief 4-day experiment, we ran an A/B test with ~50% of traffic seeing the control (no timer) and ~50% of traffic seeing the treatment (countdown timer).
experiments.keyLearnings:
Due to the small sample size entering this experiment, we were unable to draw statistically valid conclusions about the efficacy of using a countdown timer in the context of a high-urgency campaign. Showing the timer produced a 5.4% decrease in conversion rates with only a 38.2% level of confidence.
Although this experiment did not validate, the results suggest that we may not be able to assume that adding a countdown timer to a donation page has a significant impact on donation rates. This could be because it is overdone in the nonprofit fundraising space (thus decreasing its impactfulness), or perhaps because it distracts from the content of the donations page. But due to the low statistical confidence of these results, these are only additional hypotheses and we are unable to draw definitive conclusions one way or the other.
Knowing that there is risk involved with implementing a treatment that produces a decrease in conversions, even if the result is not statistically valid, we will want to test this concept again in an effort to receive an adequate sample size to validate the efficacy of a countdown timer before rolling it out to all traffic during future high-urgency campaigns.

experiments.title: How using a micro goal impacts donations during a year-end campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a micro goal for donors will achieve a change in conversion rates..
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/01/2022 - 12/31/2022
During their calendar year-end campaign, the Avila Institute had an overarching goal of raising $300,000 in the month of December. In order to reach this goal, we wanted to implement a campaign-long test on the donor segments of their emails, where the experiment was to see whether asking for $300,000 or asking for 50 donors to step up today would have an impact on conversion rates.
We ran this test across multiple emails throughout the campaign (albeit, not every email sent has this test due to data issues).
experiments.keyLearnings:
Our hypothesis, which we've seen in testing a micro goal across other clients, is that by giving a more 'reasonable' number for donors to reach (in this case 50 donors) rather than a larger goal ($300,000), conversion rates would increase.
In the case of this experiment, the impact was not very large, with a 13% increase in conversion rates overall when looking at the control versus treatment list throughout the whole campaign, and a 66% level of confidence.
In the next iteration of this test, we'd run it across every single email on the campaign, including resends, so that we'd get more volume.

experiments.title: How acknowledging someones previous gift history affects giving in a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that calling out previous giving history for subscribers and donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2022 - 11/29/2022
For Luther Seminary’s Giving Tuesday campaign, we wanted to test how calling out the user’s previous giving history would affect the conversion rate. To do this, we split their file into four segments: never donors, lapsed donors, lybunt donors, and active donors. After those were segmented, we split traffic 50/50 where the control segment saw language around “someone who supports the mission of Luther Seminary” and the treatment segment saw language that specifically called out their last gift DATE and last gift AMOUNT. (Or for never donors, specifically calling out that they had never given a gift to Luther Seminary before.)
We ran this experiment on the AM and PM emails with the same segmentation for Giving Tuesday. In order to have the potential to reach statistical validation, all results have been complied into this one experiment write up.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment email saw a -.02% decrease in conversion rate with only a .05% level of confidence. These results tell us that there was no difference in giving because of calling out previous donor history.
We would like to test this concept on a different high-urgency appeal email during a time of year when the motivation to give is not as high.

experiments.title: How using a clear call-to-action focused on taking the next step of giving a donation in a high urgency campaign affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using clear call-to-action language  for subscribers and donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 12/27/2022 - 12/31/2022
As a part of Luther Seminary’s calendar year-end campaign, we wanted to test the concept of using a clear call-to-action to give a donation vs using a more vague call-to-action of “learn more” to see how it affects donor conversion from the email to the donation page.
We believe that using a clear call-to-action that focuses on taking the next step is most effective as focusing on something vague like “learn more” doesn’t set the right expectation on the next page (donation page).
For this experiment, we tested this concept throughout the final four appeal emails of this campaign in order to receive enough traffic to have the possibility of reaching a valid level of confidence.
The control emails used clear call-to-action language to make a donation and the treatment emails used vague call-to-action language around “learn more”.
experiments.keyLearnings:
The treatment emails using the vague call-to-action saw a 14% increase in conversion rate with only a 35.3% level of confidence therefore the clear call-to-action did not have an effect on the users decision to give.
We believe this happened because the motivation for year-end giving is already higher than all other times of the year. Because of this, using vague or clear language for the call-to-action did not effect whether someone was going to make a donation or not. It's also safe to say that there was not confusion between the vague call-to-action in the treatment email and the donation page.
We would like to test this concept again on another appeal email outside of calendar year-end.

experiments.title: How campaign first messaging impacts clicks from donors in Giving Tuesday emails
experiments.researchQuestion: What messaging will get a more positive response for Giving Tuesday in an email campaign?
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2022 - 12/02/2022
Pathway to Victory is a broadcast ministry that does its primary fundraising through monthly premiums. For Giving Tuesday, they offered an exclusive bundle only available on that day.
As Giving Tuesday has grown exponentially around the world, we wanted to test how we could stand out during a small timeframe when all organizations are making an ask.
We tested this by using Giving Tuesday based messaging at the beginning of the email and then later talked about the mission of PTV for the control and developed a treatment using PTV mission-focused messaging at the beginning of the email and then later used Giving Tuesday messaging.
experiments.keyLearnings:
While we did not receive a sufficient change in clicks, only 2.5% increase with a 16.8% level of confidence. We did see a 9.2% increase in opens with a 99% level of confidence.
While email opens is no longer a reliable metric, due to Apple and other platform privacy rules. We did observe a high enough increase that warranted a deep look.
When the control email hit inboxes, the viewer saw the subject line and the first few words of the email which were "Hello [firstname], On #Giving Tuesday..." For the treatment, the viewer saw the same subject line but a different first few words. They saw "Hello [firstname], As you know Pathway to Victory..."
Our hypothesis is that the increase of opens is due to the initial response of the email not being Giving Tuesday focused like ever other email in the inbox.
As we did not see a valid response to this test, we will continue testing to see if the order of messaging can impact conversion rates.

experiments.title: How adding a humanized sticky bar to a donation page affects donations during a Giving Tuesday Campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a humanized sticky bar featuring a well-known public figure during a high-urgency campaign will increase donation rates by adding value and credibility to the appeal.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 11/29/2022 - 12/03/2022
During a Giving Tuesday campaign for a public policy client, we wanted to know how adding a humanized sticky bar including the image of a well-known public figure would affect donation rates.
This experiment ran as an A/B test for one day with roughly 50% of traffic being shown the treatment including the sticky bar and roughly 50% of traffic shown the control, sans the sticky bar.
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for the length of the Giving Tuesday campaign—one day—we observed a 20.6% DECLINE in donation rates, with a 61% level of confidence, among traffic who were shown the treatment including the humanized sticky bar. Because we did not receive a large enough sample size to validate these results, it is not possible to draw reliable inferences about this experiment.
However, observationally, we can hypothesize that by being large and colorful, the sticky bar may have actually created friction by attracting users' eyes to the top page, rather than the headline and then the body copy, and then the donation form, creating friction during the conversion process.
Because we were unable to obtain a large enough sample size to validate these results, we will want to deploy a similar experiment during a longer-term high-urgency campaign in an effort to validate the results one way or the other.

experiments.title: How adding urgency with a sticky bar affects donation rates during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that including a sticky bar that conveys urgency during a fundraising campaign will increase donation rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/21/2022 - 11/04/2022
During a special high-urgency campaign for a public policy client, we wanted to know how adding a sticky bar with a high-urgency message from the director of the project and including his image would affect donation rates.
The treatment including the sticky bar was run during the entirety of the campaign and was shown to 50% of prospective donors who clicked through to the donation page
experiments.keyLearnings: After running this experiment for just over 30 days, we observed a 5.3% decline in donation rates ... but with only a 49.4% level of confidence. Because we did not receive a large enough sample size to validate these results, it is not possible to draw reliable inferences about this experiment. However, we can hypothesize that the sticky bar either created friction by attracting the viewer's eyes back up the page, rather than progressing down the copy to the donation form. It could also be possible that the particular language chosen for this sticky bar "this can't wait" was a bit too forceful and created resentment in viewers. But in either case, we cannot know for sure.

experiments.title: How sticking to urgency in the final email of a high urgency campaign affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that sticking to urgency and removing extended value proposition for the final email in a high urgency campaign will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/06/2022
Luther Seminary ran their bi-annual Working Preacher campaign in the spring of 2022, and we wanted to test how sticking with urgency and reducing the amount of extended value proposition copy in the final email would affect donor conversion.
We did this by removing the extra language from the control email centered around what their gift would do for Working Preacher, because these points had been conveyed heavily in every email in the campaign prior to the final email.
experiments.keyLearnings:
As you can see in the chart above, removing the extended value proposition and focusing mainly on urgency did not have an impact on the conversion rate for this email. The treatment saw a 6.6% decrease with only a 20.2% level of confidence.

From this experiment, we can assume that the difference in language did not affect the donor's decision to give at this point in the campaign. We believe this is because both emails had the urgency of that being the final day, and whether or not we included the extended value proposition, it did not affect the conversion rate.

experiments.title: How deriving impact back to the donor affects donor conversion rate in a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that deriving the impact back to the donor for potential donors apart of the Working Preacher campaign will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Ended On: 06/06/2022
Luther Seminary ran their bi-annual Working Preacher campaign in the spring of 2022, and we wanted to test how deriving impact back to the donor instead of the organization would impact the results of one email.
We A/B tested this concept on email 3 of the campaign by changing the email copy from language focused on what Working Preacher will do if they gain support to the specific impact the donor will have on pastors and Christians with their gift.
experiments.keyLearnings:
Although we did not receive enough traffic on this email to see the validity, we saw a decrease of 24.1% in conversion rate with a 53.2% level of confidence. We believe this happened because Working Preacher donors are more inclined to give to this project when they know how the organization is providing and giving back.

By taking that concept away in the treatment and changing the narrative to what the donor will do with their gift, Working Preacher donors did not get the reassurance they needed to see what Working Preacher would do with their support.

In order to be confident in these results, we'll need to further test this concept with the Working Preacher audience.

experiments.title: How sending users to a campaign specific donation page during a high urgency campaign affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a campaign specific donation page for people in a high urgency campaign will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 09/30/2022 - 10/20/2022
Luther Seminary is running the bi-annual Working Preacher campaign.
Previously during high-urgency campaigns, they have always sent their users to the main donation page on workingpreacher.org which has many friction opportunities (navigation away from the page) and limited value proposition.
They wanted to test building a completely separate campaign donation that has more value proposition on why someone should give to the Working Preacher campaign and what Working Preacher will do for you (the donor).
We set up this experiment with the new copy and removed the extra friction (navigation) from the page as well.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for over 17 days with more than 630 samples to test with. All in all, we did not see any significant changes in performance between the control donation page and the campaign-specific donation page. We only saw a decrease in conversion rate of 3.8% with only a 29% level of confidence.

We believe this is because they have a very targeted and invested audience of Working Preacher members and donors therefore adding the extra value proposition or removing the extra friction did not make an overall impact on the users' decision to give to this campaign.

We will do further testing into the campaign-specific donation page by adding a countdown clock to provide more urgency toward the deadline.

experiments.title: How adding a deadline to a pop-up affects donor conversion rate
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that adding a deadline to a pop-up for users on the Working Preacher website will achieve a higher donor conversion rate.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 10/01/2022 - 11/04/2022
Luther Seminary was running their bi-annual Working Preacher campaign. We added a pop-up to the Working Preacher website and wanted to test how adding urgency / the deadline to the pop-up copy would affect donor conversion rate for users coming to the donation page from that source.
We added the deadline of October 31st to the headline and the body copy.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for about a month and saw an 8.7% lift in conversion rate with only a 60.5% level of confidence. We believe that adding the deadline to the pop-up provided more clarity for the users on the front end of what we were asking them to do and when we needed the donation.

Because this did not validate, we would like to further test this concept on pop-ups for future high-urgency campaigns.

Something else to note is that we should have tracked clicked conversion as well, to see if adding the deadline affected click-through rate. On our next test, we will need to track the clicks on the button as well.

experiments.title: How redirecting mobile traffic to a specific section of a donation form affects donor conversion
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that removing friction in the donation pathway for highly motivated users during a high urgency campaign will result in greater donor conversions.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/13/2022 - 06/10/2022
Focus on the Family runs an annual high-urgency campaign for their pro-life movement called SeeLife. During this campaign, they run a sticky banner atop all of their pages that contains a specific call to action with a donation amount on the banner. There is little to no value proposition on this banner (especially on a mobile device), just a very simple donation ask, accompanied by specific donation amounts. When clicking on this banner, the user is normally redirected to the top of a campaign donation page where they can watch a video about the campaign, read more about it, then scroll down to make a donation.
For the purposes of this experiment, Focus on the Family wanted to test if redirecting the user to an anchored location on the donation form embedded on the campaign donation page would result in a higher donor conversion. This test would bypass the content at the top of the page, and give the donor the option to make their donation quickly…especially on a mobile device where screen real estate is limited. In addition, the team wanted to test if an anchored re-direct vs. a locked redirect (where the user had to initiate a force click out of the locked screen) would result in a higher conversion.
The working theory was that a user was already highly motivated to give due to the nature of the high urgency campaign, so removing as much friction as possible could lead to higher donor conversions.
experiments.keyLearnings:
As expected, the treatments for this user group experienced a lift. While the experiment did not validate due to a small sample size and a low level of confidence, the experiment did provide some good learnings. There was an 8.1% lift in conversion rate for the locked version with a 57% level of confidence, and a 3.2% lift with 25% level of confidence for the anchored version. Both treatments saw a positive lift over the control. However, we would like to try a similar experiment during a time when the user was not as motivated by a high urgency campaign to see if this would have a similar result. We would recommend rolling this out again for other high-urgency campaigns that have similar circumstances.

Notable changes to other metrics due to the experiment:
Revenue experienced a 37.2% increase with a 87% level of confidence.
Paid Search visitors had a 136.3% increase in donations with a 100% level of confidence.

experiments.title: How a radically redesigned donation page impacted gifts during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a radically redesigned donation page for fiscal year end campaign visitors will achieve an increase in donor conversion.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/26/2022 - 08/01/2022
DTS was running their annual fiscal year-end campaign and we wanted to optimize the standard donation page that is typically used during this campaign. We developed a treatment that incorporated the following elements: we updated the headline to include urgency by referencing the deadline of “before midnight on June 30”. We also used bullet points to demonstrate opportunities for impact in the body copy. The language also gave specific evidentials of how the donor’s gift would be used, stating that for every $75 you give, you can offer our online courses to 15 people. We also introduced some donor autonomy and re-highlighted the urgency of the deadline in the subheader leading into the donation form “If you want to support…please make your gift using the form below:”
experiments.keyLearnings: What we found after running the test for the duration of the campaign was that the radical redesign treatment had a 5% decrease in conversion rate with 44% level of confidence so the results are not valid. Our hypothesis for the flat results is that 1) the donation page copy is longer than the control which could have introduced additional friction to the experience for those in the treatment group and 2) the donation page copy used in the treatment is still organization-centric and not donor-focused. All 4-5 paragraphs begin with the word "DTS" or "we" and the bullet points are organization-centric in terms of being presented as "here's what we are already doing" rather than posed as "what your gift will help accomplish". We also feel that the urgency could have been better communicated by asking for a gift "now" or "today" rather than any time before June 30. We need to better compel visitors to make their gift at the time of their visit to the page. There is no clear need or problem presented in the copy in the treatment for the donor to address or solve, which is something we will want to consider framing out for future tests.

experiments.title: How will injecting urgency into a sticky bar affect donation rates during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that injecting urgency into a sticky bar for prospective donors will achieve higher conversion rates.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/08/2022 - 07/03/2022
During a recent high-urgency campaign, we wanted to test two versions of a sticky bar to see which performed better in increasing donation rates on a campaign donation page.
For this experiment, we created two different sticky bar treatments and ran the experiment for the length of the campaign, 22 days
experiments.keyLearnings:
After running this experiment for 22 days, we observed a 41.5% lift in conversion rates produced by the sticky bar with urgency, however, we were unable to validate these results with a level of confidence of just 46%.

When reviewing the results, we also realized that the traffic who experienced each version was not evenly split ... with 58.6% of visitors experiencing version A and only 41.4% of visitors experiencing version B. Both versions earned 6 conversions, but it is not possible for us to know with certainty how this would have changed if a more even split of traffic had occurred.

We will need to test this hypothesis again in the future to find out if adding urgency in sticky bar language will improve donor conversion.

experiments.title: How longer, value prop-based language impacts donor conversion rate in a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that longer, value-prop focused language on a donation page for prospective donors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 05/24/2022 - 06/25/2022
The Navigators were running a high urgency campaign for their Missionary Care fund. In order to increase donor conversion rate, we wanted to test longer, value prop-focused language on the donation page. Our hypothesis is that the longer copy will drive more donations because it is giving the prospective donor a fuller reason to give towards Missionary Care. Traffic to this donation page came from campaign emails and sticky bar traffic from their website.
experiments.keyLearnings: While initially the results seemed to indicate that the longer copy outperformed the shorter, direct copy, the experiment ultimately resulted in a 17% increase in donor conversion rate with a level of confidence of 57%. The treatment made no substantial difference to the end results. This concept can be retested by moving the gift array to the bottom oof the donation page so that the site visitor needs to read through the copy before getting to the intended call to action. It's likely that because the gift array was at the top, some site visitors may not have scrolled down to read the intended copy.

experiments.title: How using a homepage takeover vs a pop up impacted giving during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that using a home page takeover during a high urgency campaigh for website visitors will achieve a higher donor conversion rate .
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 06/28/2022 - 07/04/2022
Reasons to Believe was running a high urgency campaign (Fiscal Year End) and wanted to drive more donations during the last week of the campaign. In previous campaigns RTB has done a homepage takeover or a pop up at the end of the campaign to direct website traffic to the donation page. In order to discern which method works best for their audience we decided to run a test where half of their site visitors would get a homepage takeover and the other half would get a pop up. Our initial hypothesis is that more web visitors would convert to donors on a homepage takeover because it takes up more real estate and in turn draws more attention to the campaign. A pop up can be easily dismissed on a webpage.
experiments.keyLearnings: When looking at all traffic, this experiment resulted in a 21% lift in donor conversion rate but with only a level of confidence of 40%.  When we observe only new visitors we see a higher level of confidence: 82% lift with a level of confidence of 77%. While this did not validate, it is probable that new visitors to the site may have been confused by seeing an entirely new landing page (the Fiscal Year End donation page) and therefore responded more to the pop up. However, since this experiment only ran for the last week of the campaign, we did not see sufficient enough traffic to validate the results. It is recommended that this is retested over a longer period of time.

experiments.title: How removing a video on a donation page affects donor conversion during a high urgency campaign
experiments.researchQuestion: We believe that a campaign donation page that does NOT feature a promotional video will convert more potential donors than a page that DOES feature a video.
experiments.summary:
Timeframe: 04/12/2022 - 05/16/2022
Focus on the Family has an annual high urgency campaign called See Life to help fund their Option Ultrasound program. This annual campaign has a $4 million dollar donor match fund and attracts a highly motivated potential donor base. With this campaign, Focus on the Family wanted to test whether or not having a video on the donation page caused any additional friction for getting visitors to make a donation. Their control featured a 3 minute promotional video for the Option Ultrasound program just above the donation form. The treatment removed the video from the donation page all together.
experiments.keyLearnings:
This experiment ran for just over a month, and saw both an increase for the treatment (no video) and a decrease for the treatment over the course of 4 weeks, before finally settling on a 1.1% increase in conversion rate for the treatment by the time the experiment ended. Due to a lack of sample size (19k samples vs the required 491k samples) and a low level of confidence (21.4%), this experiment did not validate. This shows that having a video on this particular campaign donation page had no relative difference in the donation intentions for the majority of visitors.
